# Sardis Canvases — Full Text Dump

This file is the concatenated plain-text content of every canvas at canvas.sardis.sh.
Safe to paste into any large-context AI (Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini) for summarization,
Q&A, or architecture review.

Canvases are ordered as they appear in the sidebar.
Last built: 2026-04-25T12:41:08.664Z

---



=====================================
# MANIFESTO
https://canvas.sardis.sh/manifesto
=====================================

Manifesto · April 2026

## 
The missing substrate for trustworthy agents.

Efe Baran Durmaz
·
20, writing from Ankara

The simplest way I can say it: I think AI agents are being held back,
not by models, but by the fact that they cannot be trusted with money.
The honest version of that sentence is longer. Here is the longer one.

AI agents do not just have a capability problem. They have a trust
problem.

A year ago I started thinking about agent autonomy the way an infra
person thinks about it. What is the actual, structural thing stopping
this from working in production. Models kept getting better every
month. Tool calling kept getting better. Browser use kept getting
better. And yet every agent demo I watched ended at the same wall: the
moment it needed to move money, a human had to step in. The agent could
reason about a purchase, negotiate a rate, draft a contract, book a
table. It could not actually pay for any of it without a credential
somebody had handed it by hand.

That bottleneck looked small from far away. The closer I looked, the
more I thought it was the thing holding back a huge chunk of AI's
economic value. An agent that reasons but cannot transact is a very
expensive draft. The decision matters only if it lands.

So I started where most people start. A payment rail wrapper. Something
thin. Give an agent a way to move stablecoins, plug in an on-ramp and
an off-ramp, call it a day. I was going to be done in a weekend.

## What changed my mind

I never used my own card for any of this. I knew from day one that was
the wrong thing to do. But a lot of people did, and are still doing it.
Around the same time I started, OpenAI pushed
Buy it in ChatGPT
and the whole agentic commerce pitch started getting louder everywhere.
That was the signal that the wall was about to get hit. A lot.

I did not have to wait long to see what that looked like in practice.
In February, an OpenAI engineer's
OpenClaw-based trading agent
got a simple request ("send 4 SOL for medical expenses") and, through
a quantity-parsing slip, sent 52.43 million tokens worth something
like $600K. Nothing was wrong with the model. Nothing
was wrong with the request. The failure was at the layer below: the
agent had the authority to move everything, and a small misread was
enough to trigger it. Mastercard eventually put out a
post about OpenClaw and the urgent need for AI security standards.
This is a category now, not an incident.

So I moved up a level. Forget wrapping card rails. Build something
agent-shaped: prepaid wallets per agent, on-ramp in, off-ramp out,
spend caps, kill switches. Better. Much better than a card. The agent
could no longer drain the principal's account because the principal's
account was not connected. I spent a good chunk of autumn on that.

And it was still not enough.

A prepaid wallet solves the blast-radius problem and not the
authorization problem. An agent with a funded wallet can still loop.
Can still get prompt-injected. Can still misread a quantity and spend
everything in the wallet on one transaction. The wallet shrinks the
damage; it does not prevent the class of bug. And the second you cap
the wallet, you have given up the very autonomy you were trying to
enable. The agent is no longer making real economic decisions, it is
just drawing from a small pot you pre-approved.

What I wanted was an agent that could act autonomously without me
having to pre-decide every decision. What I had built was a leash
with a slightly longer rope.

## Why payments were the sharp edge

It is worth being specific, because the shape is not obvious and the
existing infra assumes something very different.

A human makes maybe a dozen deliberate purchases a day. An agent
running a single task can make hundreds. A procurement agent polling
five suppliers, a research agent hitting paid APIs per query, an infra
agent provisioning cloud resources on demand, a trading agent reacting
to ticks. All of these look like payment floods compared to human
usage. Current estimates put agent-driven transactions at
10x to 1000x human frequency, with per-transaction
amounts often in the cent-to-dollar range rather than tens-to-thousands.
Card rails at 2.9% + $0.30 per charge are mathematically dead at this
density. ACH at 1 to 3 business days is actually dead, the decision
and the settlement live in different economic eras.

What the rails have to carry is a different shape entirely:
sub-second authorization, marginal fees close to zero on
high-frequency flows, deterministic finality for small amounts, and
escrow semantics when the stakes rise. Nobody is shipping all of that
cleanly today. That is why Sardis treats the
internal ledger as the primary rail
and external rails as on/off-ramps. PayPal's shape, applied to
machine commerce.

## The git analogy I could not stop thinking about

Around November I started noticing something that felt important. AI
adoption inside software is enormous. Engineers give agents write
access to their whole codebase and walk away. Trillion-dollar-adjacent
industries, finance, healthcare, legal, procurement, are much more
cautious. Orders of magnitude more cautious.

The thing that makes software different is git. Every change is a
commit. Every commit is reversible. Every merge is reviewable. Blame
goes down to the line. If an agent ships something bad, you revert it.
You know exactly what happened and exactly who did it, even when the
"who" is a program.

Outside of software, there is no git. An agent that pays a vendor
does not leave a diff. An agent that signs a contract does not leave
a rollback path. An agent that moves inventory does not leave a
three-way merge. The reason AI has not flooded into these industries
is not model capability. It is that the substrate does not let you
undo.

This was the point where I stopped thinking of Sardis as a payments
company. Payments were just the sharpest place to see the real
problem. The real problem was never how agents move money. It was how
non-human actors do irreversible things without requiring blind trust.
The general shape is: how does a non-human actor do any
consequential thing and leave evidence behind that makes the action
safe to allow in the first place.

## What the thesis actually demands

Once I stopped thinking about this as "agent payments," the same set
of missing primitives kept reappearing everywhere.

Trustworthy agents need reviewable state transitions instead of opaque
side effects. They need attributable identity instead of "some process
somewhere did this." They need approvals as first-class transitions,
not ad hoc modal dialogs or settings-page toggles. They need evidence
that survives the action. They need delegation and authority to be
explicit rather than inferred after the fact.

And they need all of those things to compose. That is the part people
underestimate. It is not enough to have one protocol for tool use,
another for communication, another for payments, another for identity,
and another for approvals if the shared semantics between them stay
implicit. That is where trustworthy systems break back into product
glue.

Agent systems do not just need more APIs. They need a shared trust
layer.

The names of some of the systems I have built around these primitives
are in the references. The point here is not the portfolio. The point
is the shape of the missing substrate.

## Where Sardis sits in all this

Sardis is what you build when you realize money is just the most
high-stakes version of an irreversible agent action, and the primitive
underneath has to be better than "give the agent a credential and
hope."

Sardis is not a card wrapper. It is not a prettier dashboard for
agent spend. It is not just a prepaid wallet with stricter limits.

A payment, in the model I ended up with, is a one-time object.
It exists for a single transaction. It is minted when needed, expires
in minutes, signed by three parties (principal, issuer, agent), bound
to a specific merchant session, drawn from a specific pool of funds.
You cannot steal it and use it elsewhere. You cannot replay it. You
cannot hold it and spend it next week.

Underneath the object is a mandate: a signed statement of
exactly what the agent is allowed to do. Merchants, categories,
amounts per transaction, amounts per period, approval thresholds,
currency, rails. Between the mandate and the payment is a policy
engine that lives on the execution path, not in a settings page.
Every payment that crosses the system, from any SDK, any framework,
any protocol, runs through the same twelve checks in the same order.
There is no fast lane that bypasses enforcement, because every fast
lane I ever let myself build became the bug that mattered.

The rest of this site is the spec. If you want the details, start at
Protocol Objects.

## Why the layer underneath cannot be owned

AI agents are starting to get some of their own primitives:
identity, communication, tool use, payments, verification, versioned
state. That is good. What is still missing is the layer where these
primitives meet. Right now the shared semantics between them are still
mostly implicit, hidden inside product-specific assumptions and glue
code.

The missing layer is not another app. It is shared trust semantics.

That is part of why I started writing
OAPS,
an open standard for agentic primitives: intent, authority, approval,
delegation, and evidence, expressed in a form other systems can
verify, enforce, and build against. That missing layer is not a
detail. It is the control surface. Without it, every company ends up
with its own private dialect for trust. The words sound similar. The
guarantees are not.

Without open semantics for authority, agent ecosystems drift into
private dialects.

In parallel, I have been shipping
OSP,
an open protocol for agent-to-service discovery and provisioning.
OAPS and OSP are not where the money is. They are where
interoperability either survives or dies.

OAPS is the standard. Sardis is the first production implementation
of the payment half of it.

Products should compete on distribution, rails, compliance, developer
experience, and regulated posture. But the validation primitive
underneath consequential agent action should be open, inspectable,
and portable. If the layer that decides what an agent is allowed to
do is proprietary, then trust is not infrastructure. It is rented
software. A payment authorization layer under a single company's
control is a layer that can be deprecated, repriced, bought, or
quietly changed. Plumbing does not benefit from brand loyalty.

This is the one thing I am not willing to trade. Everything else is
negotiable.

## What I think happens next

Agent payments is going to consolidate into a small number of serious
product companies with real distribution, real bank partnerships, and
real regulatory posture. They will do things I am not good at and
should not pretend to be good at.

What I want is for the primitive underneath all of them to be the
right shape. One-time payment objects. Signed mandates. Policy on the
path. Verifiable by anyone. Owned by no one. If that turns out to be
the substrate the ecosystem settles on, I do not care whose logo is
in the dashboard. I care that three years from now, when an agent I
wrote is spending real money on my behalf while I am asleep, the
primitive it is using is one I would have agreed to if I had been
awake.

That is why I am still building. If you are anywhere near this
problem, tell me about it.
efe@sardis.sh. I would rather
compare notes than build parallel versions of the same thing.

References

- OpenAI, Buy it in ChatGPT

- KuCoin, OpenClaw AI agent incident causes millions in user losses

- Mastercard, OpenClaw and the urgent need for AI security standards

- Cloudflare, Agents have their own computers with Sandboxes GA

- Cloudflare, Sandboxing AI agents, 100x faster (Dynamic Workers)

- Cloudflare, Agents changelog, Artifacts (git-compatible versioned storage)

- RFC 9421, HTTP Message Signatures

- Efe Durmaz, OSP, Open Service Protocol

- Efe Durmaz, OAPS, open standards for agentic primitives

Efe Baran Durmaz · April 2026

sardis.sh
· github
· canvases


=====================================
# ARCHITECTURE
https://canvas.sardis.sh/architecture
=====================================

Canvas 01 · System

## Architecture Overview

Sardis is a seven-layer stack. An AI agent enters at the top; real money moves at the bottom.
Every request traverses the same spine: identity, mandate, policy, orchestration, execution,
settlement, audit. This canvas shows that spine and why each layer exists.

TL;DR
AI agents can reason, but they cannot be trusted with money. Sardis inserts a verifiable control
plane between an agent's decision and a bank rail — non-custodial MPC wallets below, natural-language
spending policies above, an append-only audit trail on the side. Live on Base and Tempo mainnet.

Principles

## Five commitments the architecture is shaped around.

These aren't slogans; each one forces a specific structural choice. Change a principle and the stack
reshapes around it.

Non-custodial first

Private keys never live in Sardis memory. All signing goes through an MPC provider
(Turnkey default, Fireblocks/Circle/Lit pluggable). Why it matters: an agent
compromised through prompt injection cannot exfiltrate funds because there is no key to steal.
A platform breach limits blast radius to metadata, not movement.

Policy before execution

Every transaction passes through PaymentOrchestrator →
PreExecutionPipeline (policy + KYC/AML + mandate + rate limits + anomaly).
Why it matters: there is no "private fast path" — wallets, mandates, ap2
routers all funnel through the same gate, so one hardening improves safety everywhere.

Fail-closed by default

Compliance failure, policy failure, unreachable KYA/FIDES service — all default to deny.
Why it matters: agents run unattended; "when in doubt, allow" is how you
wake up to a regulator letter. The override flag (SARDIS_AGIT_FAIL_OPEN=true)
exists for local dev only.

Audit everything, anchor it on-chain

Every decision writes a ledger entry; batches are Merkle-rooted and anchored via
SardisLedgerAnchor.sol. Why it matters: agent disputes resolve to
cryptographic evidence, not logs that could have been edited.

Provider abstraction

Every external service — MPC, KYC, AML, card issuer, fiat rail, PSP — sits behind an
interface in packages/sardis-core. Why it matters: Didit
replaced Persona without touching business logic; Lithic can replace Stripe Issuing when
funding requirements allow. Swap cost stays low, lock-in stays zero.

Open-core moat

Framework SDKs, protocol verifiers, reference contracts — open source.
Policy engine, trust graph, enterprise compliance — proprietary.
Why it matters: distribution goes viral through SDKs; revenue defends
through the parts regulated entities actually pay for.

The Spine

## Seven layers — top to bottom.

Read top-to-bottom for a request's journey; bottom-to-top for a money movement's journey.
Canvas 07 · Payment Flow walks one request through every box.

Layer 1 · Agent Surface

AI framework SDKs + MCP + CLI

Where the agent code actually lives. Thin adapters that expose Sardis primitives (pay,
policy check, wallet balance) as native tools in each framework. Zero crypto concepts leak up.

sardis-langchain
sardis-crewai
sardis-openai-agents
sardis-agent-sdk
sardis-adk
sardis-agentkit
sardis-mcp-server
sardis-cli
+ 10 more

↓ HTTPS / JSON

Layer 2 · API Gateway

FastAPI · 123 routers · auth + rate limit + CSP

Composition root: packages/sardis-api/src/sardis_api/main.py. Every router is grouped
by domain (auth, wallets, payments, policy, checkout, compliance, ops). CSP/HSTS/CORS enforced
at middleware; principal extraction in require_principal; rate limits Redis-backed.

auth / api_keys / me
wallets / agents / groups
payments / pay / ap2 / mandates
checkout / merchant_checkout
compliance / kyc_onboarding
admin / emergency / kill_switch

↓ PaymentOrchestrator.execute()

Layer 3 · Pre-Execution Pipeline

Policy + Compliance + Mandate + Limits + Anomaly

The single gate. Implements AGIT (Agent Identity and Trust), KYA (Know Your Agent), FIDES
(trust graph), and the Spending Policy engine. Fail-closed; every deny is audited with
reason code.

spending_policy.py
agit_policy_engine.py
anomaly_detection.py
approval_service.py
rate_limiter
erc8183_policy_guard.py

↓ approved intent

Layer 4 · Protocol Verification

AP2 · TAP · x402 · A2A mandate chains

If the request arrived via a standard protocol (Google/Visa/Mastercard AP2, Visa TAP, x402
402-Payment-Required, Google A2A), the full mandate chain is verified — Ed25519/ECDSA-P256
sigs, replay cache, TTL — before the orchestrator will even schedule a transaction.

sardis-protocol
MandateVerifier
ReplayCache (Postgres + Redis)
MandateArchive

↓ signed mandate

Layer 5 · Orchestration + Wallet

ChainExecutor · WalletManager · MPC signing

Chooses the rail (Base, Tempo, Ethereum, Polygon, Arbitrum, Optimism), selects the token
(USDC, EURC, USDT, PYUSD), requests an MPC signature from Turnkey (or Fireblocks/Circle),
and submits to the appropriate RPC. Dedicated executors for Tempo
(tempo/executor.py) and EVM chains.

sardis-chain/executor.py
sardis-wallet/manager.py
tempo_keychain.py
Circle Paymaster (USDC gas)
Pimlico (backup)

↓ broadcast tx

Layer 6 · Settlement

CCTP v2 · Fiat rails · Stripe Connect

Cross-chain USDC via Circle CCTP v2 (unified addresses). Fiat off-ramp via Stripe Connect
(US), Striga (EU vIBANs + SEPA), Bridge (multi-rail). Settlement is explicit and reconciled —
not "best effort."

CCTP v2 burn/mint
merchant_payout
striga_vibans
bridge (ACH/SEPA/PIX)
reconciliation_queue

↓ state hashes

Layer 7 · Audit + Ledger

Append-only ledger · Merkle anchor · Evidence export

Every decision — approved, denied, errored — writes a ledger row. Batches are Merkle-rooted
and anchored on-chain via SardisLedgerAnchor.sol. Operators and regulators get
cryptographic evidence, not "trust our logs."

sardis-ledger
SardisLedgerAnchor.sol
policy_decisions
evidence_export
data_export

Interaction

## One request, end to end.

A simplified sequence — what happens when an agent calls sardis.pay(to, amount).
Canvas 07 · Payment Flow expands every box here with real code references.

[diagram]

sequenceDiagram
participant A as Agent (LangChain)
participant S as Sardis SDK
participant G as API Gateway
participant P as Pre-Execution Pipeline
participant V as Protocol Verifier
participant O as Orchestrator
participant W as Wallet/MPC
participant C as Chain (Base/Tempo)
participant L as Ledger + Anchor

A->>S: pay(to, amount, reason)
S->>G: POST /v1/payments (API key auth)
G->>P: require_principal + orchestrator.execute()
P->>P: Spending policy ✓
P->>P: KYC/AML ✓
P->>P: Mandate delegation ✓
P->>P: Rate limit ✓
P->>P: Anomaly score ≥ threshold ✓
P->>V: verify AP2/TAP mandate chain
V-->>P: signed ok
P->>O: approved intent
O->>W: request MPC signature (Turnkey)
W-->>O: signed tx
O->>C: broadcast (Alchemy RPC)
C-->>O: tx hash + receipt
O->>L: append ledger_entry + policy_decision
L->>L: Merkle batch + on-chain anchor
O-->>G: PaymentResult
G-->>S: 200 OK
S-->>A: { tx_hash, status }

[/diagram]

Planes

## Data plane vs. control plane.

A common source of confusion: Sardis has two planes. The data plane moves money.
The control plane moves opinions about money — policies, approvals,
trust graph updates, anomaly thresholds. They share a database but not a code path.

Data plane — "do the thing"

API gateway → orchestrator → wallet → chain. Synchronous, latency-sensitive (agents are
watching a response), fail-closed. Owned by sardis-api, sardis-chain,
sardis-wallet.

Latency budget
~2s p99 end-to-end (excluding chain confirmation)

Blast radius on bug
money moves wrong → fail-closed design contains

Owner test suite
tests/integration + on-chain replay

Control plane — "decide the policy"

Dashboard + policy manager + approvals + kill switch + trust registry + webhooks +
live events. Async, eventually consistent, human-in-the-loop friendly.
Owned by apps/dashboard, sardis-core policy modules,
event streaming routers.

Latency budget
~5s to UI from event bus

Blast radius on bug
bad policy → caught at next execution (fail-closed)

Owner test suite
Playwright (dashboard) + policy unit tests

Source of Truth

## Key files if you want to dig in.

File
Layer
Why it matters

packages/sardis-core/src/sardis_v2_core/config.py
Everything
The canonical configuration. Every env var, every provider toggle, every chain config. If you want to know what's live, start here.

packages/sardis-core/src/sardis_v2_core/orchestrator.py
3 · Gate
PaymentOrchestrator — the single chokepoint through which wallets, mandates, ap2, pay all flow.

packages/sardis-core/src/sardis_v2_core/spending_policy.py
3 · Gate
The policy engine. Natural-language input → structured policy → enforcement at runtime.

packages/sardis-api/src/sardis_api/main.py
2 · Gateway
FastAPI composition root. Shows all 123 routers wired up with middleware.

packages/sardis-chain/src/sardis_v2_chain/executor.py
5 · Execution
Chain selection, RPC submission, receipt parsing. Multi-chain orchestration lives here.

packages/sardis-wallet/src/sardis_wallet/manager.py
5 · Execution
Wallet lifecycle + MPC signing. Where Turnkey/Fireblocks/Circle is abstracted.

packages/sardis-protocol/src/sardis_v2_protocol/ap2.py
4 · Verification
AP2 mandate-chain verifier — Intent → Cart → Payment signatures.

packages/sardis-ledger
7 · Audit
Append-only ledger + Merkle batching. Feeds on-chain anchor.

contracts/src/SardisLedgerAnchor.sol
7 · Audit
On-chain anchor for Merkle-rooted evidence batches.

apps/dashboard/app/(dashboard)
Control plane
43 operator pages — policies, approvals, kill switch, live events.

Deployment Topology

## Where each piece actually runs.

Surface
Runtime
Region
Notes

API (sardis-api)
Cloud Run
us-central1
Gunicorn + uvicorn inside Docker; 300s timeout; Fluid-compute-equivalent concurrency.

Dashboard (apps/dashboard)
Vercel
Edge
Next.js 16 App Router (React 19); fluid compute for RSC; app.sardis.sh + dashboard.sardis.sh.

Landing (apps/landing)
Vercel
Edge
Next.js; sardis.sh; static + incremental for blog/compare pages.

Checkout UI (sardis-checkout-ui)
Vercel
Edge
checkout.sardis.sh. Embeddable SDK served from same origin.

Database
Neon Postgres
us-east
Serverless; 107 migrations applied in order; branching for preview envs.

Cache / rate-limit / dedup
Upstash Redis
global
Required in prod (SARDIS_REDIS_URL); rate limiter fail-closed if unreachable.

MPC signing
Turnkey API
Provider
Organization + sub-org per tenant; keys never leave Turnkey.

Chain RPC
Alchemy
Provider
Env-driven (SARDIS_BASE_RPC_URL, etc.); public RPC as fallback.

Event streaming
SSE + webhook outbox
Cloud Run
Per-merchant stream; outbox pattern for webhook at-least-once.

Smart contracts
Base + Tempo mainnet
L2
See canvas 06 for addresses.

Go deeper

## Follow-ups.

02 · System

Repo Structure

The monorepo tree, folder by folder. Start here if you cloned and got lost.

07 · Flows

Payment Flow

The sequence above, but every box expanded to the actual function it calls.

08 · Flows

Policy Engine

How natural language becomes a structured policy and how it's enforced.

19 · Ops

Security Model

Trust boundaries, fail-closed defaults, threat model.


=====================================
# REPO STRUCTURE
https://canvas.sardis.sh/repo-structure
=====================================

Canvas 02 · System

## Repo Structure

A map of the Sardis monorepo, every top-level folder, what lives inside, and where to look
when you're lost. The stack is pragmatic, not prescriptive: Python where the money logic
lives, TypeScript where the browser and SDK consumers are, Solidity for the on-chain parts.
Nothing here is load-bearing opinion about what language you should be using.

TL;DR
Three workspaces (packages/, apps/, contracts/) carry
99% of the production code. Everything else is either supporting material (docs, scripts,
demos) or build artefacts. If you're reading code, start in packages/sardis-core.
If you're running code, start in apps/dashboard. If you're verifying on-chain,
start in contracts/src.

By the numbers

## What's in the tree.

51
packages

2
Next.js apps

9
Solidity contracts

43
dashboard pages

59
docs folders

18
demos

20+
examples

50+
ops scripts

Top level

## The whole repo at a glance.

Trimmed of generated artefacts (node_modules, dist,
.venv, lockfiles, cache directories). What's left is what a new contributor
should actually care about.

sardis/
├── apps/ # 2 Next.js 16 apps (React 19)
│ ├── dashboard/ # app.sardis.sh · operator pages
│ └── landing/ # sardis.sh · marketing + blog
├── packages/ # 51 packages, see canvas 03
├── contracts/ # Foundry · Solidity 0.8.24
├── docs/ # architecture, GTM, investor notes
├── demos/ # 18 runnable demos
├── examples/ # 20+ framework-specific examples
├── tests/ # cross-package integration tests
├── scripts/ # ops, deploy, health scripts
├── pyproject.toml # uv workspace root
├── pnpm-workspace.yaml # pnpm workspace root
├── Dockerfile # Cloud Run image
└── README.md # public-facing overview

Show secondary folders (api proxies, monitoring, marketplace, legal, playground...)

├── api/ # Vercel API routes (checkout proxy, docs)
├── api-proxy/ # Edge proxy for rate-limit-heavy endpoints
├── deploy/ # GCP Cloud Run configs + env templates
├── docs-site/ # Docusaurus-style public docs
├── canvases/ # this document, HTML canvases
├── research/ # internal research notes + prototypes
├── monitoring/ # Upptime config + uptime badges
├── marketplace/ # MCP / Composio / Activepieces submissions
├── ops/ # incident templates + runbooks
├── legal/ # ToS, privacy, DPA drafts
├── playground/ # scratchpad, not shipped
└── sardis/ # thin public Python import surface

Show the packages/ expansion (top 12 of 51)

packages/
├── sardis-core/ # domain models, policy, orchestrator
├── sardis-api/ # FastAPI, 123 routers
├── sardis-chain/ # multi-chain executor + Tempo
├── sardis-wallet/ # MPC signing abstraction
├── sardis-protocol/ # AP2 / TAP / x402 verifiers
├── sardis-ledger/ # append-only audit + Merkle anchor
├── sardis-compliance/ # KYC (Didit) + AML (Elliptic)
├── sardis-cards/ # virtual cards (Stripe Issuing)
├── sardis-checkout/ # Pay with Sardis merchant flow
├── sardis-checkout-ui/ # checkout.sardis.sh · Vite
├── sardis-langchain/ # + 17 other framework adapters
└── ... # see canvas 03 for the full list

apps/

## Two Next.js apps — one for customers, one for operators.

Both apps are Next.js 16 App Router (React 19) on Vercel Edge. They share design tokens but not state.
Dashboard talks to sardis-api via authenticated fetch; landing is static with
MDX content.

## apps/dashboard — app.sardis.sh / dashboard.sardis.sh

Operator console. 43 pages under app/(dashboard)/, auth at app/auth/, public demo at app/demo/, self-service account management at app/account/. See canvas 16 for full page inventory.

apps/dashboard/
├── app/
│ ├── (dashboard)/ # 43 operator pages: agents, wallets, mandates, policies,
│ │ # approvals, kill-switch, live-events, audit-anchors, etc.
│ ├── account/ # user settings, API keys, billing
│ ├── api/ # Next.js route handlers (auth proxy)
│ ├── auth/ # better-auth sign-in/up, passkey
│ ├── demo/ # public interactive demo (no signup)
│ ├── layout.tsx # root layout + providers
│ └── providers.tsx # React Query, theme, auth

## apps/landing — sardis.sh

Marketing surface. MDX-driven blog + glossary + compare pages. Fully static with ISR for blog.

apps/landing/
├── app/
│ ├── blog/ # MDX posts + RSS
│ ├── compare/ # vs. Stripe, Crossmint, Privy, etc.
│ ├── faq/ # support FAQ
│ ├── glossary/ # agent-economy term definitions
│ ├── landing/ # variant landing pages for campaigns
│ ├── pricing/ # pricing tiers
│ ├── llms-full.txt # structured data for LLM crawlers
│ ├── llms.txt # short LLM-friendly summary
│ ├── robots.ts # programmatic robots
│ ├── sitemap.ts # programmatic sitemap
│ ├── opengraph-image.tsx # dynamic OG card
│ └── page.tsx # hero + features

packages/

## 51 packages — the product.

Every unit of shipped behavior is a package. Python packages use uv, TypeScript
packages use pnpm. Canvas 03 · Packages has the full
breakdown; here's the grouped summary.

Group
Packages
Summary

Core platform
12
sardis-core, sardis-api, sardis-chain, sardis-wallet, sardis-protocol, sardis-ledger, sardis-compliance, sardis-cards, sardis-guardrails, sardis-zk-policy, sardis-zkp, sardis-ucp

Checkout & merchant
4
sardis-checkout, sardis-checkout-ui, sardis-connect, sardis-connect-js

AI framework integrations
15+
sardis-langchain, crewai, autogpt, openai-agents, openai, agent-sdk, adk, a2a, agentkit, browser-use, composio, openclaw, stagehand, ai-sdk, activepieces, n8n-nodes-sardis, mcp-server, gpt, e2b

Payment providers
6
sardis-coinbase, sardis-lightspark, sardis-striga, sardis-mpp, sardis-mpp-proxy, sardis-ramp

SDKs & CLIs
6
sardis-sdk-python, sardis-sdk-js, sardis-cli, sardis-cli-go, sardis-cli-js, sardis-ramp-js

Misc / experimental
8
sardis-solana-program, sardis-spend-widget, sardis-telegram-bot, ui-web, and a handful of provider-specific shims

contracts/

## Solidity — the bottom of the stack.

Foundry-based. 9 contracts in src/, plus audit-surface docs and Forge scripts.
Full deep-dive in canvas 06.

contracts/
├── src/
│ ├── SardisLedgerAnchor.sol # Merkle-root anchor for audit batches
│ ├── SardisPolicyModule.sol # on-chain policy guard (Zodiac-compatible)
│ ├── SardisIdentityRegistry.sol # agent identity attestations (ERC-8004)
│ ├── SardisReputationRegistry.sol # agent reputation scores
│ ├── SardisValidationRegistry.sol # validator attestations
│ ├── SardisJobManager.sol # agent-to-agent job lifecycle
│ ├── SardisJobRegistry.sol # job registry
│ ├── SardisVerifyingPaymaster.sol # ERC-4337 paymaster
│ ├── RefundProtocol.sol # Circle RefundProtocol fork
│ ├── hooks/ # ERC-8183 trust hooks
│ ├── interfaces/ # shared interfaces
│ └── verifiers/ # ZK verifier stubs
├── script/ # Forge deploy scripts
├── test/ # Foundry tests
├── lib/ # forge-std, openzeppelin-contracts (submodules)
├── out/ # build artefacts
├── deployments/ # per-chain deployment records
├── foundry.toml # Foundry config
├── AUDIT_SURFACE.md # explicit audit scope
└── DEPLOYMENT_GUIDE.md # mainnet deploy runbook

Supporting

## Everything else — what each folder is for.

Folder
Purpose
Key files / subfolders

api/
Vercel serverless routes — checkout proxy, docs preview, health checks.
index.py, checkout/, dashboard/, landing/, base-chain-rpc/

api-proxy/
Edge proxy in front of rate-limit-heavy endpoints. Offloads Alchemy + Turnkey bursts from Cloud Run.
Cloudflare Worker source

deploy/
Deployment configs — GCP Cloud Run YAML, env templates, service specs.
gcp/, env/

scripts/
Ops tooling. Deploy scripts, health checks, data seeds, release gates, outreach pipeline.
deploy-cloudrun.sh, deploy-mainnet-contracts.sh, health_monitor.sh, seed_demo.py, outreach/, audit/, ci/

docs/
Internal + external documentation. Not all of it is public.
architecture/, engineering/, investor/, gtm/, compliance/, runbooks/, security/, plans/

docs-site/
Public documentation site — sardis.sh/docs.
Markdown + config

demos/
Runnable end-to-end demos. What a new prospect sees.
mvp_base_sepolia_demo.py, tempo_e2e_demo.py, sardis_connect_e2e_demo.py, sardis-demo-video/

examples/
Small, focused snippets per framework. One concept each.
simple_payment.py, langchain_sardis_agent.py, crewai_finance_team.py, google_adk_agent.py, vercel_ai_payment.ts

tests/
Cross-package integration tests. Per-package unit tests live in the package.
__init__.py (plus integration suites added via pytest config)

canvases/
This document. HTML canvases describing the system.
index.html, pages/, css/, js/nav.js

research/
Prototypes that may or may not ship. Never imported from production.
one-off notebooks + drafts

monitoring/
Upptime-based public status page config.
.upptimerc.yml lives at repo root; dashboards under monitoring/

marketplace/
Submissions to third-party marketplaces — MCP, Composio, Activepieces, ChatGPT Actions.
per-marketplace folders

ops/
Runbooks, incident response, on-call handoffs.
docs/on-call.md, incident templates

legal/
ToS, privacy policy, DPA, MSA drafts.
legal markdown

playground/
Scratchpad. Anything here can be deleted.
—

sardis/
Thin public Python surface — pip install sardis lands here. Re-exports from sardis-sdk-python.
__init__.py

Root files

## Repo-level manifests and gates.

File
Purpose

pyproject.toml
uv workspace root. Declares Python members and shared tooling (ruff, pytest, mypy).

pnpm-workspace.yaml
pnpm workspace root. Declares TypeScript members.

package.json
Root scripts — test, build, lint across all JS packages.

Dockerfile
Cloud Run image for sardis-api. gunicorn + uvicorn inside .venv.

docker-compose.yml
Local stack — API + Postgres + Redis for full-stack dev.

.env.example
Canonical env var list. Source of truth over CLAUDE.md for env surface.

.gitleaks.toml
Secret-scan config enforced in CI.

.pre-commit-config.yaml
pre-commit hooks — ruff, gitleaks, conventional commits.

CLAUDE.md
Agent instructions. Declared stale-by-design — trust the code.

AGENTS.md
Parallel agent harness spec.

README.md
Public-facing overview, quickstart.

OPERATIONAL_CHECKLIST.md
Pre-production readiness gate.

LAUNCH_CHECKLIST.md
Launch-day go/no-go list.

llms.txt
LLM-friendly repo summary.

Mental model

## How the workspaces fit together.

Read top-to-bottom for compile order — packages/ builds first, apps/
consume built artefacts, contracts/ are deployed separately and their ABIs are
imported back into packages/sardis-chain.

[diagram]

flowchart TD
subgraph Workspaces
P[packages/ — 51 libs]
A[apps/ — dashboard + landing]
C[contracts/ — 9 Solidity]
end
subgraph Supporting
D[docs/, docs-site/]
S[scripts/, deploy/]
DM[demos/, examples/]
T[tests/]
end

C -- ABIs / addresses --> P
P -- @sardis/* imports --> A
P -- pytest targets --> T
DM -- imports --> P
S -- deploys --> A
S -- deploys --> C
S -- deploys --> P
D -. describes .-> P
D -. describes .-> A

[/diagram]

Go deeper

## Follow-ups.

03 · System

Packages (51)

Every package, what's inside, why it exists as a separate package.

04 · Backend

API Surface

All 123 FastAPI routers grouped by domain.

06 · Backend

Smart Contracts

Every Solidity source, addresses, deploy pipeline.

16 · Product

Dashboard UI

All 43 operator pages with purpose and state.


=====================================
# PACKAGES
https://canvas.sardis.sh/packages
=====================================

Canvas 03 · System

## Packages · 51 units of shipped behavior

A unit ships as a separate package when its boundary is real — a provider that can be swapped,
a framework adapter with its own release cadence, or a protocol surface that external auditors
will pin to a version. This canvas walks all 51, grouped by purpose.

TL;DR
Core platform (12 packages) carries every line of money-moving logic. Framework integrations
(19) are thin adapters that mostly forward to the Python SDK. Payment providers (6) sit behind
uniform interfaces in sardis-core. SDKs + CLIs (6) are the public surface. The
rest are experimental or provider-specific shims.

Dependency shape

## How the core packages depend on each other.

Arrows point from consumer to provider. sardis-core is the hub — every other
package either imports from it or registers a provider into it. The API package composes
everything.

[diagram]

flowchart LR
API[sardis-api] --> CORE[sardis-core]
API --> CHAIN[sardis-chain]
API --> WALLET[sardis-wallet]
API --> PROTO[sardis-protocol]
API --> LEDGER[sardis-ledger]
API --> COMP[sardis-compliance]
API --> CHECK[sardis-checkout]
API --> CARDS[sardis-cards]
API --> GUARD[sardis-guardrails]

CHAIN --> CORE
WALLET --> CORE
PROTO --> CORE
LEDGER --> CORE
COMP --> CORE
CHECK --> CORE
CARDS --> CORE
GUARD --> CORE

CHAIN --> WALLET
CHECK --> CHAIN

SDK[sardis-sdk-python] -. HTTP .- API
SDKJS[sardis-sdk-js] -. HTTP .- API
MCP[sardis-mcp-server] --> SDKJS
LC[sardis-langchain] --> SDK

[/diagram]

Group 1 · Core platform

## The 12 packages that do the actual work.

If you removed any of these, the product would stop functioning. These carry domain logic,
provider abstractions, and runtime enforcement. All Python, all workspace-local.

Package
Key files (src/…)
Purpose — why it's separate

sardis-core
config.py, spending_policy.py, orchestrator.py, agit_policy_engine.py, anomaly_detection.py
Domain models, config, policy engine, orchestrator. Every other package imports from here. Split out so downstream can upgrade policy logic without touching transport.

sardis-api
sardis_api/routers/*.py, sardis_v2_api/routes/*.py, main.py, alembic/
FastAPI composition root + 123 routers + 107 Alembic migrations. The only package that binds to a port. Separated so contributors can iterate routes without rebuilding policy.

sardis-chain
executor.py (v2), safe_account.py, smart_router.py, cctp_forwarding.py, tempo/executor.py
Multi-chain transaction execution. Dedicated Tempo executor lives here. Isolated from wallet so chain-specific RPC quirks don't leak into signing.

sardis-wallet
manager.py, tempo_keychain.py, tempo_accounts.py, multisig.py, circle_client.py, session_manager.py, key_rotation.py
MPC signing abstraction (Turnkey / Fireblocks / Circle / Lit). Separated because swapping custody providers must be a zero-day change.

sardis-protocol
ap2.py, tap.py, x402_settlement.py, erc8122.py, erc8126.py, replay_cache_redis.py, nonce_registry.py
AP2, TAP, x402, ERC-8122/8126/8033 mandate-chain verifiers + replay protection. Pinned independently so external auditors can version-lock the verifier.

sardis-ledger
anchor.py, immutable.py, models.py, reconciliation.py, hybrid.py, db_engine.py
Append-only audit ledger + Merkle anchoring + ImmuDB hybrid mode. Separate so evidence export can be certified without dragging in the rest of the monorepo.

sardis-compliance
kyc.py, checks.py, pep.py, sar.py, circle_compliance.py, coinbase_verifications.py
KYC (Didit) + AML screening (Elliptic) + PEP + SAR filing. Separated because regulated changes have their own approval cycle.

sardis-cards
service.py, models.py, db_balance.py, db_subscriptions.py, offramp.py, auto_conversion.py
Virtual cards (Stripe Issuing primary, Lithic pending funding). Separated because issuer onboarding is a distinct revenue line.

sardis-checkout
sessions.py, payment_links.py, connectors/sardis_native.py, connectors/stripe.py
"Pay with Sardis" merchant flow — sessions, links, settlement connectors. Own release cycle driven by merchant partner ships.

sardis-guardrails
zen_engine.py, ml_fraud.py, anomaly_tuner.py, rate_limiter.py, fingerprint.py, ft3_taxonomy.py
Runtime guardrails — rule DSL (ZEN), ML anomaly, rate limits, device fingerprint. Loaded into the pre-execution pipeline as pluggable policies.

sardis-zk-policy
src/, python/, Nargo.toml, Prover.toml
Noir-based ZK proof of policy compliance. Separated because Nargo toolchain is independent of Python.

sardis-zkp
circuits/, src/, Nargo.toml
Low-level ZK circuit library — shared between zk-policy and research experiments.

sardis-ucp
capabilities/checkout.py, transports/rest.py, transports/mcp.py
Universal Commerce Protocol — a capability/transport split so the same primitives surface over REST and MCP.

Group 2 · Checkout & merchant

## Four packages that turn Sardis into a PSP.

The merchant-facing surface. UI and SDK are both cross-origin embeddable; the Python side
handles settlement and policy.

Package
Key files
Purpose — why it's separate

sardis-checkout
(see group 1)
Core backend — sessions, links, connectors.

sardis-checkout-ui
index.html, package.json, Vite + React + wagmi
The checkout page users actually see, plus the embed SDK. Served from checkout.sardis.sh. Separate deploy because it's a browser artefact, not a Python module.

sardis-connect
src/sardis_connect/*, pyproject.toml, uv.lock
Sardis Connect — zero-crypto agent-payment onboarding. Separated because Connect has a distinct onboarding flow and its own sales narrative.

sardis-connect-js
src/, package.json, tsconfig.json
JS client for Sardis Connect. Consumed by partner frontends.

Group 3 · AI framework integrations

## 19 adapters — one per place an agent lives.

Each of these wraps the Python or JS SDK in the native shape of a framework — tools,
toolkits, nodes, actions, pieces. They're intentionally thin. Real work happens in the core
platform; these just speak the local dialect.

Package
Key files
Purpose — framework shape

sardis-langchain
toolkit.py, tools.py, callbacks.py
LangChain toolkit — SardisToolkit + per-capability BaseTools. v1.0.0 on PyPI.

sardis-crewai
src/sardis_crewai/
CrewAI agent tools. Exposes Sardis primitives as BaseTools for multi-agent crews.

sardis-autogpt
sardis_autogpt/
AutoGPT block integration.

sardis-openai-agents
src/sardis_openai_agents/
OpenAI Agents SDK tool adapter.

sardis-openai
src/sardis_openai/
OpenAI direct — function-calling tool specs + runtime.

sardis-agent-sdk
src/sardis_agent_sdk/
Claude Agent SDK integration.

sardis-adk
src/sardis_adk/
Google Agent Development Kit.

sardis-a2a
src/sardis_a2a/
Google Agent-to-Agent protocol adapter — agents transacting with agents.

sardis-agentkit
src/
Coinbase AgentKit — plug Sardis policies into CDP agents.

sardis-browser-use
src/sardis_browser_use/
Browser Use — guardrails for browsing agents that need to pay.

sardis-composio
openapi.yaml, src/
Composio tool marketplace entry — OpenAPI-described.

sardis-openclaw
clawhub.json, src/
OpenClaw integration.

sardis-stagehand
package.json, TypeScript
Stagehand / Browserbase integration — JS-native browsing agents.

sardis-ai-sdk
package.json, Vercel AI SDK tools
Vercel AI SDK adapter — tool() wrappers for Next.js apps.

sardis-activepieces
package.json, TypeScript
Activepieces piece — no-code workflow automation.

n8n-nodes-sardis
nodes/, credentials/
n8n community node. Non-money triggers only — see memory: money movement postponed.

sardis-mcp-server
src/tools/spending.ts, escrow.ts, fiat.ts, mandates.ts, trust.ts, projects.ts
MCP server — Claude Desktop, Cursor, Windsurf. Each Sardis capability is an MCP tool.

sardis-gpt
openapi-actions.yaml, instructions.md
ChatGPT Custom GPT Actions spec — no runtime, just declarative.

sardis-e2b
e2b.Dockerfile, e2b.toml, examples/
E2B sandbox template — preinstalled Sardis SDK for code-execution agents.

Group 4 · Payment providers

## Six packages wrapping external payment rails.

Each sits behind a provider interface in sardis-core. Installed only when the
downstream deployment needs that rail, so a Base-only deployment doesn't pull in
Lightning dependencies.

Package
Key files
Purpose

sardis-coinbase
cdp_client.py, x402_client.py
Coinbase CDP + x402 — onramp, wallets-as-a-service, 402-Payment-Required flows.

sardis-lightspark
client.py, uma_registry.py, transfers.py, payouts.py
Lightning Network payments + UMA address registry.

sardis-striga
client.py, kyc.py, swap.py, standing_orders.py
Striga banking/cards — EU vIBANs, SEPA, embedded KYC.

sardis-mpp
client.py, stripe_method.py, services/laso.py
Stripe MPP + Tempo — Merchant Payment Protocol SDK with Sardis policy enforcement.

sardis-mpp-proxy
package.json, TypeScript
Edge proxy in front of MPP endpoints — caches capability discovery.

sardis-ramp
src/sardis_ramp/
Multi-provider on/off ramp — Coinbase Onramp primary, Striga backup.

Group 5 · SDKs & CLIs

## Six packages — the public surface.

Everything a developer installs. These are the API contract — backward-compat
guarantees apply only here.

Package
Key files
Purpose

sardis-sdk-python
docs/, examples/, pyproject.toml
Full Python SDK — pip install sardis ultimately lands here. Async client + mandates + wallets + MPC.

sardis-sdk-js
__tests__, docs/, examples/
TypeScript SDK — @sardis/sdk. Strict types, Zod validation.

sardis-cli
src/, pyproject.toml
Python CLI — sardis command.

sardis-cli-go
dist/
Go binary CLI — static-linked for ops containers that can't bring a Python runtime.

sardis-cli-js
package.json
Node-based CLI — alternative to Python CLI, bundles alongside @sardis/sdk.

sardis-ramp-js
package.json
Dedicated JS client for the ramp flow — embedded in checkout.

Group 6 · Misc & experimental

## Small packages, specific purposes.

Package
Key files
Purpose

sardis-solana-program
package.json
Solana Anchor program for non-EVM settlement experiments.

sardis-spend-widget
node_modules/, widget
Embeddable "spend meter" widget — merchants and dashboards render agent spending.

sardis-telegram-bot
__pycache__/
Telegram bot — operator notifications + manual approvals.

ui-web
components/, lib/, styles/
Shared UI component library across dashboard + checkout-ui + demos.

Go deeper

## Follow-ups.

04 · Backend

API Surface

How sardis-api wires 123 routers on top of these packages.

14 · Product

SDKs & CLIs

Public developer surface — install, auth, core methods.

15 · Product

AI Framework Integrations

How each framework adapter maps Sardis primitives to the framework's shape.

07 · Flows

Payment Flow

Which package does what during a real agent payment.


=====================================
# API SURFACE
https://canvas.sardis.sh/api-surface
=====================================

Canvas 04 · System

## API Surface

One FastAPI application, 123 routers, grouped by domain. Every router sits behind the same
middleware stack (request-id, logging, security headers, rate limit, auth) and — for anything
that moves money — funnels into a single PaymentOrchestrator.execute() chokepoint.
This canvas is the catalogue.

TL;DR
The surface is large on purpose — every money movement, policy decision, compliance check, and
webhook callback gets its own router so the seams are explicit. The surface is also uniform on
purpose — every request traverses the same middleware, the same principal extractor, and the same
pre-execution pipeline. Wide catalogue, narrow spine.

123
Routers

/api/v2
Canonical prefix

12
Domain groups

10
Middleware layers

RFC 7807
Error model

The Spine

## Middleware stack — outermost first.

Registered in packages/sardis-api/src/sardis_api/main.py. FastAPI/Starlette
middleware executes outermost-registered-first on request, innermost-first on response. If you
need to add a cross-cutting concern, add it here, not in a router.

01RequestIdMiddlewareAssigns x-request-id, propagates through logs and downstream calls.

02StructuredLoggingMiddlewareJSON log per request: method, path, status, latency_ms, principal, request_id.

03SecurityHeadersMiddlewareCSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy.

04RequestBodyLimitMiddlewareRejects payloads over configured byte cap — defends against memory-pressure DoS.

05RateLimitMiddlewareRedis-backed token bucket; fail-closed in prod if Redis unreachable (SARDIS_REDIS_URL).

06TapVerificationMiddlewareVerifies Visa TAP agent identity headers (Ed25519 / ECDSA-P256) when enabled.

07X402PaymentMiddlewareImplements x402 Payment Required on gated endpoints; verifies on-chain settlement proof.

08UsageMeteringMiddlewareRecords billable-event rows for Polar; tags by principal + endpoint class.

09ActivityLoggerMiddlewareWrite-ahead of every mutating call into the audit log (feeds Canvas 20).

10CORSMiddlewareCanonical origins: sardis.sh, app.sardis.sh, dashboard.sardis.sh, checkout.sardis.sh + merchant embed_origin.

Interaction

## One HTTPS request, traced through the stack.

From the TLS terminator on Cloud Run, through every middleware, into the router body, and
(if the route is a money-mover) into the orchestrator.

[diagram]

flowchart TD
R[HTTPS request
Cloud Run TLS term] --> MW1[RequestIdMiddleware
x-request-id]
MW1 --> MW2[StructuredLoggingMiddleware]
MW2 --> MW3[SecurityHeadersMiddleware
CSP / HSTS / Frame-Options]
MW3 --> MW4[RequestBodyLimitMiddleware]
MW4 --> MW5[RateLimitMiddleware
Redis token bucket]
MW5 --> MW6[TapVerificationMiddleware
optional — Visa TAP]
MW6 --> MW7[X402PaymentMiddleware
optional — 402 gate]
MW7 --> MW8[UsageMetering + ActivityLogger]
MW8 --> MW9[CORSMiddleware]
MW9 --> DEP[require_principal
API key / JWT / session]
DEP --> ROUTE{Router body}
ROUTE -->|money-mover| ORCH[PaymentOrchestrator.execute]
ROUTE -->|read-only| DB[(Neon Postgres)]
ORCH --> PIPE[PreExecutionPipeline
policy · KYC · mandate · rate · anomaly]
PIPE --> CHAIN[ChainExecutor → Turnkey MPC → Alchemy RPC]
CHAIN --> LED[Ledger append + Merkle anchor]
LED --> RESP[HTTP 2xx / RFC 7807 problem+json]

[/diagram]

Group 01

## Auth & Identity.

Principal establishment — how a caller proves they are allowed to address the API at all. Three
principal types coexist: human users (JWT / session), tenant service accounts (API keys
sk_live_…), and agents (AGIT identity + signed mandate).

Router
Purpose
Auth
Notable endpoints

auth.py

Human login, signup, MFA, OAuth, password reset, API-key bootstrap.

Public + JWT for protected ops

POST /login · POST /signup · POST /refresh · GET /google/callback · POST /mfa/setup · POST /api-keys

api_keys.py

Tenant API-key CRUD + scoping + hashed storage (SHA-256).

JWT (tenant admin)

Create/list/revoke keys; scope by environment (live/test).

agent_identity.py

AGIT (Agent Identity and Trust) registration — Ed25519/ECDSA-P256.

API key

Register agent identity, rotate keys, attest.

agent_auth.py

Signed-request auth for agents calling on their own behalf.

AGIT signature

Challenge-response + replay cache.

me.py

Current principal introspection — "whoami" for debugging auth.

Any

GET /me returns principal type, tenant, scopes.

email_verification.py

Email confirmation flow for new signups.

Token link

Verify, resend.

credentials.py

Provider credential vault (Turnkey / Stripe / Didit keys per tenant).

JWT (tenant admin)

Encrypted at rest; never returned cleartext.

fides_identity.py

FIDES decentralized trust graph identity lookup.

API key

Trust score + attestation chain for an agent.

Group 02

## Wallets & Agents.

Non-custodial MPC wallets plus the agent records that own them. Wallet-facing endpoints never
take a private key — signing is deferred to Turnkey (or Fireblocks / Circle) at execution time.

Router
Purpose
Auth
Notable endpoints

wallets.py

Wallet lifecycle + balances + smart-account upgrade + transfer.

API key / JWT

POST /wallets · GET /{id}/balance · GET /{id}/balances · POST /{id}/transfer · POST /{id}/freeze · POST /{id}/upgrade-smart-account

agents.py

Agent CRUD, metadata, owning-tenant linkage.

API key

Create agent, attach wallet, assign policy.

agent_registry.py

Public directory of registered agents for discovery / A2A.

Mixed

Search by capability, name, owner.

agent_heartbeat.py

Liveness signal — stale agents get KYA-suspended.

AGIT signature

Periodic ping; last-seen feeds trust score.

agent_activity.py

Per-agent activity stream (auth events, tx, policy hits).

API key

Paginated timeline for dashboard.

agent_events.py

Emit + query structured agent events (used by KYA & anomaly).

API key

Post event; stream by agent_id.

groups.py

Agent groups — shared budget + policy inheritance.

API key

Create group, attach agents, group-level spending cap.

organizations.py

Tenant (organization) management — users, roles, env scoping.

JWT (org admin)

Invite member, switch role, rotate org API keys.

holds.py

Reserve funds against a wallet for a pending charge.

API key

Create / capture / release.

Group 03

## Payments & Mandates.

The core money-movers. Every route here ultimately calls
PaymentOrchestrator.execute() — there is no private fast path. A mandate is the
delegated authority from a principal to an agent; a payment is what happens when that authority
is exercised.

Router
Purpose
Auth
Notable endpoints

pay.py

Single-shot agent payment — the flagship sardis.pay() path.

API key + AGIT

POST /pay — amount, to, reason, optional policy override.

mandates.py

Spending mandates: create, validate, execute, cancel.

API key

POST /mandates · POST /{id}/validate · POST /{id}/execute · POST /{id}/cancel

mandate_delegation.py

Principal → agent delegation chain, sub-delegation with caps.

Signed mandate

Issue, revoke, introspect.

mandate_subscriptions.py

Recurring mandates (subscriptions with budget windows).

API key

Start / pause / next-renewal.

spending_mandates.py

Natural-language spending-mandate creation and enforcement.

API key

Parse NL → structured mandate; bind to agent.

ap2.py

AP2 (Agent Payment Protocol) mandate-chain verification + execute.

AP2 signed chain

POST /ap2/payments/execute — verifies Intent→Cart→Payment.

batch_payments.py

Submit many payments in a single request; per-item policy result.

API key

Atomic batch · per-item settle.

streaming_payments.py

Micropayment streams (per-second / per-token rates).

API key

Open stream, tick, close.

payments_refund.py

Initiate refunds through Circle RefundProtocol + PSP rails.

API key

Full / partial / dispute-linked.

subscriptions.py

Merchant subscription billing (Polar adapter).

API key

Plan CRUD, usage roll-up.

mpp.py

Stripe Merchant Payment Protocol (MPP) integration for Tempo mainnet.

API key

MPP quote → pay → receipt.

swap.py

Token swaps via DEX router (Tempo, Base).

API key

Quote, execute, slippage-protect.

payment_objects.py

Canonical payment object retrieval (ID → full record + state machine).

API key

Read by id, filter by status.

Group 04

## Policy & Compliance.

The gate. Natural-language policies in, structured enforcement out. KYC via Didit, sanctions via
Elliptic, KYA (Know Your Agent) implemented internally. Every rejection is audited with a reason
code.

Router
Purpose
Auth
Notable endpoints

policies.py

Spending policy CRUD, preview, apply, history, recommendations.

API key (+ MPP gate on parse/check)

POST /parse · POST /preview · POST /apply · POST /check · GET /templates · GET /{agent}/history

policy_analytics.py

Aggregate policy-decision metrics (allow rate, top denied reason codes).

JWT

Trend by agent, by reason.

policy_simulation.py

Dry-run a candidate policy against historical traffic.

JWT

Replay window, impact report.

fallback_policies.py

Safety-net policies applied when a primary policy errors.

JWT

Global deny-cap, max-amount backstop.

approvals.py · approval_config.py

Human-in-the-loop approval queue for over-threshold agent requests.

JWT

Create request, approve / reject, notify via webhook.

compliance.py

KYC verify, KYA register/check/suspend, sanctions screen.

JWT / API key

POST /kyc/verify · GET /kyc/{agent} · POST /kya/register · POST /sanctions/screen · POST /sanctions/block

kyc_onboarding.py

Didit session bootstrap → webhook → sk_live_ issuance.

JWT

POST /kyc/initiate.

compliance_export.py

Regulated evidence bundles (PDF + JSON + Merkle proof).

JWT (admin)

Export by date range / agent.

trust.py

FIDES trust-graph mutation (upvote, revoke, peer-attest).

AGIT signature

Graph ops + local trust score.

anomaly.py

Anomaly detector scoring + rule tuning.

JWT

Score a request, tune threshold.

erc8183.py

ERC-8183 policy-guard hooks for on-chain enforcement.

API key

Guard check, USDC cap, fee cap.

guardrails (via sardis-guardrails)

Agent guardrail presets referenced from policies.py.

API key

Template by persona (buyer, trader, ops).

Group 05

## Checkout & Merchant.

"Pay with Sardis" — the consumer-facing checkout. Public endpoints auth by client_secret
(never session id); merchant management endpoints auth by require_principal.
Hardened against double-pay via SELECT … FOR UPDATE NOWAIT and DB-unique
idempotency keys.

Router
Purpose
Auth
Notable endpoints

checkout.py

Low-level checkout object CRUD + public status read.

Public (client_secret) / API key

POST /checkout · GET /{id} · GET /payment-methods

merchant_checkout.py

Merchant-scoped checkout sessions, SSE stream, links.

Merchant API key / public link

Create session, /stream SSE, reusable checkout link, external-wallet confirm.

checkout_controls.py

Per-merchant checkout controls (min/max amount, allowed tokens).

Merchant JWT

Upsert controls.

merchants.py

Merchant account CRUD, settlement address, embed_origin.

JWT

Create merchant, rotate keys.

secure_checkout.py

Step-up auth + SCA-style challenges for high-value sessions.

Session token

Start challenge, verify.

invoices.py

Invoice issuance + stable payment link.

API key

Create, mark paid on settlement.

receipts.py

Canonical receipt retrieval (rendered + JSON).

Public (receipt_id)

Signed URL for the PDF.

escrow_disputes.py

Dispute flow backed by Circle RefundProtocol.

API key

Open, respond, resolve.

Group 06

## Cards.

Virtual-card issuance for agents, via Stripe Issuing (primary, live-ready) and Lithic
(sandbox; blocked on $5M funding requirement). Cards are policy-gated at auth time — every
transaction hits PreExecutionPipeline before Stripe approves.

Router
Purpose
Auth
Notable endpoints

cards.py
Card issuance, freeze/unfreeze, limits, PAN reveal.
API key
Create card, update limits, freeze.

virtual_cards.py
Ephemeral single-use cards for one-off merchant payments.
API key
Mint + auto-destroy on settle.

stablecoin_cards.py
USDC-funded cards — wallet balance backs the card float.
API key
Link wallet to card, auto-top-up.

partner_card_webhooks.py
Inbound auth/clearing hooks from Stripe / Lithic.
HMAC signature
Authorize, clear, reverse.

Group 07

## Ramps & Treasury.

On/off-ramps, FX, and operator-level treasury functions. Coinbase Onramp (hosted, free) is
default; Stripe Connect + Striga + Bridge handle the bank-rail side; Lightspark wires up
Lightning for instant micro-settlement.

Router
Purpose
Auth
Notable endpoints

onramp.py
Fiat → USDC flow (Coinbase Onramp URL signing).
API key
Token issuance, address validation.

offramp.py
USDC → fiat payout (Stripe / Striga / Bridge).
API key
Quote, initiate, status.

ramp.py
Unified ramp router (on + off, provider-agnostic).
API key
Provider select by region.

fiat_rails.py
Raw fiat rail ops (ACH, SEPA, PIX, FPS).
API key
Create payout, track status.

stripe_connect.py
Stripe Connect account onboarding + payouts.
JWT
Create account link, check status.

striga.py
Striga vIBAN issuance + SEPA in/out.
API key
Create vIBAN, withdraw.

bridge.py
Bridge multi-rail router (ACH / SEPA / PIX).
API key
Transfer, track.

lightspark.py
Lightning Network send/receive.
API key
Pay invoice, create invoice.

treasury.py · treasury_ops.py
Treasury balances, rebalancing, liquidity ops.
JWT (admin)
Move between pools, FX hedge.

fx.py
Spot rates + conversion quotes.
API key
Quote, lock, execute.

currency.py
Currency + token metadata registry.
Public
List supported tokens per chain.

funding.py · funding_capabilities.py · stripe_funding.py
Merchant funding sources + capabilities matrix.
JWT
Attach source, check capabilities.

faucet.py
Testnet faucet for Base Sepolia / Tempo testnet.
API key (rate-limited)
Drip USDC to dev wallet.

Group 08

## Ledger & Audit.

Append-only ledger — every decision (allow / deny / error) is a row. Batches are Merkle-rooted
and anchored on-chain via SardisLedgerAnchor.sol. Regulators get cryptographic
evidence, not trust-our-logs.

Router
Purpose
Auth
Notable endpoints

ledger.py

Query ledger entries + Merkle-path verification.

JWT / API key

GET /entries · GET /entries/{tx_id} · GET /entries/{tx_id}/verify · GET /recent · GET /stats

audit_anchors.py
On-chain anchor batch visibility (batch id → tx hash).
JWT
List batches, verify anchor.

evidence.py · evidence_export.py
Evidence bundle builder (decisions + mandate chain + receipts).
JWT (admin)
Build by tx, export zip.

data_export.py
Tenant-scope bulk export (GDPR / SOC2).
JWT (admin)
Start job, download when ready.

attestation.py
Issue attestation envelope — signed claim about a decision.
API key
Create, verify.

transactions.py
Canonical transaction list view for dashboard.
JWT
Filter by status / agent / date.

settlements.py
Settlement batch records (fiat + on-chain).
JWT
Batch detail, reconciliation status.

outcomes.py
Final-state outcome store (succeeded / refunded / disputed).
API key
Read by payment_id.

reports.py
Pre-computed financial reports (daily / monthly).
JWT
Fetch, schedule.

Group 09

## Protocol Gateways — AP2 / A2A / x402 / ACP / SPT / CPN.

Sardis is the universal payment layer for the agent economy — which means speaking every
emerging protocol. Each router here adapts one external standard to the same internal
orchestrator.

Router
Purpose
Auth
Notable endpoints

x402.py

HTTP 402 Payment Required — verify + settle + challenge issuance.

x402 payload

POST /verify · POST /settle · GET /settlements/{payment_id} · POST /dry-run · POST /challenges

ap2.py
Google/Visa/Mastercard AP2 — Intent→Cart→Payment chain.
AP2 signed chain
POST /payments/execute.

a2a.py · a2a_payments.py
Google Agent-to-Agent protocol — discovery + payment.
A2A identity + API key
Public discovery router + authed payment router.

acp.py
Agent Commerce Protocol adapter.
ACP signature
Quote + purchase.

spt.py
Stripe Payment Token protocol.
API key
Token exchange.

cpn.py · cpn_webhooks.py
Commercial Payment Network router + inbound hooks.
Mixed
Send + reconcile.

mvp.py
Minimal demo/path-of-least-resistance endpoints for demos.
API key
Quick pay for pitches.

Group 10

## Webhooks — inbound & outbound.

Inbound webhooks (partners → Sardis) are signature-verified with HMAC or provider-specific
schemes and deduped by event_id. Outbound webhooks (Sardis → merchant) use an
outbox pattern with at-least-once delivery and per-delivery tracking.

Router
Direction
Signer
Notes

webhooks.py
Outbound
Sardis HMAC
Merchant endpoint configuration, delivery log, replay.

stripe_webhooks.py
Inbound
Stripe signing secret
Payment intents, charge events, Issuing authorizations.

stripe_spt_webhooks.py
Inbound
Stripe
SPT-specific event types.

mastercard_webhooks.py
Inbound
Mastercard
AP2 card-network callbacks.

visa_tap_webhooks.py
Inbound
Visa TAP
TAP identity and auth events.

partner_card_webhooks.py
Inbound
HMAC
Stripe Issuing / Lithic card auths.

cpn_webhooks.py
Inbound
Partner-specific
CPN network callbacks.

polar_webhook.py
Inbound
Polar
Billing lifecycle (until Stripe Atlas).

Group 11

## Admin & Operations.

Platform-operator surface. Separate from tenant APIs — these endpoints require a Sardis-internal
JWT and are scoped to ops / SRE / support roles. The kill switch here is the one Elizabeth Yin
and regulators want to see.

Router
Purpose
Auth
Notes

admin.py
General admin surface (tenant search, impersonate in support mode).
Ops JWT
Audit-logged.

admin_reconciliation.py
Manual reconciliation tools for settlement mismatches.
Ops JWT
Compare ledger vs. provider ledger.

emergency.py
Emergency stop (kill-switch) for tenant / agent / chain.
Ops JWT + MFA
Fail-closed; propagates across regions.

enterprise_support.py
Named-contact support routes for enterprise tier.
Ops JWT
Ticket create / escalate.

alerts.py · ws_alerts.py
Alert rules + WebSocket stream for dashboards.
JWT
Rule CRUD, live stream.

reliability.py
SRE-facing SLO data + feature flags.
Ops JWT
Error budget, flag toggles.

sandbox.py
Test-environment management for merchants.
JWT
Provision, reset.

simulation.py
Traffic replay + chaos simulation.
Ops JWT
Replay window, fault inject.

execution_modes.py
Switch chain mode (live / simulate / dry-run).
Ops JWT
Tenant-scoped.

environment_templates.py
Pre-baked env bundles for onboarding.
Ops JWT
Clone to new tenant.

workflow_templates.py
Reusable workflow (policy + wallet + mandate) templates.
JWT
Instantiate per tenant.

billing.py
Billing state (Polar today, Stripe post-Atlas).
JWT
Read invoice, usage.

usage.py
Raw usage-metering data.
JWT
Drill-down by endpoint class.

plugins.py
Plugin registry / enable / disable.
Ops JWT
Tenant opt-in plugins.

service_directory.py
Internal service catalog for A2A discovery.
Mixed
Register, list.

marketplace.py
Marketplace listings (agents / policies / templates).
Mixed
List, install.

counterparties.py
Known counterparty registry.
JWT
Tag, flag, whitelist.

notifications.py
Multi-channel notification fanout (email, Slack, webhook).
JWT
Rule CRUD.

dev.py
Dev-only helpers (seed data, principal impersonation).
Dev mode only
Disabled in prod.

exceptions.py
Central exception handlers → RFC 7807 problem+json.
n/a
Wired via app.add_exception_handler.

Group 12

## Analytics, Metrics & Live Events.

Read-side infrastructure: dashboard metrics, SDK telemetry, event streams. Event-stream
endpoints use Server-Sent Events (SSE) with polling fallback; alerts use a WebSocket.

Router
Purpose

analytics.py
Tenant analytics (spend by agent, success rate, policy hit rate).

dashboard_metrics.py
Pre-aggregated numbers for the dashboard cards.

metrics.py
Raw time-series metrics (Prometheus-compatible).

sdk_metrics.py
SDK-side telemetry ingest (install count, version, error).

event_stream.py
SSE event stream per tenant + agent.

mpp_demo.py
MPP demo-day endpoints for Tempo launch (2026-03-18).

Full catalogue

Contract

## Error model — RFC 7807 problem+json.

Every non-2xx response conforms to RFC 7807.
Clients key off type (stable URI) and code (enum) — not the HTTP
status alone, and definitely not the title string.

Shape

Every error carries: type (URI ref), title (short human), status
(HTTP code), detail (human detail), code (machine enum),
request_id (for grep'ing logs), and when applicable reason_code
(policy engine) or retry_after (rate limit).

Categories

400 validation_error
Pydantic schema mismatch — array of field errors in errors[].

401 auth_required
Missing / malformed principal. WWW-Authenticate set.

403 policy_denied
Pre-execution pipeline rejection. reason_code names the gate.

409 conflict
Idempotency collision or state-machine violation.

422 mandate_invalid
AP2 / TAP / mandate chain verification failed.

429 rate_limited
Redis token-bucket refusal. retry_after in seconds.

500 internal
Unclassified — always carries request_id.

503 unavailable
Dependency fail-closed (Redis, KYA, FIDES).

Go deeper

## Follow-ups.

01 · System

Architecture Overview

How the API layer fits in the seven-layer stack.

07 · Flows

Payment Flow

One request traced through every router and orchestrator call.

08 · Flows

Policy Engine

What /policies/parse and /policies/check actually do.

13 · Surface

Multi-Protocol Gateway

Deep dive on AP2 / A2A / x402 / ACP / SPT adapters.


=====================================
# DATABASE
https://canvas.sardis.sh/database
=====================================

Canvas 05 · Backend

## Database Schema

One Neon Postgres database. 107 sequentially-numbered SQL migrations under
packages/sardis-api/migrations/. No ORM is doing the heavy lifting — tables are
defined by hand, in SQL, and applied in strict order. When Postgres is the source of truth for
money, legibility beats abstraction.

TL;DR
Migrations are the canonical record of how the schema evolved. Reading them in order is the
fastest way to understand what Sardis does: identity first, then wallets, then policies, then
ledger, then merchants, then mandates, then protocol-specific tables (Tempo, A2A, x402),
and finally the ba_-prefixed better-auth surface.

107
Migrations

Neon
Postgres, serverless

50+
Tables

NNN_*.sql
Naming convention

Conventions

## How this schema is written.

Hand-written SQL, applied in order

Migrations live in packages/sardis-api/migrations/ as
NNN_description.sql. Applied with
psql $DATABASE_URL -f <file>. A schema_migrations table guards
against double-apply. No Alembic, no Django, no Drizzle — direct SQL keeps the diff honest.

Append-only, additive-only

Destructive changes (drop column, tighten constraint) must be proposed as a new migration with
a rollback companion (see 001_initial_schema_rollback.sql). ADD COLUMN IF
NOT EXISTS is used liberally so migrations are re-runnable on partially-applied dbs.

Three primary-key styles, chosen on purpose

uuid for records a human might quote (orgs, wallets, merchants). text
prefixed IDs (mandate_…, appr_…, mst_…) for records an
agent or operator will handle. bigserial only for high-churn internal queues
(reconciliation_queue).

ba_ prefix for better-auth tables

The better-auth library manages its own schema (ba_user, ba_session,
ba_apikey, …) and coexists with legacy Sardis auth tables. Prefix avoids
collisions while the dual-path is live. See migrations 077, 097, 098.

Indexes travel with the columns that need them

Every migration that adds a frequently-queried column also adds the index. Migration 087
(missing_indexes) exists to clean up what slipped through. Query plans stay
predictable.

JSONB for open-ended, columns for load-bearing

metadata, settings, merchant_scope are JSONB because
shape evolves. Money columns (amount_per_tx, spent_total) are
NUMERIC(20,6) because floats in finance cost real money.

Core shape

## ER diagram — the payment spine.

A simplified entity map of the tables every money-moving request touches. Ownership flows left
to right; evidence flows right to left.

[diagram]

erDiagram
organizations ||--o{ agents : "owns"
organizations ||--o{ ba_user : "has members"
organizations ||--o{ merchants : "operates"
agents ||--o{ wallets : "controls"
agents ||--o{ agent_group_members : "joins"
agent_groups ||--o{ agent_group_members : "groups"
agents ||--o{ spending_policies : "is governed by"
spending_policies ||--o{ policy_versions : "has history"
agents ||--o{ spending_mandates : "is delegated"
spending_mandates ||--o{ mandate_state_transitions : "audit log"
spending_mandates ||--o{ spending_mandates : "parent of"
spending_mandates ||--o{ policy_decisions : "produces"
policy_versions ||--o{ policy_decisions : "evaluates"
wallets ||--o{ transactions : "submits"
transactions ||--o{ receipts : "settles into"
transactions ||--o{ ledger_entries : "records"
ledger_entries ||--o{ merkle_batches : "rolls up into"
merchants ||--o{ merchant_checkout_sessions : "sells via"
merchant_checkout_sessions ||--o{ transactions : "settles through"

[/diagram]

Timeline

## Representative migrations — read these to understand the system.

20 migrations sampled from the full 107. Each one corresponds to a real product moment; reading
them in order is the fastest onboarding you can do as a backend engineer.

#
Name
What it added

001
initial_schema
Organizations, agents — the tenancy root. Rollback companion ships alongside.

002
spending_policy_state
spent_total on spending_policies; multi-token time_window_limits. First enforceable spending limits.

003
ledger_compliance_tables
Promotes ledger receipts and compliance tables from runtime CREATE IF NOT EXISTS into durable SQL.

004
approvals
Human-in-the-loop approval queue with approval_status enum (pending / approved / denied / expired / cancelled).

013
ledger_merkle_receipts
Merkle batching for ledger evidence — the foundation for on-chain anchors.

014
agent_groups
Multi-agent governance — group-level budget (per-tx, daily, monthly, total) and agent_group_members linkage.

017
erc4337_wallet_fields
Adds smart-account / user-op / entry-point columns on wallets for Safe v1.4.1 upgrade.

019
canonical_ledger_state
Canonicalizes the state machine — every payment moves through the same FSM regardless of rail.

030
merchant_checkout
"Pay with Sardis" — merchants, merchant_checkout_sessions, settlement preferences (USDC / fiat).

031
checkout_hardening
client_secret, idempotency_key (unique index), platform_fee_amount, embed_origin, reusable checkout links, webhook delivery dedup.

037
durable_idempotency
Idempotency-key storage promoted from Redis to Postgres for durability guarantees.

040
policy_versions
Immutable version records for every policy change — policy_json, hash, parent_version_id.

041
policy_decisions
Evidence log — every evaluate() produces a signed decision (verdict, steps_json, evidence_hash) for SOC 2.

056
erc8183_jobs
Job queue for ERC-8183 policy-guard on-chain enforcement.

057
reconciliation_queue
Persistent recon queue — replaces in-memory implementation so pending ledger appends survive restarts.

071
spending_mandates
Core spending-mandate table — principal, issuer, merchant_scope, amount_{per_tx, daily, weekly, monthly, total}, lifecycle enum.

072
mandate_transitions
Audit log of every lifecycle change (activate, suspend, revoke, expire) keyed to spending_mandates.id.

074
tempo_support
TIP-20 gas model — memo, fee_token, fee_token_amount, tip20_transfer on transactions.

077
better_auth_tables
ba_user, ba_session, ba_account, ba_verification — session-based auth path.

080
mandate_trees
Hierarchical mandate delegation — parent_mandate_id, delegation_depth, root_mandate_id. CFO → dept → agent.

085
a2a_tables
Google A2A — settlements and escrows tables for agent-to-agent commerce.

097
ba_apikey_table
better-auth API-key plugin table — hashed key, rate-limit fields, permissions JSONB, mode (live / test).

106
agent_registry
Public agent directory — capabilities, supported_protocols, trust_score, kya_status.

Domains

## Core tables by domain.

## Identity & Tenancy

Table
PK
Key columns
Intro'd

organizations
uuid
external_id, settings jsonb, is_active
001

agents
uuid
organization_id FK, external_id, metadata jsonb
001

agent_groups
uuid
budget_per_tx · budget_daily · budget_monthly · budget_total · merchant_policy
014

agent_group_members
uuid
group_id FK, agent_id, UNIQUE(group_id, agent_id)
014

agent_registry
uuid
capabilities[], supported_protocols[], trust_score, kya_status
106

api_keys
uuid
hashed key (SHA-256), scopes, environment
001 / 076

ba_user
text
email UNIQUE, org_id, role, kyc_status
077

ba_session
text
token UNIQUE, expires_at, user_id FK
077

ba_apikey
text
key (hashed), prefix, config_id (live/test), rate-limit fields, permissions jsonb
097

## Wallets & Transactions

Table
PK
Key columns
Intro'd

wallets
uuid
agent_id FK, chain, address, smart-account fields (entry_point, user_op_nonce)
001 · 017

wallet_addresses
uuid
wallet_id FK, chain, address — one row per chain for cross-chain wallet
008

transactions
uuid
wallet_id, chain, tx_hash, memo, fee_token, tip20_transfer
001 · 074

receipts
tx_id
tx_hash, chain, block_number, status, audit_anchor
003

holds
uuid
wallet_id, amount, expires_at — reservation against pending charge
035

wallet_freeze_events
uuid
wallet_id, reason, initiated_by, emergency flag
006 · 064

execution_intents
uuid
canonical intent record — what was authorized vs. what happened
038

## Policies & Mandates

Table
PK
Key columns
Intro'd

spending_policies
uuid
agent_id, spent_total, policy_json, active
001 · 002

policy_versions
text
agent_id, version, policy_json, policy_text, hash, parent_version_id (self-FK)
040

policy_decisions
text
verdict, steps_json, evidence_hash, group_hierarchy[], mandate_id, policy_version_id FK
041

spending_mandates
text (mandate_…)
principal_id, issuer_id, merchant_scope jsonb, purpose_scope, amount_{per_tx, daily, weekly, monthly, total}, currency, parent_mandate_id, delegation_depth, root_mandate_id
071 · 080

mandate_state_transitions
text (mst_…)
mandate_id FK, from_status, to_status, changed_by, reason, metadata
072

time_window_limits
uuid
policy_id, window, max_amount, currency
001 · 002

Canonical payment record + state machine

uuid
Composable funding unit for complex flows

checkout_mandate_bridge
uuid
Binds a checkout session to a spending mandate
103

Integrity

## How the schema defends correctness.

Unique idempotency

merchant_checkout_sessions.idempotency_key has a partial UNIQUE index. Two
concurrent creates with the same key collide at the database — there is no "last write wins"
path that could double-pay a customer (migration 031).

Row locks at claim time

Checkout claim uses SELECT … FOR UPDATE NOWAIT — if another worker already holds
the lock, the caller fails fast rather than waits, and the client retries. Prevents two
settlement workers from racing the same session.

Enum types for state machines

approval_status, approval_urgency, CHECK constraints on
kya_status, settlement_type. Invalid states become database errors,
not runtime surprises.

Cascading deletes where ownership is clear

ba_session.user_id → ba_user(id) ON DELETE CASCADE;
mandate_state_transitions.mandate_id → spending_mandates(id) ON DELETE CASCADE.
Ownership deletions don't leave orphans.

Reconciliation queue survives restarts

If a ledger append fails after on-chain execution, the reconciliation entry lands in
reconciliation_queue with status='pending' — not in RAM. A worker
drains it on the next process boot (migration 057).

Policy evidence is cryptographically hashed

policy_versions.hash and policy_decisions.evidence_hash mean a
policy decision can be proven-in-court without trusting the Sardis log pipeline. Migration 040
and 041.

Full catalogue
