=====================================
# CONTRACTS
https://canvas.sardis.sh/contracts
=====================================

Canvas 06 · Backend

## Smart Contracts

Sardis is deliberately thin on-chain. The heavy lifting — policy evaluation, mandate
verification, KYC, compliance — happens off-chain where it can be iterated safely. On-chain we
only deploy what needs public, auditable, tamper-evident behaviour: identity registries, an
evidence anchor, an escrow-based job primitive (ERC-8183), and a refund protocol.

TL;DR
Nine .sol files live under contracts/src/. Two are deprecated on
purpose (SardisPolicyModule → Zodiac Roles; SardisVerifyingPaymaster →
Circle Paymaster) — kept for reference, not deployed. The rest implement ERC-8004 (agent
identity), ERC-8183 (job escrow), a minimal ledger anchor, and Circle's audited
RefundProtocol vendored in-tree.

9
Contracts

Foundry
Build & test

0.8.20 – 0.8.24
Solidity

OpenZeppelin
Primitives

ERC-8004 / 8183 / 4337
Standards

Principles

## Why so little on-chain?

Prefer audited standards over bespoke contracts

Wallet custody: Safe v1.4.1 (pre-deployed on every EVM chain, $100B+ TVL). Policy module:
Zodiac Roles (0x9646fDAD…). Paymaster: Circle Paymaster
(0x0578cFB2…). Sardis ships no custom money-handling contract if an audited one
fits — zero audit surface at MVP.

On-chain for evidence, off-chain for decisions

Policy decisions live in Postgres (migration 041) because policies change. What goes on-chain
is the Merkle root of a batch of those decisions (SardisLedgerAnchor) —
tamper-evidence without tying policy logic to chain latency or upgrade ceremony.

Standards-compliant identity & reputation

Agent identity is ERC-8004 (ERC-721 token + reputation + validation registries). A wallet can
prove "I am agent #42 in this registry" without trusting a Sardis API. Reputation is
submitted by clients, signed on-chain, and queryable by any counterparty.

ERC-8183 for commerce, not custom escrows

Escrow-based agent commerce follows the ERC-8183 spec with a strict state machine and
selector-based hooks (IACPHook). Easier for counterparties to integrate than a
Sardis-only escrow; easier to audit.

Hook-dispatch over inheritance

SardisJobManager routes hook calls through IACPHook with a gas
limit — before-hooks can block, after-hooks are fire-and-forget. Keeps hook failures from
griefing the core flow while still letting composability extensions run.

Apache 2.0 for RefundProtocol

RefundProtocol.sol is Circle's audited implementation, vendored in-tree under
Apache 2.0 — not a rewrite. The point is to reuse the audit, not re-audit.

The Catalogue

## Every contract, file by file.

Source: contracts/src/. Purpose statements are quoted or paraphrased from each
file's NatSpec.

SardisLedgerAnchor.sol

contracts/src/SardisLedgerAnchor.sol · Solidity ^0.8.20

MIT
Production
Ownable

Stores Merkle roots of audit-log batches for tamper evidence. Minimal by design — the
ledger itself stays off-chain in Postgres; only the root crosses the chain boundary.
anchor(root, anchorId) writes once, never twice; verify(root)
returns the timestamp (0 if never anchored). This is the contract that makes "trust our
logs" obsolete.

Key API
anchor(bytes32 root, string anchorId) · verify(bytes32) → uint256 timestamp

Event
Anchored(bytes32 indexed root, string anchorId, uint256 timestamp)

Upstream
sardis-ledger batches → on-chain call from ops service.

SardisIdentityRegistry.sol

Solidity ^0.8.24 · implements ERC-8004 Identity

MIT
ERC-8004
ERC-721
EIP-712
ERC-1271

ERC-8004 Identity Registry — on-chain agent identity as an ERC-721 token. Global addressing:
eip155:{chainId}:{registryAddress}#{agentId}. Three register()
overloads (URI + metadata, URI only, bare). Agent-wallet linking verified via EIP-712
signature (EOA) or ERC-1271 (smart contract). Wallet is cleared on token transfer so the new
owner has to re-verify.

Key API
register(...) · setAgentWallet(agentId, wallet, deadline, sig) · setMetadata(agentId, key, value)

Invariant
Reserved key agentWallet cannot be written via setMetadata — only via signed flow.

Spec
eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-8004

SardisReputationRegistry.sol

Solidity ^0.8.24 · implements ERC-8004 Reputation

MIT
ERC-8004

Client-submitted on-chain feedback for agents. Each feedback is int128 value +
decimals + tags + endpoint + off-chain URI + hash. Revocation is soft (marked, never
deleted). Agent owners (or approved operators) can submit response records pointing at
their side of the story.

Rules
Submitter MUST NOT be the agent owner; valueDecimals in [0, 18]; only original submitter can revoke.

Linked to
SardisIdentityRegistry (ERC-721) — feedback keyed by agentId.

SardisValidationRegistry.sol

Solidity ^0.8.24 · implements ERC-8004 Validation

MIT
ERC-8004

Agent owners request validation from a designated validator; the validator responds with a
score (0–100). Records are queryable by requestHash. Validators can respond
multiple times to update the score.

Record shape
validatorAddress · agentId · response (uint8) · responseHash · tag · lastUpdate.

Immutable
identityRegistry address is set at construction.

SardisJobManager.sol

Solidity ^0.8.24 · implements ERC-8183

MIT
ERC-8183
Escrow
ReentrancyGuard
Pausable

Escrow-based job primitive for agentic commerce. Full ERC-8183 state machine:
Open → Funded → Submitted → Completed/Rejected/Expired with
claimRefund on expiry. Hook dispatch through IACPHook — a
before hook called with HOOK_GAS_LIMIT whose revert blocks the
action, and a fire-and-forget after hook whose revert is ignored. Bounded
gas prevents hook-griefing.

State machine
Open → Funded (fund) · Open → Rejected (reject) · Funded → Submitted (submit) · Submitted → Completed (complete) · Submitted → Rejected (reject) · Funded/Submitted → Expired (claimRefund).

Caps (MVP)
1% fee cap, USDC-only, trust hooks disabled post-launch — relaxable post-audit.

Spec
eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-8183

SardisJobRegistry.sol

Solidity ^0.8.24

MIT
Reputation
Ownable

On-chain reputation store for ERC-8183 agents. Tracks completed / rejected jobs, earnings,
spending per agent. Only authorized writers (the Job Manager, trusted hooks) can update
records. Trust score is computed on-chain in basis points (0–10000):
baseScore = completed / (completed + rejected) × 10000 + up to a 10% volume
bonus capped at 10000.

Auth model
authorizedWriters mapping — only owner can toggle.

Companion of
SardisJobManager — Manager calls into Registry on every complete/reject.

RefundProtocol.sol

Solidity ^0.8.24 · Circle, vendored

Apache 2.0
Circle (audited)
EIP-712

Circle's RefundProtocol, vendored in-tree. Time-locked payments (up to 180
days) with arbiter-enabled early withdrawal authorised by EIP-712 signed
EarlyWithdrawalByArbiter messages. Sardis uses it for dispute-resolved refunds
so neither buyer nor merchant has to trust Sardis custody.

Payment struct
to · amount · releaseTimestamp · refundTo · withdrawnAmount · refunded.

Max lockup
180 days (MAX_LOCKUP_SECONDS).

Why in-tree
Pin version under Sardis build/audit; swap upstream fixes with explicit review.

SardisPolicyModule.sol

Solidity ^0.8.20 · Safe module (reference only)

Deprecated
MIT
Safe module

Safe module that enforces spending policies for AI agent wallets — per-tx + daily limits,
co-sign mode (10× limit when co-signed by Sardis), merchant allow/deny lists, token
allowlist, pause/unpause.
Superseded by the pre-deployed, audited Zodiac Roles module at
0x9646fDAD06d3e24444381f44362a3B0eB343D337. Kept for reference; not deployed to
mainnet for MVP (no custom audit dependency).

Status
Do-not-deploy. Any agent-wallet policy enforcement routes through Zodiac Roles.

SardisVerifyingPaymaster.sol

Solidity ^0.8.20 · ERC-4337 paymaster (reference only)

Deprecated
MIT
ERC-4337

ERC-4337 verifying paymaster with wallet allowlist, per-op cap, and daily sponsor cap.
Validation optionally consumes off-chain verifier signatures in paymasterData.
Superseded by Circle Paymaster
(0x0578cFB241215b77442a541325d6A4E6dFE700Ec, permissionless, USDC gas). Kept
for reference; not deployed.

Today
Primary: Circle Paymaster. Backup: Pimlico (sponsor model).

Supporting dirs

## Hooks, interfaces, verifiers.

Path
Role

src/hooks/SardisReputationHook.sol
IACPHook impl that writes into SardisJobRegistry on complete/reject — keeps reputation accounting in-contract.

src/hooks/SardisTrustHook.sol
IACPHook impl gating ERC-8183 actions on a trust-score threshold from the reputation registry.

src/interfaces/IACPHook.sol
Selector-based hook dispatch interface — beforeAction, afterAction.

src/interfaces/IIdentityRegistry.sol
ERC-8004 Identity Registry interface.

src/interfaces/IReputationRegistry.sol
ERC-8004 Reputation Registry interface.

src/interfaces/IValidationRegistry.sol
ERC-8004 Validation Registry interface.

src/interfaces/IJob.sol
ERC-8183 job interface — events, errors, state enum.

src/interfaces/IJobRegistry.sol
Reputation-writer interface for job outcomes.

src/verifiers/FundingSufficiencyVerifier.sol
ZK-proof verifier: "agent wallet has at least X without revealing balance."

src/verifiers/IdentityProofVerifier.sol
ZK-proof verifier for identity attestation without doxxing the agent.

src/verifiers/MandateComplianceVerifier.sol
ZK-proof verifier proving a transaction complies with a mandate without leaking the mandate body.

Interaction

## How the contracts compose.

A job lifecycle touches identity, reputation, the job manager, and — when off-chain evidence
needs to be proven later — the ledger anchor.

[diagram]

flowchart LR
subgraph Identity[ERC-8004 Identity]
IR[SardisIdentityRegistry
ERC-721 agent token]
RR[SardisReputationRegistry
client feedback]
VR[SardisValidationRegistry
validator scores]
end
subgraph Commerce[ERC-8183 Commerce]
JM[SardisJobManager
escrow + state machine]
JR[SardisJobRegistry
on-chain reputation]
HK[Hooks
Reputation · Trust]
end
subgraph Refund[Dispute]
RP[RefundProtocol
Circle, Apache 2.0]
end
subgraph Evidence[Audit]
LA[SardisLedgerAnchor
Merkle roots]
end
IR --> RR
IR --> VR
IR -. agent id .-> JM
JM --> HK
HK --> JR
JM -. dispute .-> RP
OFF[sardis-ledger
off-chain batches] --> LA

[/diagram]

Build & test

## Toolchain.

What
Command
Notes

Compile
cd contracts && forge build
Foundry; Solidity 0.8.20–0.8.24.

Test
cd contracts && forge test
Fuzz + invariant tests for Job state machine + anchor.

Deps
lib/forge-std · lib/openzeppelin-contracts
Both vendored as git submodules under contracts/lib/.

Deploy script
scripts/deploy-mainnet-contracts.sh
Deploys SardisLedgerAnchor + RefundProtocol + ERC-8183 stack (4 contracts) with ERC-8183 caps pre-set.

Deploy target
Base mainnet (primary), Tempo mainnet
Needs deployer wallet with ~$2–5 ETH on Base, Alchemy API key, BaseScan API key.

External

## Audited contracts we rely on but don't deploy.

Contract
Address
Why we don't own it

Safe Smart Accounts v1.4.1
Canonical per chain (pre-deployed)
$100B+ TVL audit — wallet custody.

Zodiac Roles
0x9646fDAD06d3e24444381f44362a3B0eB343D337
Audited policy module; avoids Sardis-owned audit dependency.

Circle Paymaster
0x0578cFB241215b77442a541325d6A4E6dFE700Ec
Permissionless USDC-as-gas; no deployment needed.

Circle CCTP v2
Unified per chain (Circle)
Native cross-chain USDC burn/mint.

=====================================
# PAYMENT FLOW
https://canvas.sardis.sh/payment-flow
=====================================

Canvas 07 · Flows

## Payment Flow · end-to-end

One call — sardis.pay(to, amount, reason) — traverses fourteen distinct stages
before it returns. This canvas walks every one, with the actual file behind it, the latency
budget, and what happens when that stage fails closed.

TL;DR
Every payment is four things in sequence: identity (who is calling),
approval (is this allowed by policy + compliance + mandate),
execution (get an MPC signature, broadcast), receipt (ledger
row, Merkle batch, webhook). Fail-closed at every gate. Budget ≈ 2s p99 excluding chain
confirmation.

One request

## The full sequence — every actor, every message.

An agent running in LangChain calls sardis.pay(). The sequence below is what
really happens. Each bubble maps to a step in the walkthrough below.

[diagram]

sequenceDiagram
participant A as Agent (LangChain)
participant SDK as sardis-sdk-python
participant MW as FastAPI Middleware
participant RP as require_principal
participant O as PaymentOrchestrator
participant PP as PreExecutionPipeline
participant PV as MandateVerifier
participant CE as ChainExecutor
participant WM as WalletManager
participant TK as Turnkey MPC
participant RPC as Alchemy RPC
participant CH as Base / Tempo
participant LG as sardis-ledger
participant AN as LedgerAnchor.sol
participant OB as webhook outbox

A->>SDK: client.payments.create(to, amount, reason)
SDK->>MW: POST /v1/payments Authorization: Bearer sk_…
MW->>MW: CORS · HSTS · CSP · rate limit (Redis)
MW->>RP: extract principal (API key → agent/org)
RP->>O: orchestrator.execute(intent)
O->>PP: pipeline.run(intent, principal)
PP->>PP: spending_policy ✓
PP->>PP: KYC / AML (sardis-compliance) ✓
PP->>PP: mandate delegation ✓
PP->>PP: rate limit ✓
PP->>PP: anomaly score ✓
PP->>PV: AP2 / TAP / x402 chain ✓
PV-->>PP: verified
PP-->>O: ApprovedIntent
O->>CE: execute(approved_intent)
CE->>CE: choose chain + token + route
CE->>WM: request signature (payload)
WM->>TK: signPayload(org, sub-org, key)
TK-->>WM: signed tx
WM-->>CE: signed tx
CE->>RPC: eth_sendRawTransaction
RPC->>CH: broadcast
CH-->>RPC: tx hash
RPC-->>CE: receipt
CE-->>O: PaymentResult
O->>LG: append ledger_entry + policy_decision
LG->>AN: Merkle batch → anchor (async)
O->>OB: enqueue webhook (outbox)
O-->>MW: 200 PaymentResult
MW-->>SDK: 200 JSON
SDK-->>A: { tx_hash, status, receipt }

[/diagram]

Stage 1 · Agent surface

## From Python line to HTTP request.

01

Agent calls sardis.pay() or a framework tool.

In LangChain the agent invokes a PayTool from
sardis-langchain/toolkit.py. In the bare Python SDK the call is
client.payments.create(to, amount, reason). In MCP it's a
sardis_pay tool call from Claude Desktop / Cursor. All paths converge on the
same REST call — no framework gets a privileged fast path.

file: packages/sardis-sdk-python/src/sardis_sdk/client.py
file: packages/sardis-langchain/src/sardis_langchain/tools.py
fail: raised on client; no network call yet

02

SDK marshals request, attaches API key.

Client reads SARDIS_API_KEY, serializes the intent to JSON, sets
Authorization: Bearer sk_live_… and an idempotency key. TypeScript SDK does
the same via @sardis/sdk with Zod validation.

auth: API key HMAC-hashed at rest (SHA-256)
idempotency: client-generated uuid, enforced DB-side
fail: network retry with same idempotency key — safe

Stage 2 · API gateway

## FastAPI receives the request.

03

Middleware stack runs — CORS, CSP, HSTS, rate limit.

Request lands on Cloud Run, enters FastAPI composition root
(sardis-api/main.py). Middleware applies canonical prod origins, CSP/HSTS,
and the Redis-backed rate limiter. Rate limiter is fail-closed in prod —
if Redis is unreachable the request is denied.

file: packages/sardis-api/src/sardis_api/main.py
env: SARDIS_REDIS_URL required
fail: 429 on limit; 503 if Redis down

04

require_principal extracts identity.

Dependency resolver hashes the incoming API key, looks up the principal (org + agent +
scopes) from Postgres, attaches it to the request. Better-auth JWTs (EdDSA via JWKS)
take the same slot for dashboard-originated calls. Missing/invalid key → 401, no
downstream work.

file: packages/sardis-api/src/sardis_api/routers/pay.py
principal: org_id, agent_id, scopes[], key_id
fail: 401 — no orchestrator invocation

Stage 3 · The single gate

## PaymentOrchestrator + PreExecutionPipeline.

Every path (wallets, mandates, ap2, pay) funnels through these two files. This is the
"policy before execution" invariant at the code level.

05

PaymentOrchestrator.execute() enters.

The orchestrator receives the intent + principal. Builds a PipelineContext
containing chain, token, mandate refs (if any), agent card, trust graph snapshot. Calls
the pipeline.

file: packages/sardis-core/src/sardis_v2_core/orchestrator.py
invariant: chain_executor is private — only orchestrator touches it

06

Spending policy check.

Evaluates natural-language-derived rules against the intent — merchant allowlist, daily
cap, per-merchant cap, category block. Returns allow / deny / require_approval. Denial
ends the flow here with a reason code that's written to policy_decisions.

file: packages/sardis-core/src/sardis_v2_core/spending_policy.py
fail-closed: missing policy → deny

07

KYC / AML screen.

Hits sardis-compliance which calls Didit (KYC status cached) + Elliptic
(AML/sanctions). Missing or expired KYC for the principal → deny. Sanctioned
counterparty → deny + SAR queue.

file: packages/sardis-compliance/src/sardis_compliance/kyc.py
file: sardis_compliance/pep.py, sar.py
fail-closed: compliance provider down → deny

08

Mandate delegation check.

If the intent references a spending mandate, verifies the delegator's authority is still
valid, remaining budget covers the amount, and the mandate hasn't been revoked.
Decrements budget atomically via DB row lock.

file: packages/sardis-api/src/sardis_api/routers/mandate_delegation.py
SQL: SELECT … FOR UPDATE on mandate row
fail-closed: race → NOWAIT lock, 409 to caller

09

Rate limiter + anomaly score.

Per-agent + per-org buckets in Redis. Anomaly model (sardis-guardrails) scores the
intent against learned baselines — amount z-score, velocity, new counterparty, unusual
time-of-day. Score above threshold routes to approval queue instead of denying.

file: packages/sardis-guardrails/src/sardis_guardrails/rate_limiter.py
file: sardis-core/src/sardis_v2_core/anomaly_detection.py
fail: over threshold → approval queue, not denial

Stage 4 · Protocol verification

## If the request came in under a standard, verify the chain.

10

AP2 / TAP / x402 / A2A mandate chain verification.

If the intent carries a protocol envelope, the matching verifier walks the mandate chain —
Ed25519 / ECDSA-P256 sigs on Intent, Cart, Payment mandates; TTL; replay cache lookup in
Redis + Postgres archive. An unverifiable chain ends the flow.

file: packages/sardis-protocol/src/sardis_protocol/ap2.py
file: sardis_protocol/x402_settlement.py, erc8122.py
replay: sardis_protocol/replay_cache_redis.py
fail-closed: invalid sig → deny; replay → 409

Stage 5 · Execution

## Chain selection, MPC signing, broadcast.

11

ChainExecutor picks chain + token + route.

Reads the request's target chain (default "base"), selects the token (USDC / EURC /
PYUSD), and picks a route — direct transfer, CCTP bridge, paymaster-sponsored. Tempo
has its own dedicated executor.

file: packages/sardis-chain/src/sardis_chain/smart_router.py
file: sardis-chain/src/sardis_v2_chain/executor.py
file: sardis_chain/tempo/executor.py

12

WalletManager requests an MPC signature.

sardis-wallet/manager.py hands the payload to the active custody provider
(Turnkey default). For Turnkey: organization + sub-org + private key reference. The
key never leaves the provider — Sardis receives a signed transaction, not a key.

file: packages/sardis-wallet/src/sardis_wallet/manager.py
file: sardis_wallet/tempo_keychain.py, circle_client.py
fail-closed: provider down → 503, no broadcast

13

Broadcast via Alchemy RPC.

Signed transaction submitted to SARDIS_BASE_RPC_URL (env-driven, Alchemy
primary, public RPC fallback). Tempo uses rpc.tempo.xyz. Executor watches
for the receipt with bounded retries.

provider: Alchemy (Base/Ethereum/Polygon/Arbitrum/Optimism)
provider: Tempo public RPC
fail: receipt timeout → mark pending, reconciler will finalize

Stage 6 · Receipt + audit

## Ledger row, Merkle anchor, webhook.

14

Ledger entry + policy decision rows written.

A single transaction writes ledger_entry (the movement) plus
policy_decision (why it was allowed) in Postgres. Both rows reference each
other. This is the evidence base for disputes and regulator queries.

file: packages/sardis-ledger/src/sardis_ledger/models.py
file: sardis_ledger/hybrid.py (ImmuDB optional)

15

Merkle batch → on-chain anchor.

A background job rolls recent ledger entries into a Merkle tree and anchors the root
via SardisLedgerAnchor.sol. After anchoring, any ledger row can be proven
to have existed at or before a given block.

file: packages/sardis-ledger/src/sardis_ledger/anchor.py
contract: contracts/src/SardisLedgerAnchor.sol
async: batched, ~5 min cadence in prod

16

Webhook enqueued via outbox; SDK returns receipt.

Response assembled (PaymentResult), returned up the stack. Webhook for
payment.succeeded / payment.denied enters the outbox table —
delivered at-least-once by the outbox worker with exponential backoff and event-id
dedup.

table: webhook_outbox
dedup: event_id uniqueness + delivery tracking
fail: delivery 5xx → retry with backoff, alert after N failures

Latency budget

## Where the 2-second p99 budget goes.

Indicative p99 figures, excluding chain confirmation. Agents wait for the HTTP response;
chain finality is tracked asynchronously and surfaced via webhook.

Stage
p50
p99
Notes

01–02 · SDK marshal
<1 ms
3 ms
Local JSON + HTTP; ignored at SLO level.

03 · Middleware + rate limit
5 ms
25 ms
Redis round-trip dominates.

04 · require_principal
8 ms
30 ms
Postgres principal lookup (cached after first hit).

06–09 · Pre-exec pipeline
40 ms
180 ms
Policy eval + compliance cache + anomaly score.

10 · Protocol verify (if AP2/TAP)
12 ms
60 ms
Signature verify + replay lookup.

11 · Chain selection
3 ms
10 ms
Pure Python; no I/O.

12 · MPC signature (Turnkey)
350 ms
900 ms
Largest single slice. Cold org-auth worse.

13 · Broadcast (eth_sendRaw)
120 ms
400 ms
Alchemy p99; confirmation is async.

14 · Ledger write
12 ms
40 ms
Single Postgres tx.

15 · Merkle anchor
async
async
Background job; not on the hot path.

16 · Webhook enqueue
3 ms
10 ms
Insert into outbox only.

Total (hot path)
~560 ms
~1.9 s
Within the 2s p99 SLO; MPC dominates.

Failure behaviour

## What happens when each stage fails.

Stage
Mode
Observable effect

Rate limit
fail-closed
429; if Redis unreachable in prod → 503 (no silent pass-through).

Principal lookup
fail-closed
401; no orchestrator entry.

Spending policy
fail-closed
403 with reason code; policy_decision row written.

Compliance (Didit/Elliptic)
fail-closed
403; override only via SARDIS_AGIT_FAIL_OPEN=true (dev only).

Mandate
fail-closed
409 on race (NOWAIT); 403 on insufficient budget.

Anomaly
fail-soft
Above threshold → approval queue, HTTP 202 pending_approval.

Protocol verify
fail-closed
403 invalid sig; 409 replay.

MPC provider
fail-closed
503; no broadcast, no ledger row.

Broadcast
fail-open to reconciler
Marks pending; reconciliation queue finalizes.

Ledger write
fail-closed
Whole request rolled back; response 500. Broadcast + ledger share a DB tx where possible.

Anchor
eventual
Async; delayed batches alerted via ops channel.

Webhook delivery
at-least-once
Outbox retries with backoff; 5xx after N attempts alerts operator.

Package ownership

## Which package owns each stage.

Agent surface
sardis-sdk-python · sardis-sdk-js · sardis-langchain (+ other adapters)

Gateway
sardis-api · middleware + routers

Orchestration
sardis-core · orchestrator.py + pre_execution_pipeline.py

Policy + compliance
sardis-core · spending_policy.py; sardis-compliance; sardis-guardrails

Protocol verification
sardis-protocol · ap2.py · x402_settlement.py · erc8122.py · replay_cache_redis.py

Chain execution
sardis-chain · executor.py · smart_router.py · tempo/executor.py

Wallet + MPC
sardis-wallet · manager.py · tempo_keychain.py · circle_client.py

Audit
sardis-ledger · anchor.py · immutable.py · hybrid.py · plus SardisLedgerAnchor.sol

Go deeper

## Follow-ups.

08 · Flows

Policy Engine

How natural language turns into the deny / allow / queue decisions in steps 06–09.

09 · Flows

Wallet + MPC

Step 12 expanded — Turnkey orgs, sub-orgs, key rotation, failover.

10 · Flows

Settlement & Chains

13 · Product

Protocol Gateway

Step 10 expanded — AP2, TAP, x402, A2A mandate chains.


=====================================
# POLICY ENGINE
https://canvas.sardis.sh/policy-engine
=====================================

Canvas 08 · Flows

## Policy Engine

The hard part of agent payments isn't access to money — it's controlling authority over money.
The policy engine is Sardis's answer: natural-language intent → structured rule → enforced on every
transaction, logged with a reason code, anchored on-chain. This canvas walks the whole pipeline.

TL;DR
SpendingPolicy.evaluate() in
packages/sardis-core/src/sardis_v2_core/spending_policy.py runs twelve checks in a fixed
order; the first failure short-circuits with a reason code. Above it sits a natural-language parser
(Groq Llama 3.3 70B, regex fallback). Below it sits an approval queue, a decision log, a simulation
sandbox, and — for mandates that clear the gate — on-chain enforcement via ERC-8183.

Thesis

## Why "authority" — not "access" — is the problem.

Giving an agent a wallet takes ten minutes. Giving an agent a wallet it cannot misuse
is the actual product. Every design choice in this engine follows from that.

Access is easy. Revocation is hard.

An MPC signer solves "the agent can pay." It does nothing about "the agent decided to pay
$40K to a phishing address at 3am because a jailbreak prompt told it to." Policy is the layer
that says no on the agent's behalf, before the signer is even asked.

Authority must be structured, not trusted.

Natural language ("$500/day on API credits, openai.com only") is the input, not the
enforcement. The parser converts intent into a typed SpendingPolicy dataclass with
hard caps, allowlists, time windows — data structures that can be evaluated in <1ms on every tx.

Fail-closed, with a reason.

Every denial returns a machine-readable reason code
(per_transaction_limit, merchant_denied, goal_drift_exceeded).
No silent rejects, no vague "declined." Agents, operators, and regulators see the same root cause.

Evidence beats logs.

Every allow and every deny writes a row to the decision log. Batches are Merkle-rooted and
anchored by SardisLedgerAnchor.sol. "Trust our logs" is not a defense; a Merkle
proof is.

Pipeline

## Natural language → structured policy.

Operators type English. Agents hit a typed dataclass. The parser bridges the two and is
implemented twice — once as an LLM call, once as a regex fallback — so the stack never hard-fails
on a missing API key.

Input (what the operator types)

Spend up to $500/day on API credits,
only to api.openai.com and api.anthropic.com.
Require human approval above $200.
Block gambling (MCC 7995) and adult (MCC 5967).

Output (what the engine sees)

SpendingPolicy(
agent_id = "agent_123",
trust_level = TrustLevel.MEDIUM,
limit_per_tx = Decimal("200"),
daily_limit = TimeWindowLimit(
limit = Decimal("500"),
window = "daily",
scope = SpendingScope.COMPUTE,
),
merchant_rules = [
MerchantRule(merchant_id="api.openai.com", allowed=True),
MerchantRule(merchant_id="api.anthropic.com", allowed=True),
],
blocked_merchant_categories = ["gambling", "adult"],
approval_threshold = Decimal("200"),
)

Primary: Groq Llama 3.3 70B

NLPolicyParser in nl_policy_parser.py. Uses
llama-3.3-70b-versatile via the Groq OpenAI-compatible endpoint
(https://api.groq.com/openai/v1). ~200ms typical; open-source model so there's no
proprietary lock-in on the parsing layer itself. Falls through to OpenAI if
GROQ_API_KEY is unset and OPENAI_API_KEY is present.

Fallback: regex parser

RegexPolicyParser in the same file. Pattern-matches common phrasings
("$X/day", "only to Y", "block Z") so policy creation still works in airgapped / sandbox
environments with no LLM key. Less expressive, but never hallucinates a field. Invariants live
in ImmutableParserHardLimits — hard ceilings the LLM can't raise.

Dimensions

## What a policy can actually control.

Each field on SpendingPolicy is a lever. Most deployments only touch three or four;
the rest exist for edge cases that show up at scale.

Dimension
Field on SpendingPolicy
What it controls

Per-transaction cap
limit_per_tx
Max single payment. Category-specific overrides via _get_effective_per_tx_limit().

Lifetime cap
limit_total + spent_total
Cumulative ceiling across the policy's lifetime.

Daily / weekly / monthly
daily_limit, weekly_limit, monthly_limit
TimeWindowLimit objects — rolling windows, race-safe reads from policy_store.

Merchant allow / block
merchant_rules
Per-merchant caps, category matching. Case-insensitive — deliberate, prevents casing-trick bypass.

MCC blocking
blocked_merchant_categories
4-digit Merchant Category Codes (e.g. 7995 gambling, 5967 adult).

Scope restriction
allowed_scopes
SpendingScope enum: retail, compute, agent_to_agent, etc. ALL is the wildcard.

Chain / token allowlist
allowed_chains, allowed_tokens
Restrict which rails a policy authorizes (e.g. "Base + USDC only").

Destination allowlist
allowed_destination_addresses / blocked_destination_addresses
Hard address pins — for treasury sweeps, known suppliers.

Approval threshold
approval_threshold
Above this amount, route to human queue instead of auto-approving.

Goal-drift guard
max_drift_score
If the agent has drifted from its stated task (goal_drift_detector.py), deny.

KYA attestation gate
(via kya_client arg)
Require on-chain agent identity attestation before high-value flows.

Merchant trust
(via merchant_trust_service)
First-seen or low-trust merchants get extra scrutiny regardless of other rules.

Pre-auth hold
require_preauth, max_hold_hours
Force a two-step "authorize then capture" instead of a single settlement.

Evaluation

## Twelve checks, fixed order, first fail wins.

The order in SpendingPolicy.evaluate() is not arbitrary — cheap local checks run
first, DB and on-chain lookups later. Short-circuiting saves latency on the common "just blatantly
over the limit" case.

Amount validation
amount > 0 and fee >= 0. Reason codes: amount_must_be_positive, fee_must_be_non_negative.

Scope allowed
scope ∈ allowed_scopes (or ALL). Reason: scope_not_allowed.

MCC not blocked
_check_mcc_policy(mcc_code) — 4-digit category screen.

Per-transaction limit
Effective cap = min(category override, trust-tier cap, global cap). Reason: per_transaction_limit.

Lifetime cap
From policy_store in prod, in-memory in dev. Reason: total_limit_exceeded.

Time-window limits
Daily / weekly / monthly rolling windows. Reasons: daily_limit_exceeded, weekly_limit_exceeded, monthly_limit_exceeded.

On-chain balance
If rpc_client provided — is the wallet actually fundable? Reason: insufficient_balance.

Merchant rules
Allowlist, blocklist, per-merchant caps. Reason: merchant_denied.

Goal drift
drift_score > max_drift_score → deny. Reason: goal_drift_exceeded.

Merchant trust
First-seen or low-trust merchants fail unless the policy explicitly allows unknown destinations.

Approval threshold
If amount ≥ approval_threshold: return (True, "requires_approval") and hand off to approval queue.

KYA attestation
For high-value paths, verify on-chain agent identity via kya_client. No attestation → deny.

Decision tree

## What evaluate() looks like as a flowchart.

[diagram]

flowchart TD
A[Payment request] --> B{amount > 0 & fee >= 0?}
B -- no --> D1[deny: amount_must_be_positive]
B -- yes --> C{scope allowed?}
C -- no --> D2[deny: scope_not_allowed]
C -- yes --> D{MCC blocked?}
D -- yes --> D3[deny: mcc_blocked]
D -- no --> E{per-tx cap OK?}
E -- no --> D4[deny: per_transaction_limit]
E -- yes --> F{lifetime cap OK?}
F -- no --> D5[deny: total_limit_exceeded]
F -- yes --> G{time-window OK?}
G -- no --> D6[deny: daily/weekly/monthly_limit_exceeded]
G -- yes --> H{balance sufficient?}
H -- no --> D7[deny: insufficient_balance]
H -- yes --> I{merchant allowed?}
I -- no --> D8[deny: merchant_denied]
I -- yes --> J{drift < max?}
J -- no --> D9[deny: goal_drift_exceeded]
J -- yes --> K{amount &ge; approval_threshold?}
K -- yes --> L[requires_approval → queue]
K -- no --> M{KYA attested?}
M -- no --> D10[deny: kya_required]
M -- yes --> N[ALLOW → orchestrator → signer]

[/diagram]

Reason codes

## Every denial is explainable.

These strings are the contract between engine, operator dashboard, and the decision log. Don't
invent new ones casually — they're indexed, alerted on, and quoted in incident reports.

Reason code
Meaning
Typical fix

OK
Allowed.
—

requires_approval
Allowed if an approver signs off.
Route to ApprovalService.

amount_must_be_positive
Bad input.
SDK-level validation should catch first.

fee_must_be_non_negative
Bad input.
Gas estimator bug — investigate.

scope_not_allowed
Spending category not in allowlist.
Expand allowed_scopes or re-route request.

mcc_blocked
Merchant category blocked (e.g. gambling).
Confirm with operator before unblocking.

per_transaction_limit
Single payment above cap.
Raise limit_per_tx or split payment.

total_limit_exceeded
Lifetime ceiling hit.
Top up limit_total or rotate policy.

daily_limit_exceeded
Daily window exhausted.
Wait for reset or raise window.

insufficient_balance
Wallet can't cover.
Fund wallet or route via CCTP.

merchant_denied
Merchant blocked or not on allowlist.
Review allowlist.

goal_drift_exceeded
Agent off-task per drift detector.
Investigate agent — potential jailbreak or goal-hijack.

kya_required
No on-chain agent attestation.
Run KYA onboarding for the agent.

On-chain enforcement

## Spending mandates — from a Python object to an EVM guard.

A policy is off-chain: Sardis trusts itself. A spending mandate is the same policy
cryptographically committed — first to Sardis's append-only log via AGIT, then to an on-chain
guard via ERC-8183. If Sardis is compromised, the chain still enforces the cap.

Off-chain: AGIT policy chain

AgitPolicyEngine in agit_policy_engine.py. Every
commit_policy() hashes the policy and links it to the prior commit, forming a
tamper-evident chain. verify_policy_chain(agent_id) proves the current policy
descends from the original — a policy flip doesn't go unnoticed.

On-chain: ERC-8183 guard

ERC8183PolicyGuard in erc8183_policy_guard.py. Before funding or
settlement, the guard calls evaluate_funding / submit_funding /
submit_settlement — the solidity-side hooks reject if the on-chain policy record
doesn't match. Caps currently enforced: 1% fee ceiling, USDC-only, trust hooks disabled
(post-audit relaxation path).

Why both?

Off-chain policy runs on every request — cheap, flexible, covers all rails including fiat.
On-chain policy is the last line when counterparty trust in Sardis drops to zero (regulator,
enterprise treasury, dispute). They agree in the common case; they diverge only when Sardis
itself is the attacker, and the on-chain side wins.

ERC-20 hook surface

The guard publishes three evaluation points on-chain:
evaluate_funding (pre-transfer sanity), submit_funding (commits
the intent to a job), submit_settlement (finalizes the settlement hash).
Each raises ERC8183PolicyDenied on mismatch.

Delegation

## Parent → child mandate trees.

Real agent systems are multi-agent. A team lead agent hires helpers; a workflow fans out to
sub-tasks. Each child needs spending authority — but strictly less than the parent's, never more.
That's what mandate_tree.py enforces.

MandateTreeNode

A node holds the mandate (limits, scopes, expiry) plus a list of children. is_leaf()
and total_delegated() let callers reason about the tree without walking it manually.

MandateTreeValidator.validate_delegation()

Invariants: child limit_per_tx ≤ parent's; child total_delegated ≤ parent.limit_total;
child expiry ≤ parent expiry; scope must be a subset. Violation → deny. This is the "can't
issue more authority than you have" rule, enforced structurally.

build_mandate_tree()

Reconstructs the full tree from the DB for audit and visualization. The dashboard's mandate
view reads from this; investigators reading a decision log can reconstruct the delegation
chain that authorized a specific payment.

Revocation cascades

Revoking a parent mandate invalidates every descendant at the next evaluation. No "zombie
sub-agents kept spending after the main agent was killed" story. This matters for incident
response.

Approvals

## When policy says "maybe."

A request above approval_threshold isn't denied — it's queued. A human operator,
a second agent, or a group vote can release it. ApprovalService in
approval_service.py owns the lifecycle.

Method
What it does

create_approval()
Opens an approval record tied to the pending mandate. Notifies via ApprovalNotifier (Slack / email / webhook).

approve(approval_id, approver_id)
Marks approved; orchestrator resumes execution on next poll or event.

deny(approval_id, reason)
Hard deny — decision log captures the human reason. The mandate is archived, not executable.

cancel(approval_id)
Withdrawn by the agent (e.g. order was rescinded upstream).

expire_pending()
Cron-called. Approvals have a TTL — stale requests don't execute days later when no one remembers.

list_pending()
Powers the operator dashboard's approval inbox.

Decision log

## Every allow, every deny, one row.

The policy decision log is separate from the payment ledger — it captures policy outcomes
whether or not a payment ever executed. A denied request still has to be evidenced: that's how
the agent owner proves to a regulator that the system was working, not failing.

Table
policy_decisions (see canvas 05 · Database).

Rows written when
Every call into SpendingPolicy.evaluate(), plus every approval transition.

Fields
agent_id, mandate_id, reason_code, amount, merchant, chain, token, decided_at, approver_id (if any), evidence_hash.

Anchoring
Batched into Merkle roots by sardis-ledger, anchored via SardisLedgerAnchor.sol.

Export
evidence_export router produces signed, proof-bearing CSV/JSON for audits.

Simulation

## Test a policy before it enforces anything.

Policy changes are risky: tighten too much, block legitimate spending; loosen too much, invite
incidents. The policy_simulation router
(packages/sardis-api/src/sardis_api/routers/policy_simulation.py) replays historical
traffic against a proposed policy and returns what would have changed — allows that became denies,
denies that became allows, new approval routes.

Replay

Takes a time range of real mandates, runs them through the draft policy without touching
wallets or chains. Output: diff of outcomes, broken down by reason code.

What-if

Operator uploads a synthetic batch ("ten $150 API spends, two $2K one-offs"). Engine returns
the full evaluation trail — same twelve checks, no side effects.

Incident response

## Kill switch — the big red button.

When something is clearly wrong ("every agent is suddenly trying to pay the same new address"),
the operator needs one action that stops everything. The emergency router is that action.

Router
packages/sardis-api/src/sardis_api/routers/emergency.py

Dependency
packages/sardis-api/src/sardis_api/kill_switch_dep.py — enforced on every write path.

Effect
Global flag flips; SpendingPolicy.evaluate() short-circuits to deny with reason kill_switch_active; no new mandates accept; in-flight signatures are aborted at the wallet manager.

Scope modes
Global, per-tenant, per-agent, per-chain — you don't always want to freeze everyone.

Audit
Activation, reason, operator identity, and deactivation all log to the decision stream.

Recovery
Manual — intentionally. Restoring spending after a kill requires explicit operator action, not a timeout.

Source of Truth

## Files to open when you want to dig in.

File
Role

packages/sardis-core/src/sardis_v2_core/spending_policy.py
The engine. SpendingPolicy, TimeWindowLimit, MerchantRule, evaluate().

packages/sardis-core/src/sardis_v2_core/nl_policy_parser.py
Natural-language → typed policy. Groq primary, OpenAI fallback, regex last resort.

packages/sardis-core/src/sardis_v2_core/agit_policy_engine.py
AGIT: commits, chain verification, policy history, diff across versions.

packages/sardis-core/src/sardis_v2_core/erc8183_policy_guard.py
On-chain guard — evaluate/submit funding + settlement.

packages/sardis-core/src/sardis_v2_core/approval_service.py
Approval lifecycle: create, approve, deny, expire.

packages/sardis-core/src/sardis_v2_core/mandate_tree.py
Parent/child mandate invariants.

packages/sardis-core/src/sardis_v2_core/goal_drift_detector.py
Drift scoring — input to the drift check.

packages/sardis-core/src/sardis_v2_core/kya_trust_scoring.py
Trust tiers + per-tier caps merged into per-tx limit.

packages/sardis-api/src/sardis_api/routers/policy_simulation.py
What-if / replay endpoints.

packages/sardis-api/src/sardis_api/routers/emergency.py
Kill-switch activation and status.

packages/sardis-api/src/sardis_api/kill_switch_dep.py
FastAPI dependency that enforces the flag on every write.

Go deeper

## Follow-ups.

07 · Flows

Payment Flow

Where policy evaluation sits in the full request path.

09 · Flows

Wallet + MPC

What happens after an approved policy hands off to the signer.

10 · Flows

Settlement & Chains

Which rails the policy is allowed to authorize.

19 · Ops

Security Model

Trust boundaries and threat model the policy engine defends.


=====================================
# WALLET MPC
https://canvas.sardis.sh/wallet-mpc
=====================================

Canvas 09 · Flows

## Wallet + MPC

Sardis never holds a private key. Every signature is produced by an external MPC provider —
Turnkey by default, Fireblocks / Circle / Lit as swap-ins — behind a single abstract port.
This canvas maps the interface, the providers, and the signing path from orchestrator to chain.

TL;DR
MPCSignerPort in packages/sardis-chain/src/sardis_chain/executor.py is the
contract. TurnkeyMPCSigner, FailoverMPCSigner, and
LocalAccountSigner implement it. Wallet lifecycle is owned by
WalletManager in sardis-wallet/manager.py. On EVM chains, wallets are
Safe Smart Accounts v1.4.1 (pre-deployed). On Tempo, signing is bespoke via
TempoKeychainProvider.

Thesis

## "Non-custodial" is not a slogan — it's a blast-radius argument.

Keys never live in Sardis memory

Not in env vars, not in Redis, not in a service's process memory. Every signature is a remote
call to a provider (Turnkey, Fireblocks, Circle, Lit) that holds the key material in HSMs or
threshold-split shards. Sardis sees request → signature, never the key.

Blast radius analysis

If the API server is compromised: attacker gets request metadata, not funds. If the database
is exfiltrated: wallet addresses leak, keys don't. If an agent is prompt-injected: the signer
still only acts on policy-approved intents. Three independent breaches are required to move
money, which is exactly the point.

Pluggable by contract, not by config

The MPCSignerPort interface has three methods. Any provider that implements them
is a drop-in. Turnkey was default; Fireblocks/Circle/Lit are integrated; the port makes
migration a config change, not a refactor. Lock-in on custody would be the worst kind of lock-in.

Failover is a first-class concern

FailoverMPCSigner wraps an ordered list of providers: primary fails → next.
Custody providers occasionally have regional outages; we don't want a Turnkey incident to be a
Sardis incident. The port makes this tractable.

Interface

## The signing port — three methods.

Any MPC provider that implements MPCSignerPort can drive Sardis. That's the whole
contract. Everything else — transaction envelopes, chain selection, ERC-4337 userOp hashing — is
handled by the executor before it hits the port.

class MPCSignerPort(ABC):
async def sign_transaction(
self, wallet_id: str, tx: TransactionRequest
) -> str: ... # returns signed raw tx hex

async def get_address(
self, wallet_id: str, chain: str
) -> str: ... # derives the address for a chain

async def sign_user_operation_hash(
self, wallet_id: str, user_op_hash: str
) -> str: ... # ERC-4337 userOp signature

All three calls happen over the network — they're async by design. The caller (ChainExecutor)
never assumes key locality.

Providers

## What each implementation actually is.

Provider
Implementation file
Key material
Typical latency
Notes

Turnkey default

packages/sardis-wallet/src/sardis_wallet/turnkey_client.py, TurnkeyMPCSigner in executor.py

HSM-backed, P-256 stamp auth

~200–400ms

Organization per tenant, sub-org per agent group. Ethereum + Solana signing. Primary production signer.

Fireblocks

packages/sardis-chain/src/sardis_chain/fireblocks_signer.py

Threshold MPC (Fireblocks SGX + network)

~600–1200ms

Enterprise-grade governance, policy engine on the provider side. Higher latency, richer workflows. Used where the customer mandates it.

Circle

packages/sardis-chain/src/sardis_chain/circle_signer.py + sardis-wallet/circle_client.py

Circle Developer-Controlled Wallets (MPC)

~400–800ms

Natural pairing for USDC-native flows. Wallet sets = per-tenant scoping.

Lit Protocol

packages/sardis-chain/src/sardis_chain/lit_signer.py

Decentralized threshold (Lit nodes)

~800–1500ms

Decentralized custody option — trust split across Lit's node set rather than a single provider. Useful for "no single custodian" narratives.

Simulated

SimulatedMPCSigner in executor.py

None — deterministic hex

<1ms

Dev/test only. Never accepted in a live chain executor — guarded by config.

Local

LocalAccountSigner in executor.py

Local secp256k1 key (env var)

<5ms

Dev / CI only. Exists because integration tests need a real signature. Not to be used in production — and the config validator refuses it when SARDIS_CHAIN_MODE=live.

Failover

FailoverMPCSigner in executor.py

Wraps others

Primary + fallback

Tries primary.sign_*(); on provider error, falls through to next. Emits observability event on every failover.

Turnkey

## The organization model.

Turnkey is multi-tenant by design. Sardis uses that structure as the isolation boundary:
one organization per customer / tenant, one sub-organization per agent group, one wallet per agent.
Permission grants cascade down; revocation cascades down; nothing cross-cuts.

Config
TurnkeyConfig in packages/sardis-core/src/sardis_v2_core/config.py (env vars: TURNKEY_API_KEY, TURNKEY_ORGANIZATION_ID).

Org per tenant
Root organization is Sardis. Each customer onboarded gets a sub-org with its own API user and quorum rules.

Sub-org per group
Agent groups (agent_groups.py) map to further sub-orgs — keys for one group can't sign for another.

Wallet per agent
Each Wallet row corresponds to a Turnkey wallet; wallet_id is the Turnkey wallet id.

Address derivation
Per-chain via TurnkeyMPCSigner.get_address(wallet_id, chain) — one seed → deterministic addresses for every supported chain.

Stamp auth
Every request is signed with a P-256 "API stamp" — no bearer tokens, no replay. Turnkey rejects stale requests at the edge.

Verification guard
settings.turnkey_configured in config.py must be true in live mode; otherwise chain executor refuses to start.

Lifecycle

## From empty slot to retired wallet.

Wallets are stateful: they're created, they sign, sometimes their keys rotate, sometimes they
get frozen, eventually some are archived. WalletManager in
sardis-wallet/manager.py owns the full lifecycle.

01

create

create_turnkey_wallet() / create_circle_wallet(). Row in wallets, wallet record at provider.

02

derive

derive_address(wallet_id, chain). Per-chain address cached in DB.

03

sign

First signing request after policy approval. activity_monitor logs it.

04

rotate

rotate_wallet_key() / emergency_key_rotation(). New key material, same wallet id.

05

freeze

is_frozen=TRUE (migration 006). Signing refused pre-flight.

06

recover

setup_social_recovery() / initiate_recovery() — guardian-quorum path.

07

archive

Wallet retired; history + keys preserved at provider for audit.

Signing flow

## Orchestrator → Wallet → Provider → Chain.

[diagram]

sequenceDiagram
participant O as PaymentOrchestrator
participant WM as WalletManager
participant Ex as ChainExecutor
participant P as MPCSignerPort
participant TK as Turnkey API
participant RPC as Alchemy RPC
participant C as Base / Tempo

O->>WM: request signature for approved intent
WM->>WM: load wallet row, is_frozen? kill-switch?
WM->>Ex: build TransactionRequest (nonce, gas, chain)
Ex->>P: sign_transaction(wallet_id, tx)
P->>TK: P-256-stamped request
TK-->>P: signed raw tx
P-->>Ex: 0x... signed tx
Ex->>RPC: broadcast_transaction(signed_tx)
RPC->>C: submit
C-->>RPC: tx hash + receipt
RPC-->>Ex: SubmittedTx
Ex-->>O: result, receipt hash

[/diagram]

Smart accounts

## Safe v1.4.1 on every EVM chain.

Agent wallets on EVM are Safe Smart Accounts, not plain EOAs. The singleton is
pre-deployed; Sardis deploys a CREATE2 proxy per wallet. This gets us ERC-4337 compatibility,
modular policy, and social recovery — without paying audit costs on a bespoke account contract.

Helper
packages/sardis-chain/src/sardis_chain/safe_account.py

Singleton
0x41675C099F32341bf84BFc5382aF534df5C7461a (Safe v1.4.1 canonical, pre-deployed on all major EVM chains)

4337 module
Safe4337Module — required for UserOp execution

Deployment
CREATE2 proxy via Safe's canonical ProxyFactory. Address is predictable before deployment via predict_safe_address() — we can share deposit addresses with users before any gas is spent.

Policy wiring
Zodiac Roles module (zodiac_roles.py) is attached via enableModule in setup — pre-audited, avoids a custom-module audit for MVP.

ERC-4337 stack
packages/sardis-chain/src/sardis_chain/erc4337/ — bundler_client.py, entrypoint.py, user_operation.py, paymaster_client.py.

Gas

## Paymasters — users don't pay ETH.

Asking an agent or merchant to "hold ETH for gas" is a UX dead end. Sardis uses a paymaster on
every supported EVM chain so gas is either paid in USDC (Circle) or sponsored (Pimlico).

Paymaster
Model
Client
When it's used

Circle Paymaster v0.8 primary

User pays gas in USDC. Permissionless, no API key.

CirclePaymasterClient in erc4337/paymaster_client.py

Default for agent + merchant userOps on Base, Ethereum, Polygon, Arbitrum, Optimism (mainnet + testnet).

Pimlico

Sardis sponsors gas (paid out of operating account).

PaymasterClient in the same file

Fallback when Circle is unavailable, and for flows where the platform picks up gas (onboarding, demos, first-transaction incentives).

Invariant
Gas-payment choice is visible in the SubmittedTx metadata and the payment ledger. No
silent sponsorship — operators can always tell who paid what.

Tempo

## Why Tempo needs its own keychain.

Tempo is EVM-compatible at the execution layer but has a different account/permission model —
the Keychain precompile at 0xAAAAAAAA00000000000000000000000000000000 with
per-Access-Key spending limits and a custom 86-byte signature format. That's not something a
generic ERC-4337 signer handles — it gets its own provider.

Provider
TempoKeychainProvider in packages/sardis-wallet/src/sardis_wallet/tempo_keychain.py

Executor
packages/sardis-chain/src/sardis_chain/tempo/executor.py (dedicated — not shared with EVM executor)

Account helper
packages/sardis-wallet/src/sardis_wallet/tempo_accounts.py

Signature format
0x03 || root_address (20 bytes) || inner_secp256k1_sig (65 bytes) — 86 bytes total. Built by build_keychain_signature().

Access keys
AccessKeyConfig maps Sardis policy directly to on-chain key parameters: token_limits, allowed_targets, max_calls_per_day, expires_at. A Sardis spending policy becomes an on-chain, Tempo-enforced access key.

Root custody
Root key stays in Turnkey. Access keys are short-lived agent keys provisioned per mandate.

Fee payment
packages/sardis-chain/src/sardis_chain/tempo/fee_payer.py — Tempo's native fee-payer abstraction, USDC/pathUSD gas.

Freeze

## The wallet-level kill switch.

Freeze is narrower than the global kill switch: it targets a single wallet, not the platform.
It's the right response when KYC changes, an incident is localized, or a mandate is revoked —
without disrupting the rest of the tenant.

Columns
wallets.is_frozen, frozen_at, frozen_by, freeze_reason — added in migration 006_wallet_freeze.sql.

Enforcement
WalletManager checks is_frozen before dispatching to the signer port. Signers never see the request.

Compliance hook
KYC denial, AML hit, or kill-switch activation can all flip the freeze flag. Every flip is logged with the reason.

Unfreeze
Explicit operator action, audit-logged. No auto-expiry — a freeze stays a freeze until someone decides otherwise.

Shared wallets

## Agent groups + multisig.

Not every wallet is for a single agent. Team wallets, treasury wallets, and high-value flows
need m-of-n approvals. Sardis supports that at the signer level, not just at the policy level.

Agent groups

packages/sardis-core/src/sardis_v2_core/agent_groups.py. A group has members,
group-level policies, and a shared wallet. Turnkey sub-orgs back the grouping so the custody
model agrees with the product model.

Multisig signer logic

packages/sardis-wallet/src/sardis_wallet/multisig.py. ApprovalThreshold,
MultisigSigner, TransactionApproval — collects the required quorum
before the MPC signer is asked to produce a final signature.

Social recovery

setup_social_recovery() / initiate_recovery() on
WalletManager. A guardian set can rotate keys if the primary signer is lost —
no "the founder died and the keys died with him" failure mode.

Session keys

session_manager.py. Short-lived signing sessions scoped to specific agent flows.
Analogous to Tempo Access Keys but at the platform layer for non-Tempo chains.

Source of Truth

## Files worth reading in order.

File
Role

packages/sardis-chain/src/sardis_chain/executor.py
MPCSignerPort, TurnkeyMPCSigner, FailoverMPCSigner, LocalAccountSigner, ChainExecutor, ChainRPCClient.

packages/sardis-wallet/src/sardis_wallet/manager.py
WalletManager / EnhancedWalletManager — create, rotate, freeze, recover, spend-limit wiring, session mgmt.

packages/sardis-wallet/src/sardis_wallet/turnkey_client.py
Raw Turnkey client (P-256 stamping, create_wallet, sign_transaction).

packages/sardis-wallet/src/sardis_wallet/circle_client.py
Circle Developer-Controlled Wallet client.

packages/sardis-chain/src/sardis_chain/fireblocks_signer.py
Fireblocks signer implementation of the port.

packages/sardis-chain/src/sardis_chain/lit_signer.py
Lit Protocol signer implementation.

packages/sardis-chain/src/sardis_chain/safe_account.py
Safe v1.4.1 helpers — setup calldata, CREATE2 prediction.

packages/sardis-chain/src/sardis_chain/erc4337/paymaster_client.py
Circle Paymaster + Pimlico clients.

packages/sardis-wallet/src/sardis_wallet/tempo_keychain.py
Tempo Access Key provisioning + keychain signature construction.

packages/sardis-chain/src/sardis_chain/tempo/executor.py
Tempo-specific executor (non-EVM flow).

packages/sardis-wallet/src/sardis_wallet/multisig.py
m-of-n approval logic for shared wallets.

packages/sardis-api/migrations/006_wallet_freeze.sql
Freeze columns + index.

Go deeper

## Follow-ups.

08 · Flows

Policy Engine

What the signer refuses to sign when policy says no.

10 · Flows

Settlement & Chains

Where signed transactions actually land.

06 · Backend

Smart Contracts

Safe modules, ERC-8183 guard, ledger anchor.

19 · Ops

=====================================
# SETTLEMENT
https://canvas.sardis.sh/settlement
=====================================

Canvas 10 · Flows

## Settlement & Chains

Once a transaction is policy-approved and signed, it has to actually move money — and money
moves on many different rails. Sardis supports six EVM chains, Tempo, Circle's Arc, and fiat
off-ramps through Stripe, Striga, and Bridge. This canvas walks the rail map, the cross-chain
path via CCTP, and the reconciliation machinery that keeps it all honest.

TL;DR
Chains are defined in ChainConfig
(packages/sardis-core/src/sardis_v2_core/config.py); token contracts live in
tokens.py. ChainExecutor in
packages/sardis-chain/src/sardis_chain/executor.py owns submission; CCTP v2 handles
cross-chain USDC; Stripe / Striga / Bridge handle fiat off-ramp. Settlement state is explicit
(migration 048), provider-rail is tracked (053), and a persistent reconciliation queue (057)
catches anything that falls off.

Rails

## Supported chains — the authoritative list.

The mainnets below are defined directly in ChainConfig. Any chain with a token
contract in tokens.py can be added to that list with a single entry — no executor
changes needed for EVM-family networks.

Chain
Chain ID
RPC default
Tokens
Status

Base
8453
https://mainnet.base.org (Alchemy in prod via SARDIS_BASE_RPC_URL)
USDC, EURC
mainnet primary

Tempo
4217
https://rpc.tempo.xyz
USDC (pathUSD), USDC.e, EURC
mainnet live since 2026-03-18

Ethereum
1
Alchemy
USDC, USDT, PYUSD, EURC
mainnet (token addresses configured)

Polygon
137
Alchemy
USDC, USDT, EURC
mainnet

Arbitrum
42161
Alchemy
USDC, USDT
mainnet

Optimism
10
Alchemy
USDC, USDT
mainnet

Solana
—
Solana RPC
USDC, USDT, PYUSD
tokens configured; separate executor under sardis_chain/solana/

Morph
—
Public RPC
USDC
token configured

Base Sepolia
84532
https://sepolia.base.org
USDC
testnet

Tempo Testnet (Moderato)
42429
https://rpc.testnet.tempo.xyz
USDC (pathUSD), EURC
testnet

Arc Testnet
~421614 (confirm w/ docs)
https://rpc.testnet.arc.network
USDC, EURC
testnet Circle USDC-native L1

Tokens

## The token registry.

TokenType enum + TOKEN_REGISTRY in
tokens.py. Each token carries decimals, issuer, peg metadata, and a
contract_addresses: dict[chain, address]. A token is considered "supported on a
chain" iff the chain key exists in that dict.

Token
Decimals
Issuer
Peg
Chains with contract configured

USDC
6
Circle
USD
base, ethereum, polygon, arbitrum, optimism, solana, base_sepolia, arc_testnet, tempo (pathUSD), tempo_testnet, morph

USDC.e (bridged, TIP-20)
6
Circle (bridged)
USD
tempo

USDT
6
Tether
USD
ethereum, polygon, arbitrum, optimism, solana

PYUSD
6
PayPal
USD
ethereum, solana

EURC
6
Circle
EUR (1.08)
base, ethereum, polygon, arc_testnet, tempo, tempo_testnet

TIP-20 note
On Tempo, bridged USDC uses a TIP-20 transferWithMemo ABI — see the
is_tip20 flag on TokenMetadata. The Tempo executor branches on this;
other chains use the standard ERC-20 ABI.

Base

## The primary production rail.

Why Base is default

Coinbase-native L2, Circle Paymaster v0.8 works permissionlessly with no API key, sub-cent
fees, mature bundler ecosystem (Pimlico + Alchemy), Safe v1.4.1 singleton pre-deployed.
For USDC-denominated agent flows, Base is the path of least resistance.

RPC strategy

Alchemy primary via SARDIS_BASE_RPC_URL, public mainnet.base.org
as fallback. ChainRPCClient in executor.py handles retries and
failover between endpoints.

Checkout default

SARDIS_CHECKOUT_CHAIN defaults to base. "Pay with Sardis" resolves
to a Base USDC transfer unless the merchant explicitly opts into another rail.

Smart contracts here

SardisLedgerAnchor.sol, ERC-8183 stack, reference Safe modules — all intended
first for Base mainnet (and mirrored to Tempo). See canvas 06 · Contracts.

Tempo

## Why Tempo got first-class status.

Tempo launched mainnet on 2026-03-18 alongside Stripe's MPP (Merchant Payment Protocol) — we
shipped Sardis live on it on day one. It's not just another EVM chain; it's payments-native.

Chain
chain_id 4217, RPC https://rpc.tempo.xyz

Architecture
100K+ TPS target; EVM-compatible execution; Keychain precompile for agent permissions; fee-payer abstraction for gas-in-USDC.

Ownership
Matt Huang (Paradigm) CEO, $500M Series A at $5B valuation.

Dedicated executor
packages/sardis-chain/src/sardis_chain/tempo/executor.py (not shared with EVM executor — TIP-20 ABI + keychain sigs + fee-payer flow).

Keychain
packages/sardis-wallet/src/sardis_wallet/tempo_keychain.py — maps Sardis policy to on-chain Access Keys. See canvas 09.

Fee payer
tempo/fee_payer.py — native gas-abstraction support, no separate paymaster.

Tokens
pathUSD (USDC) at 0x20c0000000000000000000000000000000000000; USDC.e (bridged) at 0x20C000000000000000000000b9537d11c60E8b50; EURC at 0x20c0000000000000000000001621e21f71cf12fb.

DEX integration
tempo/dex.py — native DEX for on-chain swaps when cross-currency flows stay on Tempo.

Cross-chain

## CCTP v2 — USDC across chains without bridges.

Circle Cross-Chain Transfer Protocol v2: burn USDC on source, mint USDC on destination. No
wrapped assets, no pooled liquidity, no bridge counterparty risk. Sardis uses it wherever a
payment starts on one chain and settles on another.

Implementation
packages/sardis-chain/src/sardis_chain/cctp.py — CCTPBridgeService, BridgeTransfer, BridgeStatus

Constants
cctp_constants.py — domain IDs, TokenMessenger / MessageTransmitter addresses per chain

Forwarding helper
cctp_forwarding.py — destination-chain forwarding so the minted USDC lands where the agent actually wants it, not at the messenger address

Unified addresses
CCTP v2's big win — same token messenger address on every chain, so integrations aren't n² chain pairs.

Typical use
Agent holds USDC on Base; merchant is on Polygon; CCTPBridgeService.bridge_usdc() burns on Base, mints on Polygon, forwards to merchant address in one flow.

[diagram]

sequenceDiagram
participant A as Agent (Base)
participant Ex as ChainExecutor
participant TMb as TokenMessenger (Base)
participant Attest as Circle Attestation
participant TMp as MessageTransmitter (Polygon)
participant M as Merchant (Polygon)

A->>Ex: bridge_usdc(amount, to=Polygon, recipient=M)
Ex->>TMb: depositForBurnWithCaller(amount, domain=polygon, ...)
TMb-->>Ex: burn event + message hash
Ex->>Attest: poll attestation for message
Attest-->>Ex: signed attestation
Ex->>TMp: receiveMessage(message, attestation)
TMp->>M: mint USDC → recipient
Ex-->>A: BridgeStatus.COMPLETED, dst tx hash

[/diagram]

Fiat off-ramps

## USDC → merchant bank, three providers.

Merchants eventually want fiat in their bank account. Sardis doesn't pick one provider — it
picks the cheapest path per currency and region. The selector lives in
CrossCurrencySettlementService.

Stripe Connect

US / USD · ACH

USDC → USD → merchant bank. Connected Account model; Sardis handles the conversion and Stripe handles the ACH settlement.

Striga

EU / EUR · SEPA + vIBANs

EURC → EUR → SEPA. Per-merchant vIBANs so inbound SEPA lands against a known merchant identity. Fee ~0.3%.

Bridge

Multi-rail · ACH / SEPA / PIX / Wire

Provider abstraction over bank-settlement rails worldwide. Fee ~0.5% on USDC → USD. Used when Stripe / Striga don't cover the geography.

## Path selection

CrossCurrencySettlementService.get_settlement_quotes() enumerates viable paths per
request, scores by total fee + estimated time, and returns quotes. select_optimal_path()
picks the best. The path identifiers are stable: usdc_bridge_usd,
usdc_grid_fx_eur, eurc_striga_eur, direct_stablecoin.

[diagram]

flowchart LR
P[Payment settles in USDC] --> Q{Merchant currency?}
Q -- USD (US) --> S1[Stripe Connect: USDC → USD → ACH]
Q -- EUR (EU) --> S2[Striga: EURC → EUR → SEPA]
Q -- EUR (fallback) --> S3[Bridge: USDC → Grid FX → EUR → SEPA]
Q -- BRL --> S4[Bridge: USDC → PIX]
Q -- USD (non-Stripe) --> S5[Bridge: USDC → ACH/Wire]
Q -- Stablecoin --> S6[Direct: stablecoin-to-stablecoin, no FX]

[/diagram]

FX

## Quotes and cross-currency settlement.

When source and destination currency differ, a quote has to be recorded: the rate, the fee
breakdown, the expected received amount. That quote is part of the payment's evidence record —
disputes reference it directly.

Service
packages/sardis-core/src/sardis_v2_core/cross_currency_settlement.py — CrossCurrencySettlementService

Quote type
SettlementQuote: path, source & target currency/amount, exchange_rate, total_fee_cents, fee_breakdown, estimated_time_seconds, fee_percent property

Result type
SettlementResult: settlement_id, path, amounts, status, provider_tx_ids[], metadata

FX helpers
fx_quote.py (quote construction), fx_signer.py in sardis-chain (chain-side FX signing), Grid FX + Circle Cross-Currency adapters (circle_cross_currency.py).

Fee defaults (bps)
Bridge 50, Grid FX 50, Striga swap 30. Configurable at service init.

Tracking

## Settlement is explicit, not best-effort.

"Payment succeeded" has two meanings: the chain accepted the tx, and the merchant's money
actually arrived. The settlement_records table captures the full lifecycle of the
second one.

Migration
packages/sardis-api/migrations/048_settlement_tracking.sql

Table
settlement_records

Key fields
settlement_id (unique), intent_id, receipt_id, mode, status, amount, currency, fee, network_reference, credential_id

Card-path fields
authorization_status, capture_status, dispute_status, reversal_reference, liability_party

Timeline
initiated_at → confirmed_at → settled_at (or failed_at). expected_settlement_at powers the "stuck settlements" alert.

Indexes
idx_stl_intent, idx_stl_status(status, mode), partial idx_stl_pending (initiated/pending_confirmation only), partial idx_stl_disputes.

Provider rail

## Which rail actually processed a payment.

The same logical payment can ride different provider rails depending on routing. Operators
need to see which one in every row — for reconciliation, cost analysis, and incident response.

Migration
packages/sardis-api/migrations/053_provider_rail_tracking.sql

Columns on transactions
provider_rail VARCHAR(50), provider_payment_reference VARCHAR(255)

Valid values
stripe_spt, visa_tap, mastercard_agent_pay, circle_cpn, bridge_ach, bridge_sepa, bridge_pix

Index
idx_transactions_provider(provider_rail, created_at) — powers the "costs by rail this month" dashboard query.

Why it matters
When a provider has an incident, operators need a one-query answer to "which payments are currently on that rail?" — without this column, you're grepping JSON metadata at 3am.

Reconciliation

## Persistent queue — because chains reorg and RPCs flake.

If the on-chain tx lands but the ledger append fails, we can't just forget about it. The
reconciliation queue holds the half-written payment until the ledger append succeeds.
Persistence means a process restart doesn't lose a cent.

Migration
packages/sardis-api/migrations/057_reconciliation_queue.sql

Table
reconciliation_queue

Schema
entry_type TEXT, payload_json JSONB, status (pending / processing / resolved / failed), retry_count, created_at, processed_at

Index
Partial idx_recon_queue_status WHERE status='pending' — the worker only ever scans pending rows.

Why it exists
Replaces a prior InMemoryReconciliationQueue that lost entries on restart. Any payment where ledger append fails after chain execution now persists its reconciliation state across process restarts.

Operator behavior
Alert fires when retry_count > N or status = 'failed'. Human-in-the-loop resolution.

Supporting services

## Executor-adjacent modules worth naming.

File
Role

sardis_chain/executor.py
ChainExecutor, ChainRPCClient, SubmittedTx, GasEstimate, GasPriceProtection, TransactionRequest.

sardis_chain/nonce_manager.py / redis_nonce_manager.py
Per-wallet nonce coordination — Redis-backed in prod to survive concurrent submissions.

sardis_chain/gas_optimizer.py
Gas pricing + spike protection (via GasPriceProtection).

sardis_chain/mev_protection.py
Private transaction submission where available — Flashbots-style for flows where MEV risk is real.

sardis_chain/confirmation.py
Receipt polling, reorg handling.

sardis_chain/permit2.py
Uniswap Permit2 for gasless approvals where relevant.

sardis_chain/cdp_swap.py
Coinbase CDP swap service integration.

sardis_chain/liquidity_router.py
Routing for cross-venue swap execution.

sardis_chain/simulation.py
Pre-flight tx simulation — catch reverts before broadcast.

sardis_chain/provider_tracker.py
Records provider rail choice → feeds the provider_rail column in migration 053.

sardis_chain/deposit_monitor.py
Watches deposit addresses for inbound USDC (checkout funding detection).

sardis_chain/bridges/ + bridge.py + bridge_xyz.py
Bridge provider adapters (Bridge.xyz etc.) alongside CCTP.

sardis-core/fiat_orchestrator.py
Orchestrates the USDC → fiat handoff (Stripe / Striga / Bridge path selection).

Go deeper

## Follow-ups.

09 · Flows

Wallet + MPC

What produces the signed tx that the executor broadcasts.

08 · Flows

Policy Engine

Chain/token allowlists that gate what rails a policy can use.

05 · Backend

Database Schema

Migrations 048, 053, 057 in context.

06 · Backend

Smart Contracts

Ledger anchor + ERC-8183 stack deployed to Base + Tempo.


=====================================
# CHECKOUT
https://canvas.sardis.sh/checkout
=====================================

Canvas 11 · Product

## Checkout & Merchants

Two products live here. Pay with Sardis is a crypto-native checkout where the
customer brings the wallet. Sardis Connect is the zero-crypto merchant SDK — the
merchant thinks in USD while Sardis handles stablecoins under the hood. Both ride the same
session, webhook, and settlement machinery.

TL;DR
A merchant creates a merchant_checkout_session. A customer
receives a client_secret, opens checkout.sardis.sh
in an iframe, connects a wallet (or fiat onramps into one), signs a USDC transfer, and Sardis
streams the outcome back. All public endpoints are client_secret-scoped
so sessions cannot be enumerated and double-pay races cannot land.

Products

## One session engine, two front doors.

Both products create rows in the same merchant_checkout_sessions table and emit
the same webhook envelope. The difference is who holds the wallet and where the crypto knobs live.

Pay with Sardis

## Crypto-native checkout — customer brings the wallet.

Drop-in checkout, similar in shape to Stripe Checkout but paying in USDC. Customer connects
MetaMask / Coinbase Wallet / any WalletConnect provider, or fiat-onramps into a fresh wallet
via Coinbase CDP. Settlement lands on the merchant's configured address (or connected merchant bank
account for USD payout).

- checkout.sardis.sh iframe · embed via sardis-checkout.js

- wagmi + viem + WalletConnect v2 + Coinbase Wallet SDK

- CDP Onramp integrated for fiat-first customers

- EIP-191 signature binds payer wallet to session

- SSE stream for real-time status in the merchant UI

Sardis Connect

## Zero-crypto merchant SDK — three lines of code.

For API providers who want to charge AI agents without ever touching a chain.
@sardis/connect wraps an Express/Next.js route, serves a
/.well-known/sardis.json manifest, and reports usage to Sardis. The merchant
sees a USD balance; Sardis handles wallet creation, settlement, and payout.

- packages/sardis-connect (Python) · packages/sardis-connect-js (TS)

- PricedEndpoint / UsageRecord / ServiceManifest models

- Protocol-agnostic: accepts: ["sardis", "x402", "mpp"]

- Per-call, per-unit, and subscription pricing tiers

- Payout via connected merchant bank · no wallet UX on the merchant side

Pay with Sardis · End-to-end

## Seven steps from merchant intent to settled USDC.

Every step corresponds to a real endpoint in
packages/sardis-api/src/sardis_api/routers/merchant_checkout.py. Public endpoints
live under the /sessions/client/{client_secret}/... namespace; merchant endpoints
require a principal.

01

Merchant creates session.
POST /v1/merchant/checkout/sessions (authenticated with merchant principal) →
inserts a row into merchant_checkout_sessions with amount, currency, chain,
settlement target, optional idempotency_key and embed_origin.
Server generates a client_secret (24 random bytes, base64).

02

Redirect or embed.
Merchant either redirects the customer to checkout.sardis.sh/s/{client_secret}
or calls SardisCheckout.open({ clientSecret }) from the JS embed SDK. The SDK
appends ?embed_origin=... so the iframe can restrict postMessage.

03

Checkout UI loads session.
The Vite/React app at packages/sardis-checkout-ui fetches
GET /sessions/client/{client_secret}/details — returns the amount, merchant name,
and the settlement_address needed for client-side USDC transfers.

04

Wallet connect.
Customer connects via wagmi. Two paths converge here:
external wallet (POST /connect-external, EIP-191 signature verified server-side)
or CDP Onramp (GET /onramp-token, fiat → USDC in a fresh wallet). Either way,
payer_wallet_address is persisted on the session.

05

Payment.
Customer signs a USDC transfer to settlement_address. Frontend
submits via viem; server records the tx hash via
POST /confirm-external-payment. Session flips pending → confirmed.

06

Streaming update.
Merchant dashboard (and the iframe itself) subscribes to
GET /sessions/client/{client_secret}/stream (SSE). Polling fallback at
.../details exists for clients that can't hold an SSE connection.

07

Settlement + webhook.
Background job reconciles the on-chain receipt, computes
platform_fee_amount and net_amount, flips status to
settled, and enqueues a checkout.session.settled webhook. Delivery
is tracked in merchant_webhook_deliveries keyed by event_id for
dedup.

State machine

## Merchant checkout session lifecycle.

The session row moves through a narrow set of states. Every transition is audited; every
terminal state triggers a webhook.

[diagram]

stateDiagram-v2
[*] --> created : POST /merchant/checkout/sessions
created --> pending : client loads /s/{client_secret}
pending --> expired : TTL elapsed
pending --> connected : connect-external (EIP-191 verified)
pending --> connected : onramp-token (CDP, payer wallet bound)
connected --> confirmed : confirm-external-payment (tx hash)
confirmed --> settled : reconciliation job · receipt verified
settled --> paid_out : connected merchant bank transfer (if USD payout)
pending --> failed : signature / balance / policy rejection
confirmed --> failed : tx reverted on-chain
settled --> [*]
paid_out --> [*]
expired --> [*]
failed --> [*]

[/diagram]

Hardening

## Why public checkout is boring in a good way.

packages/sardis-api/migrations/031_checkout_hardening.sql is the migration that
turned Pay with Sardis from "it works on the happy path" into something safe to point at the
open internet. Polar-inspired; every column earns its keep.

Control
Column / Table
What it prevents

Client secret

merchant_checkout_sessions.client_secret (UNIQUE)

Session ID enumeration. Public endpoints use /sessions/client/{client_secret}/..., never the internal UUID.

Idempotency key

merchant_checkout_sessions.idempotency_key (UNIQUE, partial)

Duplicate session creation on merchant-side retry. DB-level uniqueness, not app-level.

Row lock on pay

SELECT ... FOR UPDATE NOWAIT in the pay path

Double-pay races when a customer clicks twice or two tabs submit simultaneously.

Embed origin

merchant_checkout_sessions.embed_origin

Clickjacking / hostile iframe. The checkout UI restricts postMessage to the declared origin.

Webhook dedup

merchant_webhook_deliveries.event_id (UNIQUE)

Double-processing on retries. Merchants can safely use at-least-once delivery.

Fee transparency

platform_fee_amount · net_amount

"Why did I get less than I charged?" support tickets. Full breakdown on every session.

Reusable links

merchant_checkout_links table

One-off sessions for static storefronts; merchant generates a slug, Sardis creates a fresh session per visitor.

Payer binding

payer_wallet_address (migration 033)

Wrong-wallet payment. The onramp token endpoint validates the wallet matches the session binding.

Embed SDK

## checkout.sardis.sh/sardis-checkout.js

One script tag, one call. Source:
packages/sardis-checkout-ui/src/embed/sardis-checkout.ts. No framework required;
renders an overlay + iframe and listens for postMessage success/cancel events.

Shape of the call

<script src="https://checkout.sardis.sh/sardis-checkout.js"></script>
<script>
SardisCheckout.open({
clientSecret: "abc123...",
onSuccess: ({ session_id, tx_hash }) => {...},
onCancel: () => {...},
});
</script>

sessionId is still accepted but deprecated — use clientSecret.
The SDK routes to /s/{clientSecret} when present, /{sessionId} for legacy.

What the SDK does for you

- Injects a full-screen overlay with z-index isolation.

- Appends ?embed_origin=<window.location.origin> so postMessage is origin-restricted.

- Handles overlay close on success / cancel / escape.

- Exposes <sardis-pay client-secret="..."> as a web component alternative.

- Zero runtime dependencies; ~4 KB gzipped.

Two ways to fund a payment

## External wallet · CDP Onramp.

Customers either already own crypto (external wallet path) or don't (CDP Onramp path).
Both converge at the same session state: a verified payer_wallet_address with
enough USDC to settle.

External wallet — EIP-191 binding

Frontend: wagmi + viem + WalletConnect v2 + Coinbase Wallet SDK.
Flow: GET /connect-params returns the challenge to sign, customer signs with
wallet, frontend posts signature to POST /connect-external. Server verifies
the EIP-191 signature recovers the claimed address and persists it as
payer_wallet_address. On pay, customer signs the actual USDC transfer;
frontend reports the hash via POST /confirm-external-payment.

CDP Onramp — fiat to USDC

For customers without crypto. Sardis mints an MPC wallet for the session, calls
POST api.developer.coinbase.com/onramp/v1/token with a CDP JWT, and returns a
hosted pay.coinbase.com/buy/select-asset URL bound to that wallet.
The /onramp-token endpoint validates the wallet address matches the
session — an attacker cannot redirect funds to a different wallet by tampering with the
query string.

Sardis Connect

## Three lines of middleware, zero crypto on the merchant side.

Sardis Connect is the opposite philosophy to Pay with Sardis. The merchant never sees a wallet,
never sees chain config, never handles stablecoin balances. They wrap their API with
sardisConnect({ apiKey }), declare pricing, and get USD in their connected merchant bank
account. Sardis absorbs the crypto layer entirely.

Merchant code — TypeScript

import { sardisConnect } from '@sardis/connect';
const sardis = sardisConnect({ apiKey: 'mch_live_xxx' });
app.use(sardis.middleware());

The middleware reads a priced endpoint manifest, rejects unpaid calls with
402 Payment Required (x402-compatible), and forwards paid calls to the merchant
handler. Protocol-agnostic: one middleware accepts Sardis native, x402, and MPP.

Service manifest

Served at /.well-known/sardis.json — agents discover pricing via
ServiceManifest:

- PricedEndpoint(path, method, price, pricing_model)

- pricing_model: per_call · per_unit · subscription

- UsageRecord for metered billing (report units after the call)

- Tiered pricing via PricingTier

Risk + merchant-side controls

## Trust scoring and the merchant kill switch.

Two pieces of machinery sit next to the session engine: per-merchant trust profiles (Sardis
decides how much to trust the merchant) and checkout controls (the merchant decides what
shape of payments to accept).

Merchant trust — migration 045

merchant_trust_profiles stores trust_level, a rolling
trust_score (default 0.3), transaction_count,
total_volume, and dispute_count. MerchantTrustService
adjusts approval thresholds: first-seen merchants face stricter KYC + lower velocity caps,
merchants with a dispute history get flagged for manual review. Verified merchants
(verified_at, verified_by) get the tightest loop.

Checkout controls router

packages/sardis-api/src/sardis_api/routers/checkout_controls.py exposes
merchant-side knobs: amount caps, country allowlists, velocity limits, required
customer-KYC tier. GET /config / PUT /config for configuration,
POST /incidents / GET /incidents for the merchant's audit trail of
auto-blocked or flagged sessions.

Endpoint surface

## What's actually wired up.

Endpoint
Auth
Purpose

POST /v1/merchant/checkout/sessions
Merchant principal
Create session (idempotent via idempotency_key).

GET /v1/merchant/checkout/sessions/{id}
Merchant principal
Inspect session from merchant side.
