=====================================
# EXTERNAL
https://canvas.sardis.sh/external
=====================================

Canvas 17 · Ops

## External Services

Sardis is not a vertical monolith. It is a control plane above a carefully chosen set of
third-party services — MPC custody, KYC, sanctions, card issuers, PSPs, ramps, RPC, rails,
observability. This canvas enumerates every one, where it's wired, and why it beat its
alternative.

Single source of truth
Every config class below lives in
packages/sardis-core/src/sardis_v2_core/config.py. Every env var uses a typed
prefix (e.g. TURNKEY_, SARDIS_STRIGA_) so a missing value fails
fast at boot, not at the first payment.

Group 01 · MPC / Signing

## Private keys never live in Sardis.

Every wallet signature routes through an MPC provider. Turnkey is the default;
Fireblocks, Circle's Programmable Wallets, and Lit Protocol are pluggable behind the
same interface. Canvas 09 covers the wallet lifecycle.

Service
Config class
Env prefix
Status
Why

Turnkey
TurnkeyConfig
TURNKEY_
Live (primary)
Sub-org per tenant, API-signed requests, deterministic HD addressing. Keys never leave Turnkey.

Fireblocks
pluggable
—
Interface-ready
Enterprise alternative; picked on a per-merchant basis when compliance requires SOC2 Type II from the custody provider.

Circle Programmable Wallets
pluggable
—
Interface-ready
Falls out naturally where Circle CPN / Gateway are already wired.

Lit Protocol
pluggable
—
Research
Threshold MPC with policy at the key — future path for "self-custodial agent" narratives.

Group 02 · KYC / AML

## Compliance without a compliance team.

Canvas 12 has the full flow. Didit won the KYC slot on
cost + velocity; Elliptic remains the AML/sanctions screen.

Service
Purpose
Env prefix
Status
Why

Didit
KYC (identity verification)
DIDIT_
Live (primary)
500 free/month, $0.33/check after; YC W26; clean v3 API with x-api-key auth; webhook working end-to-end. Replaced iDenfy (abandoned) and Persona (rejected).

Elliptic
AML + sanctions screening
ELLIPTIC_
Live
Chain-aware sanctions screening; covers OFAC + sectoral lists; embedded in the Pre-Execution Pipeline as a fail-closed check.

Persona
—
—
Rejected
Priced for enterprise; minimum commit too high for a single-founder startup.

iDenfy
—
—
Abandoned
Earlier integration; replaced by Didit after onboarding friction.

Group 03 · Card issuing

## Virtual cards + agent cards.

Multi-provider by design — CardStackConfig routes between Stripe, Lithic,
Rain, Bridge, and Striga. One interface, five issuers. Stripe is the launch default;
Lithic stays in sandbox until funding allows the $5M program commit.

Service
Config class
Env prefix
Status
Why

Stripe Issuing
StripeConfig
STRIPE_
Live (primary)
Accessible without $5M program commit; pairs with existing Stripe Connect + Treasury plumbing. stablecoin_issuing_enabled flag flips on the USDC-settled variant.

Lithic
LithicConfig
LITHIC_
Sandbox
Deep controls (ASA webhooks, on-card policies). Held back until funding qualifies for the program.

Rain
RainConfig
RAIN_
Interface-ready
Stablecoin-native card program; drops in via CardStackConfig with zero core changes.

Bridge Cards
BridgeCardsConfig
BRIDGE_
Interface-ready
Multi-rail (ACH/SEPA/PIX/wire) — issuer + funding in one integration.

Striga Cards
StrigaConfig
SARDIS_STRIGA_
Interface-ready
EEA issuance with a clean SEPA/vIBAN story; complements Bridge outside the US.

Coinbase CDP (on-chain provider)
CoinbaseConfig
COINBASE_
Live
On-chain provider slot — wallets as cards when an agent wants to transact purely on-chain.

Card stack router
CardStackConfig
SARDIS_CARDS_
Live
Picks primary / fallback / on-chain provider, with per-org overrides via JSON.

Group 04 · PSPs & Payment Rails

## Money movement beyond crypto.

Service
Config class
Env prefix
Status
Why

Stripe Connect
StripeConfig
STRIPE_
Live
Merchant payouts (fiat off-ramp, US-first). connected_account_map_json maps tenants to Stripe accounts.

Stripe Treasury
StripeConfig
STRIPE_
Live
Financial-account-backed balance behind Issuing.

Stripe MPP (Tempo)
StripeConfig
STRIPE_
Live
Early access since 2026-03-18 launch. Sardis is first-class on Tempo mainnet.

Stripe Billing
StripeBillingConfig
STRIPE_BILLING_
Ready
Subscription billing path for the operator-facing product (post Atlas entity).

Lightspark (Grid API)
LightsparkConfig
SARDIS_LIGHTSPARK_
Sandbox
Lightning + UMA (uma_domain=sardis.sh) + FX + payouts. Gated by enabled flag.

Bridge (multi-rail)
BridgeCardsConfig
BRIDGE_
Interface-ready
One integration for ACH + SEPA + PIX + wire; selects by default_rail + per-feature flags.

Striga (EEA banking)
StrigaConfig
SARDIS_STRIGA_
Sandbox
vIBANs, SEPA, Lightning, standing orders, KYC — EEA-native fiat rails.

Group 05 · On/Off Ramps & Fiat Rails

## USD ↔ USDC without writing a bank.

Service
Config class
Env prefix
Status
Why

Coinbase CDP Onramp
CoinbaseConfig
COINBASE_
Live
Hosted fiat → USDC onramp, zero infra cost. topup_api_key drives the top-up variant.

Bridge Offramp
BridgeCardsConfig
BRIDGE_
Interface-ready
USDC → fiat via ACH/SEPA/PIX.

Circle CPN
CircleCPNConfig
CIRCLE_CPN_
Live
Circle Payments Network — funding / payouts; strategy=fiat_first + primary_adapter=circle_cpn by default in FundingRoutingConfig.

Circle Gateway
CircleGatewayConfig
CIRCLE_GATEWAY_
Gated
x402 facilitator / nanopayments, enabled via x402_enabled.

Stripe (funding)
StripeConfig
STRIPE_
Live
Stripe-backed funding for Issuing top-ups.

Funding router
FundingRoutingConfig
—
Live
Strategy: fiat_first / stablecoin_first / hybrid. Primary + fallback adapters are explicit.

Group 06 · Chain RPC

## Broadcast + read.

RPC endpoints are env-var driven (SARDIS_BASE_RPC_URL, etc.) with public
RPC as a fallback. Canvas 10 covers multi-chain
settlement.

Service
Env var
Status
Why

Alchemy (Base)
SARDIS_BASE_RPC_URL
Live (primary)
Fast, reliable, free-tier-generous; first choice for Base 8453.

Alchemy (Ethereum/Polygon/Arbitrum/Optimism)
SARDIS_<chain>_RPC_URL
Live
Uniform provider across EVM chains.

Tempo RPC
SARDIS_TEMPO_RPC_URL (default rpc.tempo.xyz)
Live (mainnet 4217)
First-party Tempo RPC; dedicated executor in sardis-chain/src/sardis_chain/tempo/executor.py.

Public RPCs
fallback list
Fallback only
Used if primary RPC fails; never primary in prod.

Group 07 · Protocol Rails

## Card network + credential standards.

Service
Config class
Env prefix
Status
Why

Visa TAP
VisaTAPConfig
VISA_TAP_
Sandbox
Token Authentication Protocol — mTLS + TRID, signed tokens for agent identity at card-network level.

Mastercard Agent Pay / MDES
MastercardConfig
MASTERCARD_
Sandbox
P12/PKCS12 certificate auth; MDES token provisioning for agent-initiated card transactions.

Delegated Credentials
DelegatedCredentialConfig
—
Interface-ready
Framework for protocol-layer delegation (beyond AP2 / A2A).

x402
X402Config
X402_
Gated
enabled / server_enabled / facilitator_enabled toggles; networks base,polygon,ethereum; optional Permit2.

ERC-8183
ERC8183Config
—
Live
On-chain agent-payment standard; 1% fee cap, USDC-only MVP; canvas 06.

Group 08 · Database & Cache

## Stateful backbone.

Service
Env var
Status
Why

Neon Postgres
DATABASE_URL
Live
Serverless Postgres; 107 migrations applied in order; branching for preview envs.

Upstash Redis
SARDIS_REDIS_URL
Live
Rate-limiter (fail-closed if unreachable in prod), dedup store, replay cache, SSE fanout. Mandatory outside dev.

Group 09 · Observability

## Knowing when something's wrong.

Service
Where it lives
Status
Why

PostHog
apps/dashboard (posthog-js), VITE_POSTHOG_KEY
Live
Product analytics on the dashboard + landing. Funnels, retention, feature flags.

Sentry
@sentry/nextjs (dashboard) + Python Sentry in API
Live
Errors, performance, session replay. Integrated with Claude Code review via plugin.

Intercom
@intercom/messenger-js-sdk
Live
In-app support chat for operators and merchants.

Uptime (internal)
24 GitHub Actions workflows (uptime*.yml)
Live
Periodic health checks of API, landing, dashboard; metrics published as graph artefacts.

Group 10 · Comms & Auth

## Email + identity.

Service
Where it lives
Status
Why

Resend
API notifications + dashboard transactional email
Live
Transactional email (receipts, approvals, security alerts). Simple API, modern DX.

better-auth
apps/dashboard (v1.5.6) + plugins (passkey, api-key, agent-auth, stripe, polar-sh)
Live
EdDSA/JWKS sessions, passkeys, org roles, API-key issuance inside the dashboard.

Legacy FastAPI JWT (HS256)
packages/sardis-api
Live
Dual-path auth for SDK clients; bridged to better-auth via JWKS.

Stripe Atlas
Company entity
Live (since 2026-03-21)
Delaware C-corp "Sardis Labs, Inc." formed via Atlas; enables production Stripe + banking relationships.

Polar.sh
Billing (temp until Atlas Stripe clears)
Live
Bridge revenue path: Polar now, Stripe once Atlas entity is fully wired.

Turnstile + CaptchaFox
Dashboard sign-up / reset
Live
Bot-prevention on public auth endpoints.

Group 11 · Trust Graph

## FIDES — decentralized trust signalling.

Service
Config class
Env prefix
Status
Why

FIDES (Sardis protocol)
FidesConfig
SARDIS_FIDES_
Live
Agent + merchant trust graph consulted in the Pre-Execution Pipeline. Fail-closed if unreachable.

Shape

## How these services plug into the spine.

Every external dependency is behind an interface in sardis-core so
individual providers can be swapped without touching business logic — the Didit-over-Persona
swap happened with zero code churn outside the KYC adapter.

[diagram]

flowchart LR
API["Sardis API / Pre-Execution Pipeline"] --> KYC["Didit"]
API --> AML["Elliptic"]
API --> FID["FIDES trust graph"]
API --> ORCH["PaymentOrchestrator"]

ORCH --> MPC["Turnkey · Fireblocks · Circle · Lit"]
ORCH --> CHAIN["Alchemy / Tempo / public RPC"]

API --> FUND["Funding router"]
FUND --> CCPN["Circle CPN"]
FUND --> BRIDGE["Bridge (ACH/SEPA/PIX)"]
FUND --> STRIPE["Stripe Treasury + Connect"]
FUND --> ONRAMP["Coinbase CDP Onramp"]
FUND --> STRIGA["Striga (EEA)"]
FUND --> LS["Lightspark (Lightning / UMA)"]

API --> CARDS["CardStack router"]
CARDS --> STR["Stripe Issuing"]
CARDS --> LIT["Lithic"]
CARDS --> RAIN["Rain"]
CARDS --> BRC["Bridge Cards"]
CARDS --> STC["Striga Cards"]

API --> NET["Visa TAP · Mastercard MDES · x402 · ERC-8183"]
API --> DB["Neon Postgres"]
API --> REDIS["Upstash Redis"]

DASH["Dashboard · Next.js"] --> BA["better-auth (EdDSA/JWKS)"]
DASH --> POLAR["Polar.sh billing"]
DASH --> POSTHOG["PostHog"]
DASH --> SENTRY["Sentry"]
DASH --> INTERCOM["Intercom"]

[/diagram]

Snapshot

## Live vs. sandbox vs. interface-ready.

Status
Services

Live (production)
Turnkey, Didit, Elliptic, Stripe (Issuing + Treasury + Connect + MPP), Coinbase CDP (Onramp), Circle CPN, Alchemy (Base, Ethereum, Polygon, Arbitrum, Optimism), Tempo RPC, Neon, Upstash, better-auth, Polar.sh, PostHog, Sentry, Intercom, Resend, Turnstile, FIDES, ERC-8183.

Sandbox / gated
Lithic, Striga, Lightspark, Visa TAP, Mastercard MDES, x402, Circle Gateway, Bridge Cards.

Interface-ready
Fireblocks, Circle Programmable Wallets, Lit Protocol, Rain, Bridge Offramp.

Rejected / retired
Persona (too expensive), iDenfy (onboarding friction), Lithic production (needs $5M program commit).

Source of Truth

## Go read the code, not this table, before quoting.

Question
File

Complete list of external configs?
packages/sardis-core/src/sardis_v2_core/config.py (grep '^class.*Config')

Chain RPC URLs + chain IDs?
packages/sardis-core/src/sardis_v2_core/config.py → ChainConfig list

Token contracts per chain?
packages/sardis-core/src/sardis_v2_core/tokens.py

Card routing?
CardStackConfig in config.py

Funding routing?
FundingRoutingConfig in config.py

Deployed env vars?
Cloud Run (API) + Vercel (dashboard + landing + checkout) project settings — source of truth.

Go deeper

## Follow-ups.

12 · Product

Compliance Stack

Didit + Elliptic in the Pre-Execution Pipeline.

09 · Flows

Wallet + MPC

Turnkey sub-orgs + signing flow.

10 · Flows

Settlement & Chains

CCTP v2 + multi-chain + Tempo mainnet.

18 · Ops

CI/CD

Where these env vars get injected.


=====================================
# CICD
https://canvas.sardis.sh/cicd
=====================================

Canvas 18 · Ops

## CI / CD

Twenty-five GitHub Actions workflows guard the repo. Four classes: tests on every PR,
security scans on every push and weekly, deploys on merge to main, publishes on tag.
One branch is production (main). Everything else is preview.

TL;DR
main is the only long-lived branch. Every PR runs the full CI matrix (Python,
contracts, TS SDK, dashboard, landing) plus security. Merge to main triggers
path-scoped deploys: API → Cloud Run us-east1, Dashboard/Landing/Checkout → Vercel.
Releases to PyPI and npm fire on version tags. Uptime pings every 10 minutes.

Overview

## Branch strategy and gating.

The default branch is main. PRs against it run the CI and security suites but
do not publish. Merges to main trigger the deploy workflows that match changed
paths — only the pieces that actually changed redeploy.

What runs on a PR

ci.yml (Python lint+test, contracts strict + gas, TS SDK, dashboard build,
landing build), test-api.yml (sardis-api scoped tests), codeql.yml
(Python + JS/TS), secret-scan.yml (gitleaks), security-scan.yml
(pip-audit + Trivy + Bandit), fuzz.yml (atheris against spending_policy).
Path filters skip irrelevant workflows.

What runs on merge to main

deploy-api-cloudrun.yml, deploy-dashboard.yml,
deploy-landing.yml, deploy.yml — each gated by a
paths: filter so a docs-only merge doesn't redeploy the API. Verification step
(curl /health) runs after each Cloud Run deploy.

What runs on a version tag

release-python-sdk.yml (PyPI / TestPyPI — py-sdk-v*),
release-python-integrations.yml (batch publish for langchain/crewai/adk/agent-sdk/connect),
release-npm.yml (mcp-v*, sdk-js-v*, ai-sdk-v*,
connect-js-v*), publish.yml, sign-artifacts.yml.
Each verifies pyproject/package.json version matches the tag, runs tests, builds, publishes,
then smoke-installs from the registry.

What runs on a schedule

monitoring.yml every 10 min (synthetic /health probe +
Slack notify on failure); uptime*.yml (uptime,
uptime-response-time, uptime-graphs, uptime-static-site,
uptime-summary); nightly-sandbox-e2e.yml 04:20 UTC;
codeql.yml weekly Monday; scorecard.yml weekly Monday;
fuzz.yml weekly Saturday.

Pipeline

## PR → preview → production.

Simplified view. Not every workflow is shown — see the tables below for full coverage.

[diagram]

flowchart LR
Dev[Developer branch] --> PR[PR against main]
PR --> CI[ci.yml + test-api.yml]
PR --> Sec[codeql · secret-scan · security-scan · fuzz]
PR --> PrevVercel[Vercel preview URLs]
CI --> Gate{All green?}
Sec --> Gate
Gate -- no --> Dev
Gate -- yes --> Merge[Merge to main]
Merge --> PathFilter{paths: changed?}
PathFilter -- sardis-api --> Cloudbuild[Cloud Build]
Cloudbuild --> CloudRun[Cloud Run · us-east1]
CloudRun --> HealthCheck[curl /health]
PathFilter -- apps/dashboard --> VercelDash[Vercel · app.sardis.sh]
PathFilter -- apps/landing --> VercelLand[Vercel · sardis.sh]
Tag[Version tag py-sdk-v* / sdk-js-v* / ...] --> ReleaseMatrix[release-python-sdk · release-npm · release-python-integrations]
ReleaseMatrix --> Registry[PyPI · npm]
Registry --> Smoke[Smoke install from registry]

[/diagram]

CI

## Test workflows.

Workflow
Trigger
Jobs
What it proves

ci.yml

push main · PR

python-lint-test · idempotency-replay-e2e · contracts-gas · contracts-strict · typescript-sdk · dashboard-build · build-landing

ruff + pytest with --cov-fail-under=40 on core/api, forge gas ceiling and full strict test suite (solc 0.8.24 pinned), TS SDK + MCP build and tests, dashboard and landing typecheck + build.

test-api.yml

push main · PR (scoped to sardis-api/ and sardis-core/)

API Lint & Test

Faster feedback loop for API changes: ruff + pytest on api/core with SARDIS_ENVIRONMENT=dev.

ci-integrations.yml

push main · PR

per-framework integration builds

Validates that every framework SDK in packages/sardis-* still builds and imports.

idempotency-replay gate (in ci.yml)

PR

E2E script scripts/release/idempotency_replay_e2e_check.sh

Confirms the Phase-3 dedup store and replay cache still refuse to double-execute a payment.

Security

## Scans and attestations.

Every workflow pins actions by commit SHA, not tag. Default permissions are empty
(permissions: {}) and each job declares the minimum it needs.

Workflow
Trigger
What it does

codeql.yml
push · PR · weekly Mon 05:30 UTC
CodeQL on Python and JavaScript/TypeScript. Uploads SARIF to GitHub Security.

secret-scan.yml
push · PR
gitleaks full-history scan (fetch-depth: 0). Fails the run on any detection.

security-scan.yml
push · PR · weekly Mon 06:00 UTC
Three jobs: pip-audit (Python deps), Trivy container scan of the built API image (fails on CRITICAL/HIGH, ignore-unfixed), Bandit SAST on sardis/ + packages/sardis-api/src + packages/sardis-core/src.

scorecard.yml
push · weekly Mon 06:00 UTC
OpenSSF Scorecard → SARIF → uploaded to GitHub Security. Tracks branch protection, token permissions, pinned deps, SAST, signed releases.

sign-artifacts.yml
release tag
Produces signed build provenance for published artifacts. Complements npm --provenance in release-npm.yml.

fuzz.yml
push · PR · weekly Sat 08:00 UTC
atheris fuzzer against tests/fuzz/fuzz_spending_policy.py, capped at FUZZING_DURATION=60s for PRs.

Deploy

## Production deploys.

Workflow
Target
Path filter
Notes

deploy-api-cloudrun.yml

Cloud Run · us-east1 · service sardis-api

packages/sardis-api/**, sardis-core/**, sardis-chain/**

GCP OIDC via Workload Identity Federation (no long-lived keys). Build through cloudbuild.yaml, deploy, then curl /health — non-200 fails the run. If GCP_WORKLOAD_IDENTITY_PROVIDER is missing, the job fails loudly with remediation instructions (previously silently skipped).

deploy-dashboard.yml

Vercel · app.sardis.sh

apps/dashboard/**, packages/ui-web/**, pnpm-lock.yaml

Build job (typecheck + lint + build) on every PR for preview. Deploy job is if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && github.event_name == 'push' — prod only.

deploy-landing.yml

Vercel · sardis.sh

apps/landing/**, packages/ui-web/**

Same two-job shape as dashboard.

deploy.yml

(legacy orchestrator / retained)

—

Broad-spectrum deploy workflow kept for manual workflow_dispatch redeploys.

Environments

## Where each surface lives.

Surface
Provider
Project / service
Host

API (sardis-api)
Cloud Run
service sardis-api · region us-east1
api.sardis.sh (via LB) · direct Cloud Run URL for health checks

Dashboard
Vercel
VERCEL_PROJECT_ID_DASHBOARD
app.sardis.sh + dashboard.sardis.sh

Landing
Vercel
VERCEL_PROJECT_ID_LANDING
sardis.sh

Checkout UI
Vercel
sardis-checkout-ui
checkout.sardis.sh

Docs
Vercel / Pages
deployed via docs.yml
sardis.sh/docs

Release

## Publish to registries.

Workflow
Tag pattern
Registry
Guardrails

release-python-sdk.yml

py-sdk-v*

PyPI (or TestPyPI for a/b/rc)

Verifies pyproject.toml + __init__.py versions match the tag. Runs pytest, python -m build, twine check, upload, then smoke-installs from the registry with 6-retry backoff.

release-python-integrations.yml

py-langchain-v*, py-crewai-v*, py-adk-v*, py-agent-sdk-v*, py-connect-v*

PyPI / TestPyPI

Same shape as sardis-sdk release: version consistency check, build, twine, upload, smoke install + import check.

release-npm.yml

mcp-v*, sdk-js-v*, ai-sdk-v*, connect-js-v*

npm (--access public --provenance)

Verifies package.json version, runs pnpm tests + typecheck + build, npm pack --dry-run, asserts built artifacts exist (dist/index.js / dist/cli.js), publishes with next or latest dist-tag, smoke installs via npx or dynamic import().

publish.yml
manual + tags
npm / PyPI
Umbrella publish orchestrator.

release-dry-run.yml
PR · workflow_dispatch
none
Dry-run of the whole release pipeline — builds, twine check, npm pack --dry-run — without publishing.

sign-artifacts.yml
release tag
—
Signs built artifacts; npm provenance is already set on release-npm.yml.

Monitoring

## Synthetic checks from CI.

GitHub Actions is cheap enough that we use it as a second uptime system alongside the
application's own health endpoints. Failures Slack us.

Workflow
Schedule
What it checks

monitoring.yml
every 10 min
GET /api/v2/health — asserts 200 + payload status in {ok, healthy, partial}, warns if response time > 2.0s, Slack-webhook notify on failure.

uptime.yml
scheduled
Base uptime probe aggregated into the public status page.

uptime-response-time.yml
scheduled
Records response-time histograms.

uptime-graphs.yml
scheduled
Generates historical graphs rendered on the status site.

uptime-static-site.yml
scheduled
Builds and publishes the static status site.

uptime-summary.yml
scheduled
Rolls checks into the summary feed.

Nightly + Docs

## Long-running and documentation pipelines.

nightly-sandbox-e2e.yml

Runs 04:20 UTC. bash scripts/ci/run_nightly_sandbox_smoke.sh against
SARDIS_SANDBOX_BASE_URL. Gated live E2E behind
RUN_LIVE_SANDBOX_TESTS=1 (set via workflow_dispatch input) —
when enabled, exercises Stripe, Lithic, and Coinbase CDP with real sandbox credentials.
45-minute timeout.

docs.yml

Builds and deploys the documentation site on changes to docs/. Mirrors the
Vercel shape of dashboard/landing: build on PR for preview, deploy on merge to main.

Gotchas

## Deploy lessons paid for in incidents.

Things that silently failed at least once and now have permanent fixes in the deploy
scripts. Worth knowing before you touch Cloud Run config.

Gunicorn 25.x — use call syntax, not --factory

Launch the API as "sardis_api.main:create_app()". The --factory
flag was removed in gunicorn 25.x; using it produces a cryptic boot failure that looks
like a module-not-found error. Both Docker CMD and
scripts/deploy-cloudrun.sh encode the call form.

Install gunicorn + uvicorn inside .venv

The container's system Python shouldn't host gunicorn — uv installs into .venv,
so gunicorn must live there too. Otherwise Cloud Run boots with the wrong interpreter and
the app never binds its port, tripping the startup probe.

Always gcloud ... --update-env-vars, never --set-env-vars

--set-env-vars silently wipes every other env var on the service. Use
--update-env-vars to merge. This is encoded in
scripts/deploy-cloudrun.sh but matters every time someone pokes Cloud Run by hand.

Workload Identity Federation, not SA keys

deploy-api-cloudrun.yml authenticates through
google-github-actions/auth with
GCP_WORKLOAD_IDENTITY_PROVIDER + GCP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT. No
JSON key lives in GitHub secrets. If the WIF provider secret is missing, the job
fails loudly (older behaviour silently skipped — backend changes never shipped for weeks).

Go deeper

## Related canvases.

01 · System

Architecture Overview

The seven-layer stack these pipelines deploy.

19 · Ops

Security Model

Trust boundaries, fail-closed defaults, secret handling — all of which CI enforces.


=====================================
# SECURITY
https://canvas.sardis.sh/security
=====================================

Canvas 19 · Ops

## Security Model

Sardis handles money on behalf of AI agents that can be prompt-injected, API keys that can leak,
and merchants that may be malicious. The security model is a chain of small, auditable decisions
— every one of them fail-closed — anchored by an append-only ledger so nobody, including us, can
quietly edit history.

TL;DR
No private keys in Sardis memory. No silent fallbacks. Every payment traverses the
PreExecutionPipeline before a signature is requested. Every decision is written
to policy_decisions and anchored on-chain. One kill switch freezes every wallet in
under a second.

Trust Boundaries

## Who trusts whom, and for what.

A trust boundary is where a request crosses from a less-trusted to a more-trusted context —
or vice versa. Every boundary is also an audit point. This is the actual topology, not an
aspirational one.

[diagram]

flowchart TB
subgraph Untrusted[Untrusted zone]
Agent[AI Agent · LangChain / CrewAI / custom]
Merchant[Merchant checkout embed]
Public[Public internet]
end

subgraph Boundary1[Boundary 1 · API gateway]
ApiKey[API key + HMAC / JWT]
TAP[TAP verification]
CORS[CORS · CSP · HSTS]
RateLimit[Rate limiter · Redis]
end

subgraph SemiTrusted[Semi-trusted · Sardis core]
Orchestrator[PaymentOrchestrator]
Pipeline[PreExecutionPipeline]
Ledger[Ledger + policy_decisions]
end

subgraph Boundary2[Boundary 2 · MPC + chain]
Turnkey[Turnkey · sub-org per tenant]
Alchemy[Alchemy RPC]
Chain[Base / Tempo mainnet]
end

subgraph Boundary3[Boundary 3 · Compliance providers]
Didit[Didit KYC]
Elliptic[Elliptic AML]
Stripe[Stripe Connect / Issuing]
end

Agent --> ApiKey
Merchant --> CORS
Public --> TAP
ApiKey --> RateLimit
CORS --> RateLimit
TAP --> RateLimit
RateLimit --> Orchestrator
Orchestrator --> Pipeline
Pipeline --> Ledger
Pipeline --> Didit
Pipeline --> Elliptic
Orchestrator --> Turnkey
Turnkey --> Alchemy
Alchemy --> Chain
Orchestrator --> Stripe

[/diagram]

Boundary detail

## What each boundary asserts.

Boundary
Direction
Enforced by
Failure mode

Agent → API
untrusted → semi
API key hashed SHA-256 at rest, CORS, rate limit, request-body size limit (RequestBodyLimitMiddleware)
Deny, log with request ID

Protocol payload → API
untrusted → semi
TAP verification middleware (JWKS, 1h TTL, 4h max-stale before reject)
Reject when JWKS unreachable beyond max-stale

API → Orchestrator
semi → semi
require_principal dependency, PaymentOrchestrator.execute() as single chokepoint
No chain_executor bypass — DI lockdown (Phase 2)

Orchestrator → MPC
semi → provider
Turnkey sub-org per tenant, ECDSA-signed API requests
Signature request denied by Turnkey policy

Orchestrator → Compliance
semi → provider
Didit KYC, Elliptic AML
Unreachable provider → deny (fail-closed)

Ledger → Chain
internal → public
SardisLedgerAnchor.sol Merkle root
Batch must settle on-chain before evidence export is authoritative

Threat model

## Who breaks in, and what stops them.

Each row is an actor we actively design against. The "mitigations" column is code that
already exists — not a wishlist.

Actor
Attack
Mitigations
Residual risk

Compromised agent

Prompt injection tricks the agent into calling pay() with a hostile recipient / amount.

Non-custodial (no keys to steal), spending policy caps per-txn and per-window, AGIT fail-closed, anomaly scorer (anomaly_detection.py), human approval for risky actions (approval_service.py).

Agent can drain up to policy cap. Mitigation: tight caps + approval thresholds.

Compromised API key

Key leaks via logs, model output, or client-side reverse-engineer.

Keys hashed SHA-256 at rest, rate limiter fail-closed if Redis unreachable, anomaly detector flags new-geo usage, kill switch freezes wallets in one call.

Small window before anomaly fires. Mitigation: customer rotation workflow + audit alerts.

Compromised MPC provider

Attacker gains Turnkey org-level access.

Sub-org per tenant — blast radius capped at one customer. Turnkey policy engine enforces the same spending rules server-side.

Catastrophic if root org compromised. Mitigation: provider abstraction lets us add Fireblocks/Circle/Lit as a second root.

Malicious merchant

Merchant fakes agent signals to claim funds, or sets up checkout to phish users.

Merchant trust scoring, capability gating, embed-origin CORS restriction (embed_origin column, migration 031), session client_secret + idempotency_key unique constraint, SELECT … FOR UPDATE NOWAIT on settlement.

Policy-level abuse (false advertising etc.). Mitigation: legal review + offboarding pipeline.

Insider (Sardis employee)

Staff modifies a ledger entry to hide a bad decision.

Append-only ledger_entries, policy_decisions writes per request, Merkle-anchored batches via SardisLedgerAnchor.sol, evidence export signed by chain state.

A re-anchor could theoretically rewrite recent history if insider controls the deployer key — but divergence is visible on-chain.

Replay / forgery

Attacker re-submits a valid mandate to execute again.

Postgres + Redis replay cache (MandateArchive + dedup_store.py), nonce enforcement, DB-level idempotency_key unique constraint (migration 031), mandate TTLs.

Low — doubled on two independent stores.

Provider outage

Didit / Elliptic / Turnkey down — tempts "just approve for now."

Every pipeline step defaults to deny on timeout or error. No "allow on failure" path in prod.

Availability hit — accepted trade-off against silent compromise.

Fail-closed

## What each layer does when something goes wrong.

The override flag SARDIS_AGIT_FAIL_OPEN=true exists for local dev only. Default
for agit_fail_open in config.py is False. Production
must deny.

Layer
Control
On normal path
On failure

API gateway
Rate limiter (Redis)
Allow under threshold
Redis unreachable → deny. No in-memory fallback in prod.

API gateway
TAP JWKS fetch
Verify signature
JWKS fetch fails, stale cache > SARDIS_TAP_JWKS_MAX_STALE_SECONDS → reject.

PreExecutionPipeline
Spending policy
Evaluate against natural-language rule
Evaluator raises → deny + ledger entry with reason.

PreExecutionPipeline
AGIT (Agent Identity and Trust)
Chain-of-trust lookup
Unreachable → settings.agit_fail_open=False rejects. control_plane.py line 210.

PreExecutionPipeline
KYA / FIDES
Score agent + counterparty
Provider error → deny.

PreExecutionPipeline
MCC classifier
Map merchant category
Unknown MCC → deny (mcc_service.py line 103).

Compliance
AML / sanctions screening
Green → proceed
No sanctions service → deny deposit (inbound_payment_service.py). Any raised exception → deny.

Orchestrator
ExecutionMode
Honour explicit mode
Viability check fails → FAIL_CLOSED, no silent fallback (execution_mode.py).

State machines
A2A escrow
Only whitelisted transitions
Invalid transition → refuse (a2a_escrow.py).

Secrets + transport

## Secrets management and header posture.

Secrets never in code

All provider credentials live in env vars loaded from Google Secret Manager or Vercel env.
gitleaks runs on every PR with full history (secret-scan.yml,
fetch-depth: 0). A matched leak fails the run — no merge.
API keys are stored in Postgres as SHA-256 hashes, not plaintext.

Middleware stack (sardis-api)

Order from packages/sardis-api/src/sardis_api/main.py: RequestId →
StructuredLogging → SecurityHeaders (CSP/HSTS) → RequestBodyLimit (10MB default, 1MB
webhooks, 512KB checkout, 64KB policies) → RateLimit → TAP → x402 → UsageMetering →
ActivityLogger → CORS (no wildcards — settings.allowed_origins_list) →
routers. Every step is fail-closed.

Webhook signatures

Required in all non-dev environments. HMAC via X-Sardis-Signature and
X-Sardis-Timestamp. Delivery uses the transactional outbox pattern —
webhook_outbox (migration 032) is written in the same transaction as the
business operation and flushed by a background worker. event_id keyed dedup.

Replay + idempotency

Redis + Postgres replay caches for mandates (Phase 3 durable state).
idempotency_key DB-level unique constraint on checkout sessions
(migration 031). SELECT … FOR UPDATE NOWAIT prevents double-pay race at
settlement.

Hardening history

## TDD remediation — three phases, March 2026.

The security posture above is the result of a deliberate three-phase hardening done in
March 2026. Each phase was merged incrementally with tests. Design doc:
docs/plans/2026-03-09-tdd-remediation-90-day-design.md.

Phase
Focus
What shipped

Phase 1

Security + correctness

OAuth CSRF fix, admin fail-closed defaults, webhook signatures required in prod, gitleaks in CI, group-policy TTL + budget decrement, queue interface fix, AGIT default fail-closed.

Phase 2

Single execution path

PreExecutionPipeline built; AGIT/KYA/FIDES hooks; router migrations (wallets, mandates, ap2) onto PaymentOrchestrator; DI lockdown (chain_executor private); unified PaymentResult.

Phase 3

Durable state + moat

Redis dedup store, Postgres reconciliation queue, Redis replay cache, rate limiter enforcement, attestation envelope endpoint, README maturity tiers.

Incident response

## Kill switch.

If we see a confirmed compromise, one privileged call freezes every wallet. The action is
itself audited — see migration 064_emergency_freeze.sql.

What gets frozen

All MPC signing requests denied at the orchestrator boundary, in-flight batches cancelled
before broadcast, background reconciliation suspended. Chain-side funds aren't moved —
they simply stop being signable from Sardis. Non-custodial works in our favour here.

Audit record

emergency_freeze_events: action (freeze_all /
unfreeze_all), triggered_by, wallets_affected,
reason, notes, created_at. Indexed descending on
created_at so the operator dashboard can always show the latest action first.

Audit evidence

## Every decision, exportable.

Layer
Table / file
Evidence value

Every allow/deny
policy_decisions
Reason code + rule snapshot at decision time. Regulators can see why a transaction was allowed, not just that it was.

Every money movement
ledger_entries
Append-only. Every row carries the pre-execution decision ID.

On-chain proof
SardisLedgerAnchor.sol
Merkle-root batches anchored on Base. Once anchored, re-ordering is detectable.

Regulator package
evidence_export router
Produces a signed bundle: policy_decisions + ledger rows + Merkle proof + chain tx hash.

Incident response
emergency_freeze_events (migration 064)
Every freeze/unfreeze with actor and reason.

Webhook delivery
webhook_outbox (migration 032)
Proof of at-least-once delivery with attempts + timestamps.

Go deeper

## Related canvases.

01 · System

Architecture Overview

Where these boundaries sit in the seven-layer stack.

18 · Ops

CI / CD

How CodeQL, gitleaks, Trivy, Bandit, fuzz, and Scorecard enforce this model before merge.

08 · Flows

Policy Engine

How natural-language rules become the deny decisions above.

07 · Flows

Payment Flow

The request's journey through every boundary, end to end.


=====================================
# PROTOCOL OBJECTS
https://canvas.sardis.sh/protocol-objects
=====================================

Canvas 20 · Protocol

## Protocol Objects — the 10 signed artifacts.

Sardis v1.0 defines 10 protocol objects that chain together from delegation through settlement,
dispute resolution, and fulfillment. Every object is cryptographically signed, immutable once issued,
and references its predecessors. Together they form a complete audit chain — no trust in Sardis required.

TL;DR
A reusable funding credential — card, wallet, bank credential, or API key — is the wrong primitive for
the agent economy. The right primitive is the One-Time Payment Object: a signed,
short-lived, context-bound instrument backed by a UTXO-style funding commitment, tied to a verified
agent and principal, bounded by policy, and auditable with a complete cryptographic trail.

Object chain

## From delegation to fulfillment in 10 objects.

## 1 · Agent Certificate
Identity

Identifies the agent and binds it to an issuer and a principal context. Without cryptographic agent identity, you don't have agent payments — you have bot payments. TAP-compatible so agents can prove identity to any Visa-network merchant.

agent_id · issuer_id · principal_id · agent_public_key (Ed25519 or P-256 JWK)
attestation_type (self_signed | issuer_attested | network_verified)
trust_tier (0–3) · capabilities (browse, pay, subscribe, delegate) · expires_at · signature

Trust tiers: 0 = unverified ($10 max tx, email only) · 1 = issuer-attested ($500, KYC-lite) · 2 = network-verified ($10K, full KYC + TAP) · 3 = enterprise-grade (policy-defined, full IAM + continuous monitoring).

## 2 · Spending Mandate
Authorization

The signed statement of allowed authority from the principal — a programmable, cryptographically signed power of attorney for spending. Heart of the system. Expressible as an AP2 Verifiable Digital Credential, extended with agent-native fields (in_flight_limit, fx_policy, UTXO cell strategy).

mandate_id · principal_id · agent_id · merchant_scope · category_scope
max_amount_per_tx · max_amount_per_period · period_type (daily | weekly | monthly)
currency_scope · rail_scope · approval_threshold · retry_limit
in_flight_limit · fx_policy · valid_from · expires_at · policy_hash
revocation_handle · nonce · signature (Ed25519/P-256)

Security — in_flight_limit: prevents the T4 "shadow lock" attack where a malfunctioning agent freezes all funds by requesting objects without presenting them. Agent must settle or cancel before minting new objects beyond the limit.

## 3 · Funding Commitment
Reserve

Reserve-backed container bound to a specific mandate. Guarantees funds are available before any payment object is minted. In v0 this was a monolithic lock against a vault balance — v1 replaces it with a UTXO model to allow concurrent spending without race conditions.

commitment_id · mandate_id
funding_source_type (custodial_fiat | smart_wallet | card | bank | stablecoin | credit_line)
funding_source_ref · total_committed · cell_strategy (pre_split | on_demand | hybrid)
default_cell_denomination · commitment_expiry · settlement_preferences
fallback_options · refund_destination · issuer_signature

Security — refund_destination: must always point to a permanent Principal Master Treasury, never a temp vault or cell. Prevents the T6 "refund-to-closed-vault" failure where disputes resolve months after the original transaction and the funding container is gone.

## 4 · FundingCell (UTXO)
Concurrency

A discrete, atomic unit of spendable value within a Funding Commitment. Inspired by Bitcoin's UTXO model: each cell has exactly one owner at any time; concurrent payments claim different cells; double-spending is structurally impossible without distributed consensus.

cell_id (primary key for locking) · commitment_id · mandate_id
denomination (fixed) · state (AVAILABLE | CLAIMED | SPENT | RETURNED | EXPIRED | SPLIT | MERGED)
claimed_by · claimed_at · payment_object_id · parent_cell_id · vault_ref
created_at · expires_at · nonce · issuer_signature

Why it beats a global counter: no distributed locking. PostgreSQL FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED ensures concurrent transactions never block each other. Cells split on-demand (an agent needing $7 from a $10 cell produces $7 CLAIMED + $3 AVAILABLE), merge in background to prevent fragmentation. Horizontally scalable.

## 5 · One-Time Payment Object
Execution

The single-use executable payment artifact — what the agent presents to the merchant. Derived from the mandate + funding commitment cell(s). Every payment creates a fresh object; no credential is ever reused. This is the core innovation of the protocol.

payment_object_id · mandate_id · commitment_id · cell_ids (UTXO inputs)
merchant_id · session_hash (sha256: merchant_id | cart_id | timestamp)
exact_amount · source_currency · target_currency · nonce · one_time_use (true)
expires_at (minutes–hours) · privacy_mode (transparent | hybrid | full_zk)
zk_proof (if non-transparent) · escrow_required · escrow_profile · dispute_profile
signature_chain [principal_sig, issuer_sig, agent_sig]

Security — session_hash: binds payment to a specific merchant session/cart. Prevents the T3 relay/MITM attack where an intermediary intercepts a valid payment and replays it in a different shopping context.

## 6 · Settlement Receipt
Proof

Canonical proof of what actually happened. Closes the audit chain. v1 adds fulfillment_status to track whether the agent acknowledged receipt of the purchased service/good, plus FX fields for cross-currency transactions.

receipt_id · payment_object_id · settlement_rail · settlement_tx_ref
settled_amount · fees · fx_quote_id · actual_fx_rate · fx_fee
timestamp · status (settled | partial_settled | failed | reversed)
fulfillment_status (pending_ack | acknowledged | dispute_auto_triggered)
merchant_signature · network_signature

fulfillment_status (new in v1): if not acknowledged within escrow timeout, dispute_auto_triggered fires automatically. Closes the T11 "paid but not delivered" gap — settlement succeeds but merchant fails to provide the service.

## 7 · EscrowHold
Trust

For transactions above the escrow threshold, funds land in escrow before the merchant receives them. Merchant sees cryptographic proof of locked funds and delivers the service; agent confirms receipt (or timelock expires); then escrow releases. If dispute: arbitration decides.

escrow_id · payment_object_id · held_amount
held_at (issuer_vault | smart_contract) · contract_address · beneficiary
release_condition (AGENT_CONFIRM | TIMELOCK | ORACLE) · timeout_duration · auto_release_at
state (HELD | CONFIRMING | AUTO_RELEASING | RELEASED | DISPUTING | ARBITRATING | RESOLVED_*)
dispute_window · evidence_deadline · arbitration_endpoint · issuer_signature

Default timeouts by transaction type: API/SaaS = 1h escrow, 24h dispute, 3-day evidence · Software license = 24h / 7d / 5d · Physical goods = 14d / 30d / 7d · B2B services = 30d / 60d / 14d. Timeouts match delivery verification complexity.

## 8 · FXQuote
Cross-currency

Records the cross-currency conversion applied during settlement. Linked from the Settlement Receipt. Time-bounded quote with explicit slippage tracking.

fx_quote_id · source_currency · target_currency
quoted_rate · quoted_at · quote_valid_until (default 30s)
source_amount · target_amount · fx_fee · total_source_debit
slippage_actual_bps · provider · provider_tx_ref

Matched against the mandate's fx_policy at verification time. If slippage_actual_bps exceeds max_slippage_bps or the provider isn't in preferred_fx_providers, the payment fails before settlement.

## 9 · Subscription
Recurring

Recurring charge intents + billing cycles + dunning rules. Not a single mandate but a parent that emits child one-time payment objects on schedule. Covers SaaS subscriptions, usage-based billing, and agent-mediated BNPL (credit-backed mandates).

subscription_id · parent_mandate_id · merchant_id
charge_intent · billing_cycle (ISO 8601 duration) · dunning_policy
amendment_protocol (price_creep_protection)
grace_period · usage_counter · countersignature (for usage-based)

Price-creep protection: if the merchant issues an amendment that exceeds mandate.amendment_tolerance_bps, the subscription pauses pending principal re-signature. Prevents silent subscription inflation that consumer credit cards suffer from.

## 10 · Mandate Tree
Delegation

Hierarchical delegation. CFO creates a master mandate ($500K/quarter); VP Engineering creates a child mandate ($100K/quarter) under it; team lead creates a grandchild ($20K/month). Each level further constrains — never expands — the parent's bounds. Maps to how enterprise budgets actually work.

tree_root_mandate_id · parent_mandate_id · depth · path
inherited_constraints (merchant_scope ∩, category_scope ∩, limits ≤ parent)
delegation_signature (parent signs child) · revocation_cascade

Enforcement rule: child mandate constraints MUST be a strict subset of parent. Issuer rejects creation if any dimension expands. Revocation cascades down the tree — revoking a parent invalidates all descendants.

Chain

## How they reference each other.

Each object is signed and references its predecessors, forming a chain that any party can verify
independently. Canvas 21 · 22-State Machine shows how these objects
transition through states over time.

[diagram]

flowchart LR
AC[Agent Certificate] --> SM[Spending Mandate]
SM --> FC[Funding Commitment]
FC --> FCell[FundingCell UTXO]
SM --> MT[Mandate Tree children]
SM -. recurring .-> SUB[Subscription]
FCell --> POO[One-Time Payment Object]
SM --> POO
POO --> EH[EscrowHold optional]
POO --> SR[Settlement Receipt]
SR --> FXQ[FXQuote if cross-currency]
EH --> SR

[/diagram]

Cross-refs

## Related canvases.

21 · Protocol

22-State Machine

The lifecycle every Payment Object follows — issue through settle, escrow, dispute.

08 · Flows

Policy Engine

The 12-check enforcement pipeline that evaluates every Spending Mandate.

22 · Protocol

Dual-Rail Architecture

Where these objects actually settle — internal ledger vs external rails.

23 · Protocol

Ecosystem Gap Map

Why AP2/TAP/x402 need these objects — no existing standard covers the lifecycle.


=====================================
# STATE MACHINE
https://canvas.sardis.sh/state-machine
=====================================

Canvas 21 · Protocol

## 22-State Machine — one Payment Object's full life.

Every Payment Object passes through a deterministic state machine covering the happy path, escrow hold,
partial settlement, dispute arbitration, FX routing, and fulfillment acknowledgment. 22 states, each with
strict allowed transitions and terminal conditions. This is the most comprehensive payment lifecycle
spec designed for agent commerce.

TL;DR
Card networks have one happy path and a dispute process bolted on. Sardis treats every possible
outcome — direct settle, escrow hold, timelock release, dispute file, arbitration decision, partial
settlement, refund, cancel, failure — as a first-class state with explicit transitions. An agent
payment never ends up in an undefined condition.

Happy path · 7 states

## Direct settlement — no escrow.

ISSUED
→
PRESENTED
→
VERIFIED
→
LOCKED
→
SETTLING
→
SETTLED
→
FULFILLED

State
Meaning
Allowed next

ISSUED
Payment Object minted by Issuer; cell(s) CLAIMED
PRESENTED, REVOKED, EXPIRED

PRESENTED
Agent presented object to merchant
VERIFIED, FAILED, EXPIRED

VERIFIED
Merchant verified claims (cleartext / hybrid / ZKP)
LOCKED, ESCROWED, FAILED

LOCKED
Funds locked — direct settlement (below escrow threshold)
SETTLING, CANCELLED

SETTLING
Settlement executing on chosen rail (may include FX)
SETTLED, PARTIAL_SETTLED, FAILED

SETTLED
Full settlement complete
FULFILLED, DISPUTED, REFUNDED

FULFILLED
Agent acknowledged receipt of service/good (terminal)
— terminal —

Escrow path · +5 states

## When delivery verification matters.

Transactions above the escrow threshold route through an explicit hold. Merchant sees cryptographic
proof of locked funds; agent confirms delivery (or timelock auto-releases); dispute routes to
arbitration with one of three outcomes.

VERIFIED
→
ESCROWED
→
CONFIRMING
│ or
AUTO_RELEASING
→
RELEASED
→ SETTLING → SETTLED → FULFILLED

State
Meaning
Allowed next

ESCROWED
Funds in escrow hold (above threshold)
CONFIRMING, AUTO_RELEASING, DISPUTING

CONFIRMING
Agent reviewing delivery from merchant
RELEASED, DISPUTING

AUTO_RELEASING
Timelock expired without dispute; auto-releasing to merchant
RELEASED

RELEASED
Escrow released to merchant
SETTLING, DISPUTING (within dispute_window)

PARTIAL_SETTLED
Partial amount settled (less than locked)
UNLOCKING, DISPUTED

Default escrow timeouts
API/SaaS digital delivery = 1h escrow / 24h dispute / 3-day evidence · Software license = 24h / 7d / 5d
· Physical goods = 14d / 30d / 7d · B2B services = 30d / 60d / 14d.

Dispute path · 5 states

## Three deterministic outcomes.

DISPUTING
→
ARBITRATING
→
RESOLVED_REFUND
│
RESOLVED_RELEASE
│
RESOLVED_SPLIT

State
Meaning
Allowed next

DISPUTING
Dispute filed, evidence collection phase
ARBITRATING

ARBITRATING
Arbitration node reviewing submitted evidence
RESOLVED_REFUND, RESOLVED_RELEASE, RESOLVED_SPLIT

RESOLVED_REFUND
Full refund to principal (dispute upheld)
— terminal —

RESOLVED_RELEASE
Full release to merchant (dispute rejected)
— terminal —

RESOLVED_SPLIT
Partial refund + partial release (compromise)
— terminal —

Terminal states · 10

## Every object lands somewhere explicit.

Success outcomes

FULFILLED — agent acknowledged service delivery.

RESOLVED_RELEASE — arbitration ruled for merchant.

UNLOCKED — residual FundingCells returned to AVAILABLE.

Failure outcomes

REVOKED — pre-use revocation by principal or issuer.

EXPIRED — expiry time reached before use.

CANCELLED — pre-settlement cancellation → UNLOCKING.

FAILED — processing failure (may retry within mandate.retry_limit).

REFUNDED — post-settlement refund issued.

RESOLVED_REFUND — arbitration ruled for principal.

RESOLVED_SPLIT — arbitration ruled partial.

All transitions

## The full graph.

[diagram]

stateDiagram-v2
[*] --> ISSUED
ISSUED --> PRESENTED
ISSUED --> REVOKED
ISSUED --> EXPIRED
PRESENTED --> VERIFIED
PRESENTED --> FAILED
PRESENTED --> EXPIRED
VERIFIED --> LOCKED: below threshold
VERIFIED --> ESCROWED: above threshold
VERIFIED --> FAILED
LOCKED --> SETTLING
LOCKED --> CANCELLED
ESCROWED --> CONFIRMING
ESCROWED --> AUTO_RELEASING
ESCROWED --> DISPUTING
CONFIRMING --> RELEASED
CONFIRMING --> DISPUTING
AUTO_RELEASING --> RELEASED
RELEASED --> SETTLING
RELEASED --> DISPUTING
SETTLING --> SETTLED
SETTLING --> PARTIAL_SETTLED
SETTLING --> FAILED
SETTLED --> FULFILLED
SETTLED --> DISPUTED: disputed
SETTLED --> REFUNDED
PARTIAL_SETTLED --> UNLOCKING
PARTIAL_SETTLED --> DISPUTING
DISPUTING --> ARBITRATING
ARBITRATING --> RESOLVED_REFUND
ARBITRATING --> RESOLVED_RELEASE
ARBITRATING --> RESOLVED_SPLIT
CANCELLED --> UNLOCKING
UNLOCKING --> UNLOCKED
FULFILLED --> [*]
RESOLVED_REFUND --> [*]
RESOLVED_RELEASE --> [*]
RESOLVED_SPLIT --> [*]
REVOKED --> [*]
EXPIRED --> [*]
UNLOCKED --> [*]
FAILED --> [*]
REFUNDED --> [*]

[/diagram]

Why this matters

## The "undefined ending" problem.

Card networks

Happy path + dispute chargeback, bolted on. No state for "settled but not delivered." No state
for "partial settlement." Merchant and cardholder reconcile via support tickets; evidence is
assembled ad hoc; outcomes depend on issuer discretion.

Sardis

Every possible outcome is an explicit state with cryptographic evidence at each transition.
An agent running thousands of payments per hour needs deterministic end-states to trigger the
right downstream workflow — retry, escalate, refund, reconcile — without human judgment in the loop.

Cross-refs

## Related canvases.

20 · Protocol

Protocol Objects (10)

The objects that travel through these states.

07 · Flows

Payment Flow

Happy-path sequence expanded to real code references.


=====================================
# DUAL RAIL
https://canvas.sardis.sh/dual-rail
=====================================

Canvas 22 · Protocol

## Dual-Rail Architecture — internal ledger first.

Sardis v1.1 inverts the usual fintech stack: the internal ledger is the primary rail,
and external rails (ACH, card, stablecoin, wire, FX bridge) become on-ramp and off-ramp only. When both
parties are on Sardis, a payment completes in ~1ms with $0 fee and zero external API calls. When the
merchant is off-Sardis, we fall back to the cheapest external rail that matches the mandate's
rail_scope. This is the PayPal model applied to agent commerce.

TL;DR
Rails are a settlement surface, not a settlement strategy. Most agent payments in a mature ecosystem
will be Sardis-to-Sardis — the same way most PayPal dollars never leave PayPal. External rails stay
as ramps; internal ledger carries the volume.

The two rails

## Primary vs. fallback.

Primary rail

Sardis internal ledger

An atomic, double-entry ledger inside Sardis. Payer's FundingCells (UTXO) debit in one row;
merchant's Sardis balance credits in another. Both writes happen in a single PostgreSQL
transaction with FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED on the cell row.

- ~1ms end-to-end (database commit latency)

- $0 marginal fee (no external API calls)

- Full audit — every ledger row hash-chained, Merkle-batched, on-chain anchored

- No chain reorg risk — database is final

- Reversible via arbitration up to dispute_window expiry

Fallback rails

External settlement (when merchant is off-Sardis)

The liquidity router selects the best rail based on: merchant's preferred settlement currency,
mandate's rail_scope, FX slippage budget, real-time rail health.

- x402 — stablecoin, HTTP-native

- Card (Stripe Connect, Adyen) — existing merchant account

- Bank (ACH / RTP / SEPA / PIX / Wire) — direct deposit

- FX bridge (Bridge.xyz, Wise) — cross-currency

- MPP (Stripe Merchant Payment Protocol) — Tempo settlement

On-ramp

## How money enters Sardis.

Principals fund their vaults via any rail that makes sense for their treasury. Once on Sardis,
those funds become FundingCells and circulate between agents and merchants entirely inside the ledger.

Bank transfer
ACH pull, wire, SEPA, Faster Payments. Settles to principal's custodial vault in 1–3 business days (varies by rail).

Card funding
Debit card load for quick top-up. Standard card processing fees apply. Covers agents that need liquidity inside minutes.

USDC / stablecoin
On-chain deposit to a Sardis-controlled smart wallet. Near-instant, low fee. Most common for crypto-native principals.

Credit line
A credit-backed mandate draws against a pre-approved revolving line; repayment schedules as a subscription mandate back to the principal's treasury.

Stablecoin bridges
USDC.e and other variants bridge in via Circle CCTP or LayerZero; normalized to canonical USDC inside Sardis.

Smart wallet
An ERC-4337 smart account pre-authorizes Sardis as a spender; agent payments draw down from there without per-tx signatures.

Off-ramp

## How money leaves Sardis.

Only when the merchant is off-Sardis. The liquidity router picks the cheapest rail that matches the
merchant's preferred payout currency and the mandate's rail_scope.

Rail
Typical use
Latency
Fee pattern

x402 (stablecoin)
API monetization, agent-to-agent micro-pay
seconds
~$0.005 per call

Stripe Connect (card)
Pay with Sardis merchants, legacy e-commerce
~3 seconds auth, T+2 payout
2.9% + $0.30

ACH (US)
Large US merchant payouts, vendor settlement
1–3 business days (Same-day ACH 4 hrs)
$0.25–$1

SEPA (EU) / Faster Payments (UK)
EU/UK merchant payouts
seconds–hours
€0.10–€0.50

RTP (US real-time)
Critical payouts, gig economy settlement
seconds
~$0.50

PIX (Brazil), UPI (India)
LATAM / APAC merchant reach
seconds
free–$0.10

Wire (domestic + SWIFT)
Large B2B, enterprise reconciliation
hours–1 day
$15–$40

FX bridge (Bridge.xyz)
Cross-currency settlement
seconds
~25 bps

Why this architecture

## The PayPal parallel.

PayPal processes roughly 95% of its dollar-flow inside its own ledger. Users top up
once, receive payments from other PayPal users, then off-ramp occasionally. The external rails (bank,
card) are funnels, not settlement. PayPal's moat isn't the rails — it's the closed loop.

Why it matters for agent commerce

Agents transact at sub-cent granularity. Running every micro-payment through card rails (2.9%+$0.30)
or ACH (min $0.25) is economically dead. The only way agent commerce scales is if ~90% of volume
stays on an internal ledger where marginal cost is a database write. Sardis is built around that
reality from day one.

Why external rails still matter

Merchants have suppliers, payroll, landlords — obligations that live in fiat. The off-ramp has to
work every day on time. Sardis doesn't pretend the dollar doesn't exist; it just doesn't route
agent-to-agent dollars through the ACH network.

The loop

## Full diagram.

[diagram]

flowchart TB
subgraph Onramp["On-ramp · external → Sardis"]
BT[Bank transfer]
CD[Card]
UC[USDC]
SC[Stablecoin]
CR[Credit line]
end
subgraph Internal["Sardis internal ledger · primary rail"]
PV["Payer vault
FundingCells (UTXO)"]
MB["Merchant account
Sardis balance"]
PC["Protocol core
+ escrow"]
ZK["ZKP +
recurring"]
ID["Identity +
trust"]
PV -->|"atomic ledger transfer
~1ms, $0 fee"| MB
end
subgraph Decision
D{Merchant
off-Sardis?}
end
subgraph Offramp["Off-ramp · Sardis → external (fallback only)"]
X402[x402]
CS[Card · Stripe]
BA["Bank · ACH/RTP"]
FX[FX bridge]
WR[Wire]
end

BT --> PV
CD --> PV
UC --> PV
SC --> PV
CR --> PV
MB --> D
D -->|No| MB
D -->|Yes fallback| X402
D -->|Yes fallback| CS
D -->|Yes fallback| BA
D -->|Yes fallback| FX
D -->|Yes fallback| WR

[/diagram]

Cross-refs

## Related canvases.

10 · Flows

Settlement & Chains

Multi-chain executor, CCTP, paymaster, fiat rails — the external side.

20 · Protocol

Protocol Objects

FundingCell, Settlement Receipt, FXQuote — what flows through these rails.


=====================================
# CREDIT
https://canvas.sardis.sh/credit
=====================================

Canvas 23 · Product

## Agent Credit — facility access without Sardis becoming the lender.

Facility Gate is Sardis's credit control plane for agents. It lets an agent request temporary
purchase authority against a partner-backed facility, but keeps Sardis out of custody, issuing,
balance-sheet lending, and collections. Sardis decides whether authority exists; the sponsor or
partner remains the economic actor.

New
Facility Gate
Non-custodial control plane
Simulator-first
No live card issuance by default

TL;DR
Sardis does not hand an agent a general-purpose credit card. The agent submits a facility request;
Sardis loads persisted mandate, facility, policy, risk, evidence, revocation, and liability state;
then appends an immutable decision packet. Execution is gated through a simulator today and a
provider adapter later. Authorization and money movement stay separated.

Boundary

## The product is a credit authority firewall.

The architecture deliberately avoids the tempting but dangerous framing: "credit card for agents."
A card is only one possible execution rail. The durable primitive is delegated facility authority.

What Sardis controls

Identity binding, mandate scope, policy evaluation, risk scoring, evidence capture, human step-up,
authorization, revocation, exception handling, and audit reconstruction. These are control-plane
functions, not custody functions.

What Sardis does not become

Sardis is not the lender, issuer of record, stored-value holder, collections system, or repayment
obligor. Liquidity and settlement belong to the sponsor, customer-controlled account, or partner
facility provider.

Control plane

## How a facility request actually moves.

[diagram]

flowchart TB
A["Agent
request"] --> FG["Sardis Facility Gate"]
FG --> M["Persisted mandate
+ facility versions"]
FG --> P["Policy, risk,
evidence, revocation"]
P --> D{"Decision"}

D -->|"approved"| L1["Append approved
decision packet"]
L1 --> X["Execution adapter"]
X --> S["Simulator now"]
X --> R["Partner rail later"]

D -->|"step-up"| L2["Append step-up
decision"]
L2 --> O["Approval queue"]
O --> P2["Recheck revocation
+ facility state"]
P2 --> L3["Append final
decision"]

D -->|"denied"| L4["Append denial
with reason codes"]
L1 --> AU["Audit link"]
L3 --> AU
L4 --> AU
AU --> A

[/diagram]

Non-custodial line

## Where custody stays out of Sardis.

Agent

Delegated actor

Can request authority and attach evidence. Cannot create a facility, expand a limit, or become the repayment obligor.

Sponsor

Economic principal

Owns the mandate and liability. The audit packet names the repayment obligor and loss owner explicitly.

Sardis

Authority control plane

Evaluates, gates, records, revokes, and reconstructs decisions. It does not hold funds or issue credit.

Facility

Partner-backed liquidity

Represents an external or sponsor-backed line. Sardis stores state and version refs, not the balance-sheet obligation.

Adapter

Execution gate

Simulator today; real provider later. No credential is created unless an authorization event already exists.

Partner

Rail / issuing / settlement

Moves money or provides credit under its own contract. Webhooks mirror external state back into append-only events.

Decision packet

## Every approval is reconstructable.

The audit surface is not a screenshot of current state. It is a versioned packet that says exactly
what Sardis knew and which authority it relied on when the decision happened.

Mandate version

Explicit facility draw authority, agent binding, merchant/category scope, draw caps, approval threshold.

Facility version

Provider, sponsor, status, limit payload, allowed categories, blocked merchants, repayment mirror if present.

Policy and risk versions

Deterministic policy snapshot, risk model version, feature snapshot, reason codes. No hidden model magic.

Evidence considered

Only evidence attached before decision time is considered. Later evidence requires a new decision path.

Liability assignment

Repayment obligor, sponsor, loss owner, and facility provider are first-class fields, not buried in metadata.

Event hash chain

Requests, approvals, executions, exceptions, revocations, webhooks, and exports sit on append-only events.

Pilot shape

## What is live in the repo now.

Surface
Status
Why it matters

Feature gate
pilot-ready
Global enablement plus SARDIS_FACILITY_GATE_ORG_ALLOWLIST prevents broad accidental exposure.

Strict authority
pilot-ready
Authorization loads persisted mandate, facility, and policy records instead of trusting request snapshots.

Simulator adapter
default
Exercises execution semantics without live provider money movement.

Real provider
disabled
Provider adapter work stays behind contract tests, sandbox credentials, and capability acceptance.

Pilot readiness
scripted
facility_gate_pilot_readiness.py checks flags, allowlist, alert artifacts, and tabletop evidence.

Live card issuance
not yet
Intentionally out of scope until revocation, audit, adapter, webhook, and partner semantics are accepted.

Related canvases

## Read next.

08 · Flows

Policy Engine

The deterministic enforcement layer Facility Gate composes with.

22 · Protocol

Dual-Rail Architecture

Where facility-backed authority fits into the broader rail strategy.


=====================================
# SARDIS CONNECT
https://canvas.sardis.sh/sardis-connect
=====================================

Canvas 20 · Extended

## Sardis Connect

Make any API agent-ready in three lines of code. The merchant ships USD,
the agent pays in stablecoins, Sardis handles every crypto primitive in between.
This canvas explains what Connect is, how it differs from Pay with Sardis, and how
the full onboarding flow works end-to-end.

TL;DR
Pay with Sardis is a customer-facing checkout (buyer clicks "Pay with Sardis" on a merchant site).
Sardis Connect is the merchant-facing inverse — a drop-in SDK that turns any HTTP API into an
agent-payable service: discovery, payment, verification, and USD settlement. Merchant integrates in
5 minutes, never touches a wallet, receives USD in their connected bank account.

Audience

## Who actually ships Sardis Connect.

Connect is a B2B2Agent wedge. The merchant is any company that already runs an HTTP API and wants
AI agents as a new revenue channel — without learning what a gas fee is.

AI API providers

Text-generation, embeddings, analysis, transcription, image, speech APIs. Today they gatekeep with
API keys + human-signed Stripe subscriptions. With Connect, every endpoint gets a price and an agent
can pay per call — no signup, no CAPTCHA, no credit card form.

Data and scraping APIs

The long tail of priced HTTP APIs (proxies, weather, FX, company data). The per-request monetization
loop is the native Connect shape: agent discovers via /.well-known/sardis.json, pays
$0.001 per call, uses it.

SaaS platforms exposing agent features

A traditional SaaS (CRM, analytics, ticketing) adds Connect to bill agents for programmatic access.
Human users stay on the existing subscription plan; agents get a per-call plan without any pricing-page
gymnastics.

Services in the OSP ecosystem

A provider implementing Open Service Protocol (OSP) can declare sardis_wallet in
accepted_payment_methods. Connect is the drop-in library that makes that declaration
real — payment, verification, settlement. See Canvas 21 · OSP Integration.

The Flow

## Merchant onboarding, end to end.

Four moving parts: the merchant app, the Connect SDK, the Sardis API (merchant checkout),
and the merchant's connected bank rail. The merchant touches three of the four; Sardis orchestrates the fourth.

[diagram]

sequenceDiagram
participant M as Merchant app (FastAPI)
participant SC as sardis-connect SDK
participant AG as AI Agent
participant SA as Sardis API
participant ST as Bank rail

Note over M,SC: 1. One-time setup
M->>SA: POST /merchants/register
SA-->>M: merchant_id + mch_live_xxx key
M->>ST: Express onboarding (hosted)
ST-->>M: acct_xxx, charges_enabled=true
M->>SC: SardisConnect(api_key, merchant_id)
M->>SC: sardis.price("/api/generate", "0.05")
M->>M: app.include_router(sardis.router)

Note over AG,M: 2. Agent runtime
AG->>M: GET /.well-known/sardis.json
M-->>AG: manifest {endpoints, prices, accepts}
AG->>M: POST /sardis/pay {endpoint, mandate_id}
M->>SA: POST /merchant-checkout/sessions
SA-->>M: session_id + client_secret
M-->>AG: PaySession
AG->>SA: pay(session_id) via SDK + mandate
SA->>SA: policy + KYC + mandate checks
SA->>SA: USDC settlement on Base/Tempo

Note over SA,ST: 3. Settlement
SA->>ST: Connect Transfer (USD)
ST-->>M: auto-payout to bank
AG->>M: POST /api/generate (X-Sardis-Session)
M->>SC: paywall verifies session
SC->>SA: GET /sessions/{id}
SA-->>SC: status=paid
M-->>AG: 200 OK (API response)

[/diagram]

Python SDK

## Three lines in FastAPI.

Straight from packages/sardis-connect/README.md. The router exposes three endpoints:
agent discovery, payment session creation, and payment verification.

from fastapi import FastAPI
from sardis_connect import SardisConnect

app = FastAPI()

# 1. Initialize with your merchant credentials
sardis = SardisConnect(
api_key="mch_live_xxx",
merchant_id="merch_xxx",
service_name="My AI API",
)

# 2. Define priced endpoints (or sardis.meter() for per-unit)
sardis.price("/api/generate", amount="0.05", description="Generate text")
sardis.price("/api/analyze", amount="0.10", description="Analyze data")

# 3. Mount the router
app.include_router(sardis.router)

Endpoint
Method
Purpose

/.well-known/sardis.json
GET
Agent discovery manifest — name, accepted rails, endpoints, prices.

/sardis/pay
POST
Create a merchant-checkout session; returns session_id + client_secret.

/sardis/verify
POST
Look up session status; returns { verified, amount, payer_id }.

/sardis/webhooks
POST
Signed (HMAC-SHA256) webhook receiver for payment events.

/sardis/usage
POST
Report metered usage (tokens/units) for per-unit pricing — see SardisConnect.meter().

Protect any endpoint with the SardisPaywall FastAPI dependency — the client passes
X-Sardis-Session: mcs_… and the dependency verifies status against the Sardis API before the
handler runs. Source: packages/sardis-connect/src/sardis_connect/middleware.py.

Node SDK

## Express / Node.js, same three lines.

Published as @sardis/connect. Source in
packages/sardis-connect-js/src/middleware.ts.

import express from "express";
import { sardisConnect } from "@sardis/connect";

const app = express();

const sardis = sardisConnect({
apiKey: "mch_live_xxx",
merchantId: "merch_xxx",
serviceName: "My AI API",
baseUrl: "https://api.example.com",
});

sardis.price("/api/generate", "0.05", "Generate text");
sardis.price("/api/analyze", "0.10", "Analyze data");

app.use(sardis.middleware());

The middleware hands off to next() for anything that isn't a Sardis route, so it composes
cleanly with existing Express apps. The manifest declares accepts: ["sardis", "x402", "mpp"]
— agents can choose whichever rail they prefer; the server-side flow is identical.

Reference Demo

## The E2E script, step by step.

demos/sardis_connect_e2e_demo.py walks the full lifecycle in ~320 lines.
Each step maps to one subsystem.

1

Merchant registration

Merchant(name, settlement_preference="stripe_connect", mcc_code="5734"). Produces a
merchant_id and an API key in the mch_live_… namespace. Backed by
sardis-core/merchant.py and the merchants router.

2

Merchant onboarding

Hosted Stripe Express flow (KYB + bank details). On return: charges_enabled,
payouts_enabled, onboarding_state=complete. The merchant record is
stamped with stripe_account_id; this is where USD will land.

3

Agent discovery

Agent does GET /.well-known/sardis.json. The manifest is built by
SardisConnect._get_manifest() from the in-process endpoint registry plus
accepts: ["sardis", "x402", "mpp"].

4

Spending mandate check

Before calling /sardis/pay, the agent's SpendingMandate is evaluated:
per-tx, daily, monthly caps, merchant_scope allowlist, allowed_rails.
Denials include a machine-readable reason code. See Canvas 08 · Policy Engine.

5

Natural-language policy

The demo parses phrases like "Maximum $50 per day on AI API calls" via
RegexPolicyParser in sardis-core/nl_policy_parser.py. The parsed
structure becomes a signed mandate.

6

Multi-protocol execution

Same agent, same mandate, three rails: direct USDC (Base/Tempo), x402
(HTTP 402 on micropayments), and MPP (Stripe session). Connect is rail-agnostic because the
manifest advertises all three and the Sardis orchestrator selects the best fit.

7

Settlement to USD

Sardis settlement engine fires a transfer to acct_…; Stripe's
auto-payout pushes USD to the merchant's bank. Reconciliation enqueued in the Postgres
reconciliation queue (see Canvas 10 · Settlement & Chains).

Settlement Provider

## Why the merchant never touches crypto.

Why a bank-connected merchant account

The merchant already trusts Stripe for card payments. Connect's Express account model
amortizes KYB/KYC across their existing compliance stack, so Sardis does not rebuild it.
The tradeoff is Stripe's fee on the USD leg — Sardis eats none of that margin on the crypto
leg (gasless via Circle Paymaster, USDC native).

Alternative rails

EU merchants can opt into Striga (SEPA + vIBANs). Multi-rail merchants can use Bridge
(ACH/SEPA/PIX). Lightspark is wired for Lightning. The settlement_preference field on
the Merchant record is the toggle — abstracted behind
sardis-core/settlement.

Economics

## Pricing + settlement model.

Mechanism
How it works
When to use

Fixed price
sardis.price(path, amount) — agent pays the posted price per call.
Predictable endpoints: generation, embeddings, search.

Metered (per-unit)
sardis.meter(path, per_unit, unit) — agent calls, merchant reports units via /sardis/usage, Sardis computes charge.
LLM completions (tokens), vision (pixels), data egress (bytes).

Platform fee
Sardis takes a flat percentage on the USDC leg. Stored as platform_fee_amount on each session. Breakdown exposed to both sides.
How Sardis monetizes. Transparent on every receipt.

Merchant payout
Transfer to merchant bank → auto-payout; configurable schedule (daily/weekly).
US default. Swap to Striga/Bridge for EU/multi-rail.

Two Products, One Rail

## Sardis Connect vs. Pay with Sardis.

Both sit on the same merchant-checkout session primitive, but the audience and ergonomics
diverge. Don't conflate them in sales conversations.

Sardis Connect
Pay with Sardis (Checkout)

Who calls it
An AI agent, programmatically
A human buyer, in a browser

UI
None — JSON over HTTP
Hosted React checkout at checkout.sardis.sh

Discovery
/.well-known/sardis.json on the merchant domain
Merchant-issued checkout link / embedded button

Payer source
Agent's Sardis wallet + signed mandate
External wallet (WalletConnect, Coinbase, MetaMask) or Sardis wal_…

Auth
Merchant mch_live_… key + session client_secret
Session client_secret; merchant endpoints use require_principal

Settlement
Bank rail → USD
Same settlement engine; Stripe/Striga/Bridge

Typical ticket
$0.001 – $10 per call
$5 – $500 per order

SDK
sardis-connect (py) + @sardis/connect (js)
sardis-checkout-ui + embed SDK

Canvas
This page
Canvas 11 · Checkout & Merchants

Source of Truth

## Key files.

File
Role

packages/sardis-connect/src/sardis_connect/middleware.py
The FastAPI router — manifest, pay, verify, webhook, usage, paywall dependency.

packages/sardis-connect/src/sardis_connect/models.py
PricedEndpoint, PricingModel, ServiceManifest, UsageRecord.

packages/sardis-connect/README.md
The quickstart; source of the Python example above.

packages/sardis-connect-js/src/middleware.ts
Node/Express middleware; same three routes, hand-rolled with fetch to avoid SDK bloat.

packages/sardis-connect-js/src/types.ts
TS types shared with the Python models by contract.

packages/sardis-api/src/sardis_api/routers/merchants.py
Merchant registration + key issuance. Upstream of every Connect flow.

packages/sardis-api/src/sardis_api/routers/service_directory.py
Registry side: where discovered services can be listed globally.

demos/sardis_connect_e2e_demo.py
The seven-step reference walkthrough.
